title: On the complexity of covering points by disjoint segments and by guillotine cuts
summary: We show that two geometric cover problems in the plane, related to covering points with disjoint line segments, are NP-complete. Given $n$ points in the plane and a value $k$, the first problem asks if all points can be covered by $k$ disjoint line segments; the second problem treats the analogous question for $k$ guillotine cuts.

AI.tldr: Two plane geometric cover problems are NP-complete: covering n points with at most k disjoint line segments; and covering with at most k guillotine cuts.
AI.motivation: Investigate the computational complexity of intuitive geometric covering tasks and identify the boundary between tractable and intractable cases; relevance to applications in VLSI design, sensor networks, and geometric partitioning.
AI.method: Provide NP-membership via polynomial verification of a given set of k segments/cuts; establish NP-hardness through polynomial-time reductions from a known NP-complete problem, using gadget constructions that encode problem constraints; adapt gadgets to enforce disjointness in the line-segment version and guillotine constraints in the cut version.
AI.result: Both decision problems are NP-complete: deciding whether all points can be covered by at most k disjoint line segments, and by at most k guillotine cuts.
AI.conclusion: The result demonstrates that even seemingly simple geometric covering problems in the plane are computationally intractable; motivates further study into fixed-parameter tractability, special-case algorithms, and approximation strategies for practical instances.

title: Computational Hardness of Private Coreset
summary:
We study the problem of differentially private (DP) computation of coreset for the $k$-means objective. For a given input set of points, a coreset is another set of points such that the $k$-means objective for any candidate solution is preserved up to a multiplicative $(1 \pm α)$ factor (and some additive factor).
  We prove the first computational lower bounds for this problem. Specifically, assuming the existence of one-way functions, we show that no polynomial-time $(ε, 1/n^{ω(1)})$-DP algorithm can compute a coreset for $k$-means in the $\ell_\infty$-metric for some constant $α> 0$ (and some constant additive factor), even for $k=3$. For $k$-means in the Euclidean metric, we show a similar result but only for $α= Θ\left(1/d^2\right)$, where $d$ is the dimension.

AI.tldr: DP coreset construction for k-means is computationally hard: under standard cryptographic assumptions, no polynomial-time DP algorithm can produce a coreset that preserves the k-means objective in the l∞-metric (even for k=3) with vanishing error, and a similar hardness holds in Euclidean metric with error Θ(1/d²).
AI.motivation: To establish fundamental limits on privately computing data summaries (coresets) for k-means, filling a gap in lower bounds and clarifying the trade-off between privacy and accurate coreset construction.
AI.method: Reductions from one-way functions to show computational hardness of DP coreset construction. The results quantify impossibility for DP algorithms to produce a coreset that approximately preserves the k-means objective within a (1±α) multiplicative factor (plus an additive term) in l∞-metric (and Euclidean metric) for small α, even for small k (k=3) and fixed dimension d, with ε-privacy and 1/n^{ω(1)}-DP.
AI.result: First computational lower bounds for DP coreset construction for k-means. No polynomial-time ε, 1/n^{ω(1)}-DP algorithm can compute a coreset preserving the k-means objective within a constant α>0 in l∞-metric (even for k=3). In Euclidean metric, a similar bound holds but only for α = Θ(1/d²).
AI.conclusion: Under standard cryptographic assumptions (existence of one-way functions), private coreset construction for k-means is computationally intractable for polynomial-time DP algorithms, establishing fundamental limits on DP data summarization for clustering.

title: Three-dimensional Damage Visualization of Civil Structures via Gaussian Splatting-enabled Digital Twins
summary: Recent advancements in civil infrastructure inspections underscore the need for precise three-dimensional (3D) damage visualization on digital twins, transcending traditional 2D image-based damage identifications. Compared to conventional photogrammetric 3D reconstruction techniques, modern approaches such as Neural Radiance Field (NeRF) and Gaussian Splatting (GS) excel in scene representation, rendering quality, and handling featureless regions. Among them, GS stands out for its efficiency, leveraging discrete anisotropic 3D Gaussians to represent radiance fields, unlike NeRF's continuous implicit model. This study introduces a GS-enabled digital twin method tailored for effective 3D damage visualization. The method's key contributions include: 1) utilizing GS-based 3D reconstruction to visualize 2D damage segmentation results while reducing segmentation errors; 2) developing a multi-scale reconstruction strategy to balance efficiency and damage detail; 3) enabling digital twin updates as damage evolves over time. Demonstrated on an open-source synthetic dataset for post-earthquake inspections, the proposed approach offers a promising solution for comprehensive 3D damage visualization in civil infrastructure digital twins.

AI.tldr: Gaussian Splatting (GS)–based digital twins for 3D damage visualization provide efficient, scalable 3D reconstructions that visualize 2D damage segmentation and support time-evolving updates, demonstrated on synthetic post-earthquake data.
AI.motivation: There is a growing need to visualize damage in 3D on digital twins, beyond 2D image-based segmentation, with accurate scene representation, rendering quality, and effective handling of featureless regions.
AI.method: Propose a GS-based 3D reconstruction pipeline that visualizes 2D damage segmentation results, introduces a multi-scale reconstruction strategy to balance efficiency and detail, and supports updates to the digital twin as damage evolves over time.
AI.result: Validated on an open-source synthetic dataset for post-earthquake inspections, showing that the GS-enabled approach can efficiently render damage in 3D and adapt to damage evolution, offering a promising solution for comprehensive 3D visualization in civil infrastructure digital twins.
AI.conclusion: A GS-enabled digital twin framework provides accurate, efficient, and evolvable 3D damage visualization, bridging 2D segmentation outputs and dynamic, time-varying civil infrastructure models.

title: ICP-Based Pallet Tracking for Unloading on Inclined Surfaces by Autonomous Forklifts
comment: Accepted and published in IEEE/SICE SII 2024
summary: This paper proposes a control method for autonomous forklifts to unload pallets on inclined surfaces, enabling the fork to be withdrawn without dragging the pallets. The proposed method applies the Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm to point clouds measured from the upper region of the pallet and thereby tracks the relative position and attitude angle difference between the pallet and the fork during the unloading operation in real-time. According to the tracking result, the fork is aligned parallel to the target surface. After the fork is aligned, it is possible to complete the unloading process by withdrawing the fork along the tilt, preventing any dragging of the pallet. The effectiveness of the proposed method is verified through dynamic simulations and experiments using a real forklift that replicate unloading operations onto the inclined bed of a truck.

AI.tldr: ICP-based pose tracking from upper-pallet point clouds enables real-time alignment of a forklift fork with an inclined surface to unload pallets without dragging.
AI.motivation: Autonomous unloading on inclined surfaces is challenging due to pallet-slack and risk of dragging; precise pose estimation and alignment are needed to avoid contact with the pallet or bed.
AI.method: Use Iterative Closest Point (ICP) on point clouds from the pallet’s upper region to estimate relative pose (position and attitude) between pallet and fork in real time. Drive fork to align parallel to the target surface, then withdraw along the tilt to unload without dragging. Validate via dynamic simulations and experiments with a real forklift on an inclined truck bed.
AI.result: The approach achieves real-time tracking of pallet–fork pose and alignment with the surface, enabling unloading without dragging. Effectiveness is demonstrated through dynamic simulations and real-world forklift experiments on an inclined bed.
AI.conclusion: ICP-based perception and pose alignment can enable autonomous unloading of pallets on inclined surfaces by aligning the fork with the surface before withdrawal, reducing dragging and potential damage.

title: AIdentifyAGE Ontology for Decision Support in Forensic Dental Age Assessment
summary: Age assessment is crucial in forensic and judicial decision-making, particularly in cases involving undocumented individuals and unaccompanied minors, where legal thresholds determine access to protection, healthcare, and judicial procedures. Dental age assessment is widely recognized as one of the most reliable biological approaches for adolescents and young adults, but current practices are challenged by methodological heterogeneity, fragmented data representation, and limited interoperability between clinical, forensic, and legal information systems. These limitations hinder transparency and reproducibility, amplified by the increasing adoption of AI- based methods. The AIdentifyAGE ontology is domain-specific and provides a standardized, semantically coherent framework, encompassing both manual and AI-assisted forensic dental age assessment workflows, and enabling traceable linkage between observations, methods, reference data, and reported outcomes. It models the complete medico-legal workflow, integrating judicial context, individual-level information, forensic examination data, dental developmental assessment methods, radiographic imaging, statistical reference studies, and AI-based estimation methods. It is being developed together with domain experts, and it builds on upper and established biomedical, dental, and machine learning ontologies, ensuring interoperability, extensibility, and compliance with FAIR principles. The AIdentifyAGE ontology is a fundamental step to enhance consistency, transparency, and explainability, establishing a robust foundation for ontology-driven decision support systems in medico-legal and judicial contexts.

AI.tldr: AIdentifyAGE ontology standardizes dental age assessment workflows in medico-legal contexts, enabling traceability, interoperability, and FAIR, supporting AI-assisted methods.
AI.motivation: To address methodological heterogeneity, fragmented data representation, and limited interoperability across clinical, forensic, and legal systems, which impair transparency and reproducibility, especially with increasing use of AI in age assessment for undocumented individuals and unaccompanied minors.
AI.method: Develop a domain-specific ontology that integrates manual and AI-assisted forensic dental age assessment workflows; model the complete medico-legal workflow; link observations, methods, reference data, imaging, statistical reference studies, and AI methods; incorporate judicial context and individual-level data; build on established biomedical, dental, and machine-learning ontologies; ensure interoperability, extensibility, and FAIR compliance; developed in collaboration with domain experts.
AI.result: Ontology is currently being developed as a framework to enable interoperability, explainability, and traceable linkage across observations, methods, data, and outcomes; aims to underpin ontology-driven decision-support systems in medico-legal and judicial contexts.
AI.conclusion: AIdentifyAGE represents a fundamental step toward consistency, transparency, and explainability in medico-legal dental age assessment, providing a robust foundation for ontology-driven decision-support systems.

title: MMCAformer: Macro-Micro Cross-Attention Transformer for Traffic Speed Prediction with Microscopic Connected Vehicle Driving Behavior
summary: Accurate speed prediction is crucial for proactive traffic management to enhance traffic efficiency and safety. Existing studies have primarily relied on aggregated, macroscopic traffic flow data to predict future traffic trends, whereas road traffic dynamics are also influenced by individual, microscopic human driving behaviors. Recent Connected Vehicle (CV) data provide rich driving behavior features, offering new opportunities to incorporate these behavioral insights into speed prediction. To this end, we propose the Macro-Micro Cross-Attention Transformer (MMCAformer) to integrate CV data-based micro driving behavior features with macro traffic features for speed prediction. Specifically, MMCAformer employs self-attention to learn intrinsic dependencies in macro traffic flow and cross-attention to capture spatiotemporal interplays between macro traffic status and micro driving behavior. MMCAformer is optimized with a Student-t negative log-likelihood loss to provide point-wise speed prediction and estimate uncertainty. Experiments on four Florida freeways demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed MMCAformer compared to baselines. Compared with only using macro features, introducing micro driving behavior features not only enhances prediction accuracy (e.g., overall RMSE, MAE, and MAPE reduced by 9.0%, 6.9%, and 10.2%, respectively) but also shrinks model prediction uncertainty (e.g., mean predictive intervals decreased by 10.1-24.0% across the four freeways). Results reveal that hard braking and acceleration frequencies emerge as the most influential features. Such improvements are more pronounced under congested, low-speed traffic conditions.

AI.tldr: A cross-attention Transformer MMCAformer fuses macro traffic features with CV-based micro driving behaviors for speed prediction, enabling uncertainty estimation via a Student-t NLL loss; it outperforms macro-only baselines with notable reductions in RMSE/MAE/MAPE and predictive intervals; hard braking/acceleration are key features, especially in congested conditions.
AI.motivation: Macro models miss fine-grained driver behaviors that influence speed; CV data provide micro-features (e.g., braking/acceleration) that can improve predictions and quantify uncertainty.
AI.method: A Macro-Micro Cross-Attention Transformer (MMCAformer) with self-attention on macro features and cross-attention between macro status and micro driving behavior; trained with Student-t negative log-likelihood to predict speed and its uncertainty.
AI.result: On four Florida freeways, MMCAformer surpasses baselines; accuracy metrics (RMSE, MAE, MAPE) improved by ~9.0%, 6.9%, 10.2%; predictive intervals shrink 10.1-24.0%; hard braking/acceleration features most influential; gains larger under congested, low-speed traffic.
AI.conclusion: Integrating CV-based micro behaviors with macro traffic data via MMCAformer yields better speed prediction and calibrated uncertainty, highlighting the value of micro features in traffic forecasting.

title: Analytic Score Optimization for Multi Dimension Video Quality Assessment
summary: Video Quality Assessment (VQA) is evolving beyond single-number mean opinion score toward richer, multi-faceted evaluations of video content. In this paper, we present a large-scale multi-dimensional VQA dataset UltraVQA that encompasses diverse User-Generated Content~(UGC) annotated across five key quality dimensions: Motion Quality, Motion Amplitude, Aesthetic Quality, Content Quality, and Clarity Quality. Each video in our dataset is scored by over 3 human raters on these dimensions, with fine-grained sub-attribute labels, and accompanied by an explanatory rationale generated by GPT based on the collective human judgments. To better leverage these rich annotations and improve discrete quality score assessment, we introduce Analytic Score Optimization (ASO), a theoretically grounded post-training objective derived for multi-dimensional VQA. By reframing quality assessment as a regularized decision-making process, we obtain a closed-form solution that naturally captures the ordinal nature of human ratings, ensuring alignment with human ranking preferences. In experiments, our method outperforms most baselines including closed-source APIs and open-source models, while also reducing mean absolute error (MAE) in quality prediction. Our work highlights the importance of multi-dimensional, interpretable annotations and reinforcement-based alignment in advancing video quality assessment.

AI.tldr: Proposes UltraVQA, a large-scale multi-dimensional VQA dataset with five quality dimensions and GPT-generated rationales, and Analytic Score Optimization (ASO), a post-training objective that models ordinal, multi-dimensional judgments to improve quality prediction.
AI.motivation: Video quality assessment benefits from richer, multi-dimensional, and interpretable annotations beyond a single mean opinion score, enabling better alignment with human judgments and actionable insights.
AI.method: Construct UltraVQA with >3 human raters per video per dimension, five annotated dimensions with sub-attributes, and GPT-generated rationales. Introduce Analytic Score Optimization (ASO), a post-training objective that reframes quality assessment as regularized decision-making, yielding a closed-form solution that respects ordinal ratings.
AI.result: ASO-based models outperform most baselines (including closed-source APIs and open-source models) and reduce MAE in quality prediction, validating the value of multi-dimensional and interpretable annotations with alignment-driven learning.
AI.conclusion: The work underscores the importance of multi-dimensional, interpretable quality annotations and a reinforcement-alignment approach (ASO) for advancing video quality assessment.

title: Smooth trajectory generation and hybrid B-splines-Quaternions based tool path interpolation for a 3T1R parallel kinematic milling robot
comment: 30 pages, 17 figures, published in Elsevier Precision Engineering (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0141635925001266)
summary: This paper presents a smooth trajectory generation method for a four-degree-of-freedom parallel kinematic milling robot. The proposed approach integrates B-spline and Quaternion interpolation techniques to manage decoupled position and orientation data points. The synchronization of orientation and arc-length-parameterized position data is achieved through the fitting of smooth piece-wise Bezier curves, which describe the non-linear relationship between path length and tool orientation, solved via sequential quadratic programming. By leveraging the convex hull properties of Bezier curves, the method ensures spatial and temporal separation constraints for multi-agent trajectory generation. Unit quaternions are employed for orientation interpolation, providing a robust and efficient representation that avoids gimbal lock and facilitates smooth, continuous rotation. Modifier polynomials are used for position interpolation. Temporal trajectories are optimized using minimum jerk, time-optimal piece-wise Bezier curves in two stages: task space followed by joint space, implemented on a low-cost microcontroller. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method offers enhanced accuracy, reduced velocity fluctuations, and computational efficiency compared to conventional interpolation methods.

AI.tldr: A smooth, multi-stage trajectory generation framework for a 4-DOF parallel milling robot that combines B-spline, quaternions, Bezier-based arc-length synchronization, and two-stage minimum-jerk optimization, achieving accurate, stable, and efficient motion with multi-agent constraints.
AI.motivation: Need for decoupled position/orientation handling, smooth tool orientation, avoidance of gimbal lock, and reliable, real-time trajectories for a compact parallel milling robot; address non-linear path-length-to-orientation mapping and coordination among multiple agents using convex-hull guarantees.
AI.method: Interpolate orientation with unit quaternions; interpolate position with modifier polynomials; synchronize using piecewise Bezier curves by solving a non-linear arc-length/orientation relation via sequential quadratic programming; leverage Bezier convex hull properties to enforce spatial-temporal separation in multi-agent contexts; perform temporal optimization with minimum-jerk and time-optimal Bezier curves in two stages (task space then joint space); implement on a low-cost microcontroller.
AI.result: Experimental results show higher accuracy, reduced velocity fluctuations, and improved computational efficiency relative to conventional interpolation methods.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a robust, efficient trajectory generation framework for 4-DOF parallel milling robots, yielding smooth, synchronized motion with strong guarantees for multi-agent constraints and feasibility on hardware-limited platforms.

title: A Few-Shot LLM Framework for Extreme Day Classification in Electricity Markets
summary: This paper proposes a few-shot classification framework based on Large Language Models (LLMs) to predict whether the next day will have spikes in real-time electricity prices. The approach aggregates system state information, including electricity demand, renewable generation, weather forecasts, and recent electricity prices, into a set of statistical features that are formatted as natural-language prompts and fed to an LLM along with general instructions. The model then determines the likelihood that the next day would be a spike day and reports a confidence score. Using historical data from the Texas electricity market, we demonstrate that this few-shot approach achieves performance comparable to supervised machine learning models, such as Support Vector Machines and XGBoost, and outperforms the latter two when limited historical data are available. These findings highlight the potential of LLMs as a data-efficient tool for classifying electricity price spikes in settings with scarce data.

AI.tldr: A few-shot LLM-based classifier predicts next-day electricity price spikes from aggregated system features, achieving competitive performance with SVM/XGBoost and superior performance when data are scarce.
AI.motivation: Forecasting price spikes in electricity markets is important for risk management, but labeled data are often limited in many markets. A data-efficient, prompt-driven approach using LLMs could provide competitive performance without requiring large labeled datasets.
AI.method: Aggregate system-state features (demand, renewable generation, weather forecasts, recent prices) into statistical features, then formulate them as natural-language prompts with general instructions and feed to an LLM. The model outputs the probability of a spike day and a confidence score. Evaluation uses historical Texas electricity-market data, comparing against supervised models (SVM, XGBoost) in both standard and data-scarce settings.
AI.result: The few-shot LLM approach achieved performance comparable to SVM and XGBoost under typical data availability, and outperformed them when historical data were limited.
AI.conclusion: LLMs in a few-shot prompting setup can be a viable, data-efficient tool for classifying electricity price spikes in data-scarce regimes. This suggests potential for broader application in time-series spike classification, while highlighting considerations for prompt design and calibration.

title: DODO: Discrete OCR Diffusion Models
summary: Optical Character Recognition (OCR) is a fundamental task for digitizing information, serving as a critical bridge between visual data and textual understanding. While modern Vision-Language Models (VLM) have achieved high accuracy in this domain, they predominantly rely on autoregressive decoding, which becomes computationally expensive and slow for long documents as it requires a sequential forward pass for every generated token. We identify a key opportunity to overcome this bottleneck: unlike open-ended generation, OCR is a highly deterministic task where the visual input strictly dictates a unique output sequence, theoretically enabling efficient, parallel decoding via diffusion models. However, we show that existing masked diffusion models fail to harness this potential; those introduce structural instabilities that are benign in flexible tasks, like captioning, but catastrophic for the rigid, exact-match requirements of OCR. To bridge this gap, we introduce DODO, the first VLM to utilize block discrete diffusion and unlock its speedup potential for OCR. By decomposing generation into blocks, DODO mitigates the synchronization errors of global diffusion. Empirically, our method achieves near state-of-the-art accuracy while enabling up to 3x faster inference compared to autoregressive baselines.

AI.tldr: DODO leverages block discrete diffusion for OCR to enable parallel decoding, addressing instability in prior masked diffusion models; achieves near-SOTA accuracy with up to 3x faster inference than autoregressive baselines.
AI.motivation: OCR is highly deterministic and benefits from parallel decoding, but existing diffusion approaches introduce synchronization issues that harm exact-match requirements; autoregressive models are slow on long documents.
AI.method: Introduce block discrete diffusion (DODO) that decomposes generation into blocks to reduce synchronization errors and stabilize diffusion for OCR; first VLM to apply this approach to OCR.
AI.result: Near state-of-the-art accuracy with substantial speedup, achieving up to 3x faster inference compared with autoregressive baselines.
AI.conclusion: Block discrete diffusion enables reliable, fast OCR in VLMs, closing the gap between accuracy and speed for long-document text extraction; suggests potential for further acceleration and broader adoption in rigid text-generation tasks.

title: RRT$^η$: Sampling-based Motion Planning and Control from STL Specifications using Arithmetic-Geometric Mean Robustness
summary:
Sampling-based motion planning has emerged as a powerful approach for robotics, enabling exploration of complex, high-dimensional configuration spaces. When combined with Signal Temporal Logic (STL), a temporal logic widely used for formalizing interpretable robotic tasks, these methods can address complex spatiotemporal constraints. However, traditional approaches rely on min-max robustness measures that focus only on critical time points and subformulae, creating non-smooth optimization landscapes with sharp decision boundaries that hinder efficient tree exploration.
  We propose RRT$^η$, a sampling-based planning framework that integrates the Arithmetic-Geometric Mean (AGM) robustness measure to evaluate satisfaction across all time points and subformulae. Our key contributions include: (1) AGM robustness interval semantics for reasoning about partial trajectories during tree construction, (2) an efficient incremental monitoring algorithm computing these intervals, and (3) enhanced Direction of Increasing Satisfaction vectors leveraging Fulfillment Priority Logic (FPL) for principled objective composition. Our framework synthesizes dynamically feasible control sequences satisfying STL specifications with high robustness while maintaining the probabilistic completeness and asymptotic optimality of RRT$^\ast$. We validate our approach on three robotic systems. A double integrator point robot, a unicycle mobile robot, and a 7-DOF robot arm, demonstrating superior performance over traditional STL robustness-based planners in multi-constraint scenarios with limited guidance signals.

AI.tldr: Introduces RRT^η using AGM robustness for STL in sampling-based motion planning, evaluating satisfaction across all time points and subformulae, with interval monitoring and FPL-guided objective; preserves RRT* guarantees and demonstrates improved performance across three robotic systems.
AI.motivation: Standard min-max STL robustness yields non-smooth optimization landscapes and poor tree exploration; there is a need for a robustness measure that accounts for all time instants and subformulas to improve planning efficiency and robustness.
AI.method: Develop AGM robustness interval semantics for partial trajectories during tree construction; implement an efficient incremental monitoring algorithm to compute these intervals; extend Direction of Increasing Satisfaction vectors with Fulfillment Priority Logic (FPL) for principled objective composition; integrate into RRT^* to retain probabilistic completeness and asymptotic optimality.
AI.result: Validation on three robotic systems (double integrator point robot, unicycle, 7-DOF robot arm) showing superior performance over traditional STL robustness-based planners in multi-constraint scenarios with limited guidance signals.
AI.conclusion: AGM robustness provides smoother, more informative satisfaction measures across time and subformulas, enabling more effective tree exploration while preserving formal guarantees; demonstrates practical effectiveness for complex STL-constrained planning.

title: Contextuality from Single-State Representations: An Information-Theoretic Principle for Adaptive Intelligence
comment: This paper addresses contextuality from a representation-theoretic and information-theoretic perspective in adaptive systems. It is conceptually and technically distinct from the authors' earlier arXiv works (QTOW/QTOW2), which pursue different formulations of contextuality
summary: Adaptive systems often operate across multiple contexts while reusing a fixed internal state space due to constraints on memory, representation, or physical resources. Such single-state reuse is ubiquitous in natural and artificial intelligence, yet its fundamental representational consequences remain poorly understood. We show that contextuality is not a peculiarity of quantum mechanics, but an inevitable consequence of single-state reuse in classical probabilistic representations. Modeling contexts as interventions acting on a shared internal state, we prove that any classical model reproducing contextual outcome statistics must incur an irreducible information-theoretic cost: dependence on context cannot be mediated solely through the internal state. We provide a minimal constructive example that explicitly realizes this cost and clarifies its operational meaning. We further explain how nonclassical probabilistic frameworks avoid this obstruction by relaxing the assumption of a single global joint probability space, without invoking quantum dynamics or Hilbert space structure. Our results identify contextuality as a general representational constraint on adaptive intelligence, independent of physical implementation.

AI.tldr: Contextuality arises from reusing a fixed internal state across multiple contexts; any classical model reproducing contextual statistics must incur a nontrivial cost—context dependence cannot be captured by the internal state alone. Nonclassical probabilistic frameworks avoid this by abandoning a single global joint probability space. Contextuality is thus a general representational constraint on adaptive intelligence, not tied to quantum physics.
AI.motivation: To understand the representational consequences of reusing a fixed internal state across diverse contexts, and to clarify why contextuality emerges as a feature of both natural and artificial adaptive systems, independent of underlying physics.
AI.method: Provide a formal argument: with contexts as interventions on a shared internal state, show that reproducing contextual outcome statistics with a classical model requires irreducible context dependence beyond the internal state; supply a minimal constructive example; discuss how relaxing the single global joint probability space circumvents the obstruction.
AI.result: Proves irreducible information-theoretic cost for classical models under single-state reuse; presents a minimal constructive example; explains how nonclassical frameworks avoid the obstruction by forgoing a single joint probability space.
AI.conclusion: Contextuality is a general representational constraint on adaptive intelligence, applicable beyond quantum mechanics and independent of the physical substrate.

title: Real-time Secondary Crash Likelihood Prediction Excluding Post Primary Crash Features
summary: Secondary crash likelihood prediction is a critical component of an active traffic management system to mitigate congestion and adverse impacts caused by secondary crashes. However, existing approaches mainly rely on post-crash features (e.g., crash type and severity) that are rarely available in real time, limiting their practical applicability. To address this limitation, we propose a hybrid secondary crash likelihood prediction framework that does not depend on post-crash features. A dynamic spatiotemporal window is designed to extract real-time traffic flow and environmental features from primary crash locations and their upstream segments. The framework includes three models: a primary crash model to estimate the likelihood of secondary crash occurrence, and two secondary crash models to evaluate traffic conditions at crash and upstream segments under different comparative scenarios. An ensemble learning strategy integrating six machine learning algorithms is developed to enhance predictive performance, and a voting-based mechanism combines the outputs of the three models. Experiments on Florida freeways demonstrate that the proposed hybrid framework correctly identifies 91% of secondary crashes with a low false alarm rate of 0.20. The Area Under the ROC Curve improves from 0.654, 0.744, and 0.902 for the individual models to 0.952 for the hybrid model, outperforming previous studies.

AI.tldr: Hybrid real-time secondary crash predictor using dynamic spatiotemporal features and ensemble learning; achieves AUC 0.952 and 91% detection with 0.20 false alarms on Florida freeways.
AI.motivation: Post-crash features limit real-time applicability of secondary crash prediction. A real-time, post-crash-independent method is needed to support active traffic management.
AI.method: Introduce a dynamic spatiotemporal window to extract real-time traffic flow and environmental features from primary crash locations and upstream segments. Propose three models (primary crash likelihood; and two secondary crash models for crash and upstream segments) and integrate six ML algorithms via ensemble learning, with a voting mechanism to fuse outputs.
AI.result: Hybrid model achieves AUC 0.952, improving from 0.654/0.744/0.902 (individual models) to the hybrid. Identifies 91% of secondary crashes with a false alarm rate of 0.20 on Florida freeways.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hybrid framework demonstrates strong real-time predictive performance and outperforms prior studies, supporting its potential for proactive traffic management without relying on post-crash features.

title: StereoAdapter-2: Globally Structure-Consistent Underwater Stereo Depth Estimation
summary: Stereo depth estimation is fundamental to underwater robotic perception, yet suffers from severe domain shifts caused by wavelength-dependent light attenuation, scattering, and refraction. Recent approaches leverage monocular foundation models with GRU-based iterative refinement for underwater adaptation; however, the sequential gating and local convolutional kernels in GRUs necessitate multiple iterations for long-range disparity propagation, limiting performance in large-disparity and textureless underwater regions. In this paper, we propose StereoAdapter-2, which replaces the conventional ConvGRU updater with a novel ConvSS2D operator based on selective state space models. The proposed operator employs a four-directional scanning strategy that naturally aligns with epipolar geometry while capturing vertical structural consistency, enabling efficient long-range spatial propagation within a single update step at linear computational complexity. Furthermore, we construct UW-StereoDepth-80K, a large-scale synthetic underwater stereo dataset featuring diverse baselines, attenuation coefficients, and scattering parameters through a two-stage generative pipeline combining semantic-aware style transfer and geometry-consistent novel view synthesis. Combined with dynamic LoRA adaptation inherited from StereoAdapter, our framework achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on underwater benchmarks with 17% improvement on TartanAir-UW and 7.2% improvment on SQUID, with real-world validation on the BlueROV2 platform demonstrates the robustness of our approach. Code: https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/StereoAdapter-2. Website: https://aigeeksgroup.github.io/StereoAdapter-2.

AI.tldr: StereoAdapter-2 introduces a ConvSS2D operator (selective state-space-based) to replace ConvGRU for underwater stereo depth, enabling efficient long-range disparity propagation in a single, linear-cost update. It is paired with UW-StereoDepth-80K, a large synthetic underwater stereo dataset built via semantic-aware style transfer and geometry-consistent novel view synthesis. With dynamic LoRA adaptation, the approach achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot results on underwater benchmarks and real-world validation on BlueROV2.
AI.motivation: Underwater depth estimation suffers from strong domain shifts caused by wavelength-dependent attenuation, scattering, and refraction. GRU-based iterative refinements require multiple updates for long-range disparities and textureless regions, limiting performance.
AI.method: Replace ConvGRU updater with ConvSS2D, a four-directional selective-state-space operator aligned with epipolar geometry to enable long-range propagation in a single update at linear cost. Integrate with monocular foundation-model adaptation via dynamic LoRA. Construct UW-StereoDepth-80K with two-stage generative pipeline: semantic-aware style transfer and geometry-consistent novel view synthesis.
AI.result: Zero-shot gains: ~17% improvement on TartanAir-UW and ~7.2% on SQUID. Real-world validation on BlueROV2 demonstrates robustness. Code and project pages provided.
AI.conclusion: ConvSS2D enables efficient long-range disparity propagation with linear complexity and, coupled with a large synthetic, geometry-consistent underwater dataset and dynamic LoRA adaptation, delivers strong zero-shot and real-world performance in underwater stereo depth estimation.

title: Sound of Touch: Active Acoustic Tactile Sensing via String Vibrations
summary: Distributed tactile sensing remains difficult to scale over large areas: dense sensor arrays increase wiring, cost, and fragility, while many alternatives provide limited coverage or miss fast interaction dynamics. We present Sound of Touch, an active acoustic tactile-sensing methodology that uses vibrating tensioned strings as sensing elements. The string is continuously excited electromagnetically, and a small number of pickups (contact microphones) observe spectral changes induced by contact. From short-duration audio signals, our system estimates contact location and normal force, and detects slip. To guide design and interpret the sensing mechanism, we derive a physics-based string-vibration simulator that predicts how contact position and force shift vibration modes. Experiments demonstrate millimeter-scale localization, reliable force estimation, and real-time slip detection. Our contributions are: (i) a lightweight, scalable string-based tactile sensing hardware concept for instrumenting extended robot surfaces; (ii) a physics-grounded simulation and analysis tool for contact-induced spectral shifts; and (iii) a real-time inference pipeline that maps vibration measurements to contact state.

AI.tldr: Sound of Touch uses vibrating tensioned strings as a scalable tactile sensor. From short audio, it estimates contact location, normal force, and detects slip; backed by a physics-based string-vibration simulator; demonstrates millimeter localization and real-time inference across extended surfaces.
AI.motivation: Scaling tactile sensing over large areas is hard due to wiring, cost, and fragility of dense sensor arrays, and many alternatives sacrifice coverage or dynamics. An active acoustic, string-based approach promises large-area coverage with minimal wiring and fast interaction dynamics.
AI.method: Continuous electromagnetic excitation of tensioned strings. Contact microphones observe spectral changes caused by touch. Short-duration audio is mapped to contact state (location, normal force, slip) via a physics-grounded string-vibration simulator that predicts how contact shifts vibration modes. Real-time inference maps spectral measurements to contact state.
AI.result: Experiments show millimeter-scale localization, reliable force estimation, and real-time slip detection.
AI.conclusion: The work presents (i) a scalable string-based tactile sensing hardware concept for large robot surfaces, (ii) a physics-grounded tool for understanding contact-induced spectral shifts, and (iii) a real-time inference pipeline to infer contact state from vibration measurements.

title: Mobility-Aware Cache Framework for Scalable LLM-Based Human Mobility Simulation
summary: Large-scale human mobility simulation is critical for applications such as urban planning, epidemiology, and transportation analysis. Recent works treat large language models (LLMs) as human agents to simulate realistic mobility behaviors using structured reasoning, but their high computational cost limits scalability. To address this, we design a mobility-aware cache framework named MobCache that leverages reconstructible caches to enable efficient large-scale human mobility simulations. It consists of: (1) a reasoning component that encodes each reasoning step as a latent-space embedding and uses a latent-space evaluator to enable the reuse and recombination of reasoning steps; and (2) a decoding component that employs a lightweight decoder trained with mobility law-constrained distillation to translate latent-space reasoning chains into natural language, thereby improving simulation efficiency while maintaining fidelity. Experiments show that MobCache significantly improves efficiency across multiple dimensions while maintaining performance comparable to state-of-the-art LLM-based methods.

AI.tldr: MobCache proposes a cache-based framework to accelerate large-scale mobility simulations by encoding reasoning steps as latent-space embeddings and using a decoding path trained with mobility law-constrained distillation, achieving large efficiency gains with fidelity to state-of-the-art LLM-based methods.
AI.motivation: LLM-based mobility simulations are accurate but computationally expensive and non-scalable for large populations. A cache-based approach aims to reuse/recombine reasoning steps to reduce cost while preserving fidelity.
AI.method: Two components: (1) reasoning component with latent-space embeddings and a latent-space evaluator enabling reuse/recombination of reasoning steps; (2) decoding component: a lightweight decoder trained via mobility law-constrained distillation to translate latent reasoning chains into natural language. Also reconstruction of caches to support scalable simulation.
AI.result: Experiments show significant efficiency gains across multiple dimensions with performance comparable to state-of-the-art LLM-based methods.
AI.conclusion: MobCache offers a scalable, efficient approach to large-scale mobility simulation by combining latent-space reasoning caches with constrained decoding to preserve fidelity while reducing computation.

title: Quantifying LLM Attention-Head Stability: Implications for Circuit Universality
comment: Main Body: 8 pages, Total length: 33 pages, Code repo: https://github.com/karanbali/attention_head_seed_stability , Weights repo: https://huggingface.co/karanbali/attention_head_seed_stability
summary: In mechanistic interpretability, recent work scrutinizes transformer "circuits" - sparse, mono or multi layer sub computations, that may reflect human understandable functions. Yet, these network circuits are rarely acid-tested for their stability across different instances of the same deep learning architecture. Without this, it remains unclear whether reported circuits emerge universally across labs or turn out to be idiosyncratic to a particular estimation instance, potentially limiting confidence in safety-critical settings. Here, we systematically study stability across-refits in increasingly complex transformer language models of various sizes. We quantify, layer by layer, how similarly attention heads learn representations across independently initialized training runs. Our rigorous experiments show that (1) middle-layer heads are the least stable yet the most representationally distinct; (2) deeper models exhibit stronger mid-depth divergence; (3) unstable heads in deeper layers become more functionally important than their peers from the same layer; (4) applying weight decay optimization substantially improves attention-head stability across random model initializations; and (5) the residual stream is comparatively stable. Our findings establish the cross-instance robustness of circuits as an essential yet underappreciated prerequisite for scalable oversight, drawing contours around possible white-box monitorability of AI systems.

AI.tldr: Systematic study of cross-instance stability of transformer 'circuits' across independently trained language models. Key findings: middle-layer attention heads are the least stable yet representationally distinct; deeper models show stronger mid-depth divergence; unstable deep heads become more functionally important; weight decay improves head stability; residual stream remains comparatively stable. This supports cross-instance robustness as a prerequisite for scalable oversight and monitorability.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether mechanistic interpretability circuits are universal across random initializations or idiosyncratic to a particular training run, addressing reliability for safety-critical AI deployment.
AI.method: Train multiple independently initialized transformers of varying sizes; layer-by-layer quantify similarity of attention-head representations across runs; assess the impact of optimization (weight decay); examine stability of the residual stream; analyze results across increasingly complex models.
AI.result: (1) Middle-layer heads are the least stable yet most representationally distinct; (2) deeper models exhibit stronger mid-depth divergence; (3) unstable heads in deeper layers become more functionally important than peers in the same layer; (4) weight decay substantially improves attention-head stability across random initializations; (5) the residual stream is comparatively stable.
AI.conclusion: Cross-instance robustness of circuits is essential for scalable oversight, outlining feasibility for white-box monitorability of AI systems.

title: SemCovNet: Towards Fair and Semantic Coverage-Aware Learning for Underrepresented Visual Concepts
summary: Modern vision models increasingly rely on rich semantic representations that extend beyond class labels to include descriptive concepts and contextual attributes. However, existing datasets exhibit Semantic Coverage Imbalance (SCI), a previously overlooked bias arising from the long-tailed semantic representations. Unlike class imbalance, SCI occurs at the semantic level, affecting how models learn and reason about rare yet meaningful semantics. To mitigate SCI, we propose Semantic Coverage-Aware Network (SemCovNet), a novel model that explicitly learns to correct semantic coverage disparities. SemCovNet integrates a Semantic Descriptor Map (SDM) for learning semantic representations, a Descriptor Attention Modulation (DAM) module that dynamically weights visual and concept features, and a Descriptor-Visual Alignment (DVA) loss that aligns visual features with descriptor semantics. We quantify semantic fairness using a Coverage Disparity Index (CDI), which measures the alignment between coverage and error. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate that SemCovNet enhances model reliability and substantially reduces CDI, achieving fairer and more equitable performance. This work establishes SCI as a measurable and correctable bias, providing a foundation for advancing semantic fairness and interpretable vision learning.

AI.tldr: Introduces SemCovNet to address Semantic Coverage Imbalance (SCI) by learning semantic descriptor representations and aligning them with visuals, reducing CDI and improving fairness.
AI.motivation: Existing vision datasets exhibit SCI due to long-tailed semantic representations; semantics beyond class labels create bias in learning and reasoning about rare yet meaningful concepts. A measurable, correctable bias is needed to achieve semantic fairness in vision models.
AI.method: Propose Semantic Descriptor Map (SDM) to learn semantic representations; Descriptor Attention Modulation (DAM) to dynamically weight visual and concept features; Descriptor-Visual Alignment (DVA) loss to align visual features with descriptor semantics; Introduces Coverage Disparity Index (CDI) to quantify semantic fairness.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple datasets show SemCovNet reduces CDI and yields more reliable, fairer and more equitable performance.
AI.conclusion: Semantic Coverage Imbalance is a measurable and correctable bias; SemCovNet provides a foundation for semantic fairness and interpretable vision learning.

title: "Hello, I'm Delivering. Let Me Pass By": Navigating Public Pathways with Walk-along with Robots in Crowded City Streets
summary: As the presence of autonomous robots in public spaces increases-whether navigating campus walkways or neighborhood sidewalks-understanding how to carefully study these robots becomes critical. While HRI research has conducted field studies in public spaces, these are often limited to controlled experiments with prototype robots or structured observational methods, such as the Wizard of Oz technique. However, the autonomous mobile robots we encounter today, particularly delivery robots, operate beyond the control of researchers, navigating dynamic routes and unpredictable environments. To address this challenge, a more deliberate approach is required. Drawing inspiration from public realm ethnography in urban studies, geography, and sociology, this paper proposes the Walk-Along with Robots (WawR) methodology. We outline the key features of this method, the steps we applied in our study, the unique insights it offers, and the ways it can be evaluated. We hope this paper stimulates further discussion on research methodologies for studying autonomous robots in public spaces.

AI.tldr: Proposes Walk-Along with Robots (WawR), an ethnography-inspired field method for studying autonomous delivery robots in public spaces, addressing limitations of lab experiments and Wizard-of-Oz studies by observing robots in real-world, dynamic environments.
AI.motivation: Public-space HRI studies often rely on controlled experiments or prototype robots, which fail to capture the complexities of autonomous robots operating in real-world public contexts; there is a need for ecologically valid, in-situ methodologies.
AI.method: Walk-Along with Robots (WawR) is an ethnographic-inspired field method. It outlines key features, the steps used in the study, and how the approach yields unique insights. The method draws on public realm ethnography from urban studies, geography, and sociology and emphasizes real-time co-navigation, situational context, and reflexive observation.
AI.result: WawR offers rich qualitative insights into human–robot interactions in public spaces, highlighting how people perceive autonomy, navigate shared spaces, and adapt to robot behaviors; the paper also discusses evaluation criteria for the method (validity, reliability, ethical considerations, and transferability).
AI.conclusion: The paper positions WawR as a valuable addition to HRI methodology for studying autonomous robots in public spaces and calls for further discussion and refinement of field-based approaches.

title: When AI Benchmarks Plateau: A Systematic Study of Benchmark Saturation
summary: Artificial Intelligence (AI) benchmarks play a central role in measuring progress in model development and guiding deployment decisions. However, many benchmarks quickly become saturated, meaning that they can no longer differentiate between the best-performing models, diminishing their long-term value. In this study, we analyze benchmark saturation across 60 Large Language Model (LLM) benchmarks selected from technical reports by major model developers. To identify factors driving saturation, we characterize benchmarks along 14 properties spanning task design, data construction, and evaluation format. We test five hypotheses examining how each property contributes to saturation rates. Our analysis reveals that nearly half of the benchmarks exhibit saturation, with rates increasing as benchmarks age. Notably, hiding test data (i.e., public vs. private) shows no protective effect, while expert-curated benchmarks resist saturation better than crowdsourced ones. Our findings highlight which design choices extend benchmark longevity and inform strategies for more durable evaluation.

AI.tldr: Around half of 60 LLM benchmarks saturate, with saturation growing as benchmarks age; public vs. private data provides no protection, while expert-curated benchmarks resist saturation better than crowdsourced ones; the study identifies 14 design/evaluation properties and five hypotheses to guide durable benchmark design.
AI.motivation: Benchmark saturation erodes the discriminatory power of evaluations; understanding drivers enables creation of more durable, informative benchmarks for LLM progress and deployment decisions.
AI.method: Catalog 14 properties spanning task design, data construction, and evaluation format across 60 LLM benchmarks from major developers' technical reports; formulate and test five hypotheses about how each property influences saturation rates; quantify saturation prevalence and its relation to benchmark age and curation method.
AI.result: Approximately 50% of benchmarks saturate; saturation rates increase with benchmark age. Hiding test data (public vs. private) shows no protective effect. Expert-curated benchmarks resist saturation better than crowdsourced ones.
AI.conclusion: Design choices affect benchmark longevity; the findings inform strategies for creating more durable evaluations by prioritizing properties associated with resilience to saturation and considering curation approaches to extend the useful lifetime of benchmarks.

title: DeepVision-103K: A Visually Diverse, Broad-Coverage, and Verifiable Mathematical Dataset for Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has been shown effective in enhancing the visual reflection and reasoning capabilities of Large Multimodal Models (LMMs). However, existing datasets are predominantly derived from either small-scale manual construction or recombination of prior resources, which limits data diversity and coverage, thereby constraining further gains in model performance. To this end, we introduce \textbf{DeepVision-103K}, a comprehensive dataset for RLVR training that covers diverse K12 mathematical topics, extensive knowledge points, and rich visual elements. Models trained on DeepVision achieve strong performance on multimodal mathematical benchmarks, and generalize effectively to general multimodal reasoning tasks. Further analysis reveals enhanced visual perception, reflection and reasoning capabilities in trained models, validating DeepVision's effectiveness for advancing multimodal reasoning. Data: \href{https://huggingface.co/datasets/skylenage/DeepVision-103K}{this url}.

AI.tldr: Introduce DeepVision-103K, a large-scale RLVR dataset for training LMMs to improve visual perception, reflection, and reasoning in multimodal mathematics and general reasoning; demonstrates strong results and generalization on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current RLVR datasets are limited in diversity and coverage due to reliance on small-scale manual construction or recombination of existing data, constraining improvements in multimodal reasoning.
AI.method: Create DeepVision-103K RLVR dataset covering diverse K12 mathematical topics, knowledge points, and rich visual elements; train LMMs with RLVR data; evaluate on multimodal mathematical benchmarks and general multimodal reasoning tasks; analyze perceptual and reasoning capabilities.
AI.result: Models trained on DeepVision-103K achieve strong performance on multimodal mathematical benchmarks and generalize to broader multimodal reasoning tasks; analyses indicate enhanced visual perception, reflection, and reasoning.
AI.conclusion: DeepVision-103K is an effective resource to advance multimodal reasoning and scaling RLVR data, with demonstrated improvements and public availability (dataset link).

title: Xray-Visual Models: Scaling Vision models on Industry Scale Data
summary: We present Xray-Visual, a unified vision model architecture for large-scale image and video understanding trained on industry-scale social media data. Our model leverages over 15 billion curated image-text pairs and 10 billion video-hashtag pairs from Facebook and Instagram, employing robust data curation pipelines that incorporate balancing and noise suppression strategies to maximize semantic diversity while minimizing label noise. We introduce a three-stage training pipeline that combines self-supervised MAE, semi-supervised hashtag classification, and CLIP-style contrastive learning to jointly optimize image and video modalities. Our architecture builds on a Vision Transformer backbone enhanced with efficient token reorganization (EViT) for improved computational efficiency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Xray-Visual achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse benchmarks, including ImageNet for image classification, Kinetics and HMDB51 for video understanding, and MSCOCO for cross-modal retrieval. The model exhibits strong robustness to domain shift and adversarial perturbations. We further demonstrate that integrating large language models as text encoders (LLM2CLIP) significantly enhances retrieval performance and generalization capabilities, particularly in real-world environments. Xray-Visual establishes new benchmarks for scalable, multimodal vision models, while maintaining superior accuracy and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: Xray-Visual is a unified vision model for large-scale image and video understanding trained on 15B image-text and 10B video-hashtag pairs, using a three-stage training pipeline (MAE, semi-supervised hashtag classification, CLIP-style contrastive learning) with a ViT backbone and EViT for efficiency, achieving state-of-the-art results on ImageNet, Kinetics, HMDB51, and MSCOCO, and enhanced by LLM-based text encoders (LLM2CLIP).
AI.motivation: The work targets scalable, robust, multimodal vision models capable of handling both images and videos at industry scale, leveraging noisy social-media data while mitigating noise and distribution shifts.
AI.method: Data: extensive curated image-text and video-hashtag pairs with balancing and noise suppression. Model: Vision Transformer with efficient token reorganization (EViT). Training: three-stage pipeline combining self-supervised MAE, semi-supervised hashtag classification, and CLIP-style contrastive learning to align modalities.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on ImageNet (image classification), Kinetics and HMDB51 (video understanding), and MSCOCO (cross-modal retrieval). Demonstrates robustness to domain shift and adversarial perturbations. Integrating large language models as text encoders (LLM2CLIP) further improves retrieval and generalization in real-world settings.
AI.conclusion: Xray-Visual establishes scalable, high-accuracy, and efficient multimodal vision capabilities and introduces benchmarks for large-scale multimodal models; LLM-augmented retrieval shows practical gains in real-world deployments.

title: SimToolReal: An Object-Centric Policy for Zero-Shot Dexterous Tool Manipulation
summary: The ability to manipulate tools significantly expands the set of tasks a robot can perform. Yet, tool manipulation represents a challenging class of dexterity, requiring grasping thin objects, in-hand object rotations, and forceful interactions. Since collecting teleoperation data for these behaviors is challenging, sim-to-real reinforcement learning (RL) is a promising alternative. However, prior approaches typically require substantial engineering effort to model objects and tune reward functions for each task. In this work, we propose SimToolReal, taking a step towards generalizing sim-to-real RL policies for tool manipulation. Instead of focusing on a single object and task, we procedurally generate a large variety of tool-like object primitives in simulation and train a single RL policy with the universal goal of manipulating each object to random goal poses. This approach enables SimToolReal to perform general dexterous tool manipulation at test-time without any object or task-specific training. We demonstrate that SimToolReal outperforms prior retargeting and fixed-grasp methods by 37% while matching the performance of specialist RL policies trained on specific target objects and tasks. Finally, we show that SimToolReal generalizes across a diverse set of everyday tools, achieving strong zero-shot performance over 120 real-world rollouts spanning 24 tasks, 12 object instances, and 6 tool categories.

AI.tldr: A single, generalizable sim-to-real RL policy trained on diverse tool-like primitives enables broad dexterous tool manipulation with zero-shot transfer and competitive performance to specialist methods.
AI.motivation: Overcome the need for per-tool engineering in dexterous tool manipulation by learning a general policy across varied tool geometries and tasks.
AI.method: Procedurally generate a large distribution of tool-like object primitives in simulation; train one RL policy to manipulate each object to random goal poses using a universal goal formulation; transfer to real robots without object- or task-specific training.
AI.result: Outperforms prior retargeting and fixed-grasp baselines by 37%; matches performance of specialist RL policies trained on specific target objects and tasks; demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization across 120 real-world rollouts (24 tasks, 12 object instances, 6 tool categories).
AI.conclusion: SimToolReal demonstrates scalable generalization in sim-to-real tool manipulation, reducing the need for object-/task-specific engineering and enabling broad applicability across everyday tools; supports a path toward universal dexterous manipulation policies.

title: Simple Baselines are Competitive with Code Evolution
summary: Code evolution is a family of techniques that rely on large language models to search through possible computer programs by evolving or mutating existing code. Many proposed code evolution pipelines show impressive performance but are often not compared to simpler baselines. We test how well two simple baselines do over three domains: finding better mathematical bounds, designing agentic scaffolds, and machine learning competitions. We find that simple baselines match or exceed much more sophisticated methods in all three. By analyzing these results we find various shortcomings in how code evolution is both developed and used. For the mathematical bounds, a problem's search space and domain knowledge in the prompt are chiefly what dictate a search's performance ceiling and efficiency, with the code evolution pipeline being secondary. Thus, the primary challenge in finding improved bounds is designing good search spaces, which is done by domain experts, and not the search itself. When designing agentic scaffolds we find that high variance in the scaffolds coupled with small datasets leads to suboptimal scaffolds being selected, resulting in hand-designed majority vote scaffolds performing best. We propose better evaluation methods that reduce evaluation stochasticity while keeping the code evolution economically feasible. We finish with a discussion of avenues and best practices to enable more rigorous code evolution in future work.

AI.tldr: Simple baselines rival sophisticated code-evolution pipelines across three domains; highlights issues with evaluation, search-space design, and data sensitivity; recommends stronger baselines and evaluation practices; calls for rigorous code-evolution methodology.
AI.motivation: Assess whether advanced code-evolution methods truly outperform simple baselines and diagnose evaluation and design bottlenecks in current practice.
AI.method: Empirically compare two simple baselines against more sophisticated code-evolution pipelines across three domains: finding improved mathematical bounds, designing agentic scaffolds, and ML competition performance. Analyze what governs performance ceilings (search space, domain knowledge), the impact of variance and data size, and the effectiveness of evaluation protocols.
AI.result: Baselines match or exceed complex approaches in all three domains. For mathematical bounds, search-space design and prompt-domain knowledge chiefly determine performance; the code-evolution pipeline is secondary. In agentic scaffolds, high variance with small datasets leads to suboptimal scaffolds, making hand-designed majority-vote scaffolds often best. Propose evaluation methods that reduce stochasticity while maintaining economic feasibility.
AI.conclusion: The primary gains come from careful search-space design and robust evaluation practices rather than from novel code-evolution pipelines. The work advocates rigorous evaluation, domain-expert prompt design, and best-practice guidelines to improve future code-evolution research.

title: PETS: A Principled Framework Towards Optimal Trajectory Allocation for Efficient Test-Time Self-Consistency
summary: Test-time scaling can improve model performance by aggregating stochastic reasoning trajectories. However, achieving sample-efficient test-time self-consistency under a limited budget remains an open challenge. We introduce PETS (Principled and Efficient Test-TimeSelf-Consistency), which initiates a principled study of trajectory allocation through an optimization framework. Central to our approach is the self-consistency rate, a new measure defined as agreement with the infinite-budget majority vote. This formulation makes sample-efficient test-time allocation theoretically grounded and amenable to rigorous analysis. We study both offline and online settings. In the offline regime, where all questions are known in advance, we connect trajectory allocation to crowdsourcing, a classic and well-developed area, by modeling reasoning traces as workers. This perspective allows us to leverage rich existing theory, yielding theoretical guarantees and an efficient majority-voting-based allocation algorithm. In the online streaming regime, where questions arrive sequentially and allocations must be made on the fly, we propose a novel method inspired by the offline framework. Our approach adapts budgets to question difficulty while preserving strong theoretical guarantees and computational efficiency. Experiments show that PETS consistently outperforms uniform allocation. On GPQA, PETS achieves perfect self-consistency in both settings while reducing the sampling budget by up to 75% (offline) and 55% (online) relative to uniform allocation. Code is available at https://github.com/ZDCSlab/PETS.

AI.tldr: PETS offers a principled, efficient framework for test-time self-consistency by optimizing trajectory allocation under offline (known questions) and online (streaming) regimes, using a crowdsourcing lens and a new self-consistency rate; achieves strong empirical gains and theoretical guarantees, including perfect self-consistency on GPQA and substantial sampling reductions.
AI.motivation: Improve sample-efficient test-time self-consistency for stochastic reasoning trajectories under limited budgets; provide a principled allocation framework anchored in crowdsourcing theory and formal guarantees.
AI.method: Define self-consistency rate as agreement with the infinite-budget majority vote. Offline: model reasoning traces as workers from crowdsourcing theory, apply a majority-voting-based allocation algorithm with theoretical guarantees. Online: develop an adaptive-budget method that adjusts per-question allocations by difficulty while preserving guarantees and efficiency.
AI.result: Outperforms uniform allocation across settings. On GPQA, achieves perfect self-consistency in both offline and online regimes. Reduces sampling budget by up to 75% offline and 55% online. Code released.
AI.conclusion: PETS provides a principled, efficient framework for test-time self-consistency with strong theoretical and empirical performance in both offline and online regimes, validated by substantial sample-efficiency gains and a clear pathway for future analysis via crowdsourcing theory.

title: HS-3D-NeRF: 3D Surface and Hyperspectral Reconstruction From Stationary Hyperspectral Images Using Multi-Channel NeRFs
summary: Advances in hyperspectral imaging (HSI) and 3D reconstruction have enabled accurate, high-throughput characterization of agricultural produce quality and plant phenotypes, both essential for advancing agricultural sustainability and breeding programs. HSI captures detailed biochemical features of produce, while 3D geometric data substantially improves morphological analysis. However, integrating these two modalities at scale remains challenging, as conventional approaches involve complex hardware setups incompatible with automated phenotyping systems. Recent advances in neural radiance fields (NeRF) offer computationally efficient 3D reconstruction but typically require moving-camera setups, limiting throughput and reproducibility in standard indoor agricultural environments. To address these challenges, we introduce HSI-SC-NeRF, a stationary-camera multi-channel NeRF framework for high-throughput hyperspectral 3D reconstruction targeting postharvest inspection of agricultural produce. Multi-view hyperspectral data is captured using a stationary camera while the object rotates within a custom-built Teflon imaging chamber providing diffuse, uniform illumination. Object poses are estimated via ArUco calibration markers and transformed to the camera frame of reference through simulated pose transformations, enabling standard NeRF training on stationary-camera data. A multi-channel NeRF formulation optimizes reconstruction across all hyperspectral bands jointly using a composite spectral loss, supported by a two-stage training protocol that decouples geometric initialization from radiometric refinement. Experiments on three agricultural produce samples demonstrate high spatial reconstruction accuracy and strong spectral fidelity across the visible and near-infrared spectrum, confirming the suitability of HSI-SC-NeRF for integration into automated agricultural workflows.

AI.tldr: A stationary-camera, multi-channel NeRF framework (HSI-SC-NeRF) enables high-throughput hyperspectral 3D reconstruction of postharvest produce by combining stationary multi-view hyperspectral data with a rotated-object chamber, achieving joint geometric and spectral reconstruction with a two-stage training strategy.
AI.motivation: Integrating hyperspectral imaging with 3D geometry improves phenotypic characterization, but existing methods require complex hardware or moving-camera NeRFs that hurt throughput in indoor agriculture. A stationary-camera approach could democratize high-throughput HSI-3D reconstruction.
AI.method: Propose HSI-SC-NeRF: stationary camera captures multi-view hyperspectral data as the object rotates in a Teflon chamber under diffuse illumination. Pose estimation via ArUco markers, transformed to camera frame with simulated pose transformations. Train a multi-channel NeRF across spectral bands with a composite spectral loss; two-stage training (geometric initialization, radiometric refinement) to decouple geometry and spectral calibration.
AI.result: Experiments on three produce samples show high spatial reconstruction accuracy and strong spectral fidelity across visible and near-infrared, validating suitability for automated workflows.
AI.conclusion: HSI-SC-NeRF enables scalable, automated hyperspectral 3D reconstruction in indoor farming settings, facilitating integration into phenotyping and breeding pipelines; potential for higher throughput and consistent illumination, with room for scaling and robustness studies.

title: Boreas Road Trip: A Multi-Sensor Autonomous Driving Dataset on Challenging Roads
comment: 23 pages, 15 figures, 12 tables, submitted to The International Journal of Robotics Research (IJRR)
summary: The Boreas Road Trip (Boreas-RT) dataset extends the multi-season Boreas dataset to new and diverse locations that pose challenges for modern autonomous driving algorithms. Boreas-RT comprises 60 sequences collected over 9 real-world routes, totalling 643 km of driving. Each route is traversed multiple times, enabling evaluation in identical environments under varying traffic and, in some cases, weather conditions. The data collection platform includes a 5MP FLIR Blackfly S camera, a 360 degree Navtech RAS6 Doppler-enabled spinning radar, a 128-channel 360 degree Velodyne Alpha Prime lidar, an Aeva Aeries II FMCW Doppler-enabled lidar, a Silicon Sensing DMU41 inertial measurement unit, and a Dynapar wheel encoder. Centimetre-level ground truth is provided via post-processed Applanix POS LV GNSS-INS data. The dataset includes precise extrinsic and intrinsic calibrations, a publicly available development kit, and a live leaderboard for odometry and metric localization. Benchmark results show that many state-of-the-art odometry and localization algorithms overfit to simple driving environments and degrade significantly on the more challenging Boreas-RT routes. Boreas-RT provides a unified dataset for evaluating multi-modal algorithms across diverse road conditions. The dataset, leaderboard, and development kit are available at www.boreas.utias.utoronto.ca.

AI.tldr: Boreas-RT extends Boreas with 60 sequences over 9 routes (643 km) to stress-test odometry/localization using multi-sensor data, ground-truth GNSS-INS, and a development kit with a live leaderboard; results show current state-of-the-art methods overfit on simple drives and degrade on Boreas-RT’s challenging routes.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified, diverse, real-world benchmark for evaluating robustness and generalization of multi-modal odometry/localization algorithms beyond common, less challenging datasets.
AI.method: Data collection using a multi-sensor rig (5MP FLIR camera, Navtech 360 radar, Velodyne Alpha Prime lidar, Aeva FMCW lidar, IMU, wheel encoder) with centimetre-level ground truth from post-processed GNSS-INS. Each route is traversed multiple times under varying traffic and weather. The dataset includes precise calibrations, a development kit, and a live leaderboard for odometry and metric localization.
AI.result: Benchmarking reveals that many state-of-the-art odometry/localization methods overfit to simple driving scenes and degrade significantly on Boreas-RT’s more challenging routes, highlighting the need for cross-condition generalization.
AI.conclusion: Boreas-RT provides a unified, multi-modal benchmark for evaluating algorithms across diverse road conditions, with public data, tools, and leaderboards to facilitate robust evaluation and comparison.

title: Improved Upper Bounds for Slicing the Hypercube
summary: A collection of hyperplanes $\mathcal{H}$ slices all edges of the $n$-dimensional hypercube $Q_n$ with vertex set $\{-1,1\}^n$ if, for every edge $e$ in the hypercube, there exists a hyperplane in $\mathcal{H}$ intersecting $e$ in its interior. Let $S(n)$ be the minimum number of hyperplanes needed to slice $Q_n$. We prove that $S(n) \leq \lceil \frac{4n}{5} \rceil$, except when $n$ is an odd multiple of $5$, in which case $S(n) \leq \frac{4n}{5} +1$. This improves upon the previously known upper bound of $S(n) \leq \lceil\frac{5n}{6} \rceil$ due to Paterson reported in 1971. We also obtain new lower bounds on the maximum number of edges in $Q_n$ that can be sliced using $k<n$ hyperplanes. We prove the improved upper bound on $S(n)$ by constructing $8$ hyperplanes slicing $Q_{10}$ aided by the recently introduced CPro1: an automatic tool that uses reasoning LLMs coupled with automated hyperparameter tuning to create search algorithms for the discovery of mathematical constructions.

AI.tldr: Improved upper bound on the edge-slicing number S(n) for the n-cube: S(n) ≤ ceil(4n/5), with an exception when n is an odd multiple of 5 where S(n) ≤ 4n/5 + 1. This tightens the previous bound ceil(5n/6). Also develops new lower bounds on the maximum number of edges that can be sliced by k < n hyperplanes. The bound is achieved by an explicit 8-hyperplane construction for Q10, aided by CPro1, an AI-assisted search tool that uses LLMs to generate search algorithms for mathematical constructions.
AI.motivation: To determine the minimum number of hyperplanes needed to intersect every edge of the n-dimensional hypercube, a fundamental problem in extremal combinatorial geometry and hyperplane arrangements. The work aims to close the gap between known upper and lower bounds and explores AI-assisted methods for constructive proofs.
AI.method: Combines combinatorial construction with explicit hyperplane design and lower-bound analysis. Proves the improved S(n) bound by providing an explicit set of hyperplanes for a representative case (n=10, eight planes) and extends to general n with the stated bound. Employs CPro1, an automatic tool that couples reasoning LLMs with automated hyperparameter tuning to discover search algorithms for constructing such hyperplane configurations.
AI.result: Main results: S(n) ≤ ceil(4n/5) for all n, with the exception that if n is an odd multiple of 5, S(n) ≤ 4n/5 + 1. This improves the prior bound S(n) ≤ ceil(5n/6). Also obtains new lower bounds on the maximum number of edges that can be sliced by k < n hyperplanes. Demonstrates the bound by an 8-hyperplane construction for Q10, aided by CPro1.
AI.conclusion: The paper sharpens the asymptotic upper bound on the edge-slicing number for hypercubes and contributes new lower bounds; it also showcases a novel AI-assisted constructive approach for geometric combinatorics via CPro1. Open questions include tightening bounds for remaining n, understanding the odd-multiple-of-5 exception, and extending AI-assisted search to broader constructions.

title: Low-Dimensional and Transversely Curved Optimization Dynamics in Grokking
summary: Grokking -- the delayed transition from memorization to generalization in small algorithmic tasks -- remains poorly understood. We present a geometric analysis of optimization dynamics in transformers trained on modular arithmetic.
