PCA of attention weight trajectories reveals that training evolves predominantly within a low-dimensional execution subspace, with a single principal component capturing 68-83% of trajectory variance. To probe loss-landscape geometry, we measure commutator defects -- the non-commutativity of successive gradient steps -- and project them onto this learned subspace. We find that curvature grows sharply in directions orthogonal to the execution subspace while the trajectory remains largely confined to it. Importantly, curvature growth consistently precedes generalization across learning rates and hyperparameter regimes, with the lead time obeying a power law in the grokking timescale. Causal intervention experiments show that motion along the learned subspace is necessary for grokking, while artificially increasing curvature is insufficient. Together, these results support a geometric account in which grokking reflects escape from a metastable regime characterized by low-dimensional confinement and transverse curvature accumulation. All findings replicate across this learning-rate range, a qualitatively different slow regime (lr=5e-5, wd=0.1, 3 layers), and three random seeds, though alignment dynamics differ quantitatively between regimes. Causal intervention experiments establish that orthogonal gradient flow is necessary but not sufficient for grokking: suppressing it prevents generalization with a monotonic dose-response across four operations, while artificially boosting curvature defects has no effect.

AI.tldr: Grokking is explained as a geometric transition: training dynamics concentrate in a low-dimensional execution subspace; transverse curvature accumulation, rather than in-subspace dynamics, triggers generalization; orthogonal gradient flow is necessary but not sufficient.
AI.motivation: Understand late generalization (grokking) in transformers on modular arithmetic tasks; identify geometric/dynamical signals predicting grokking.
AI.method: PCA on attention-weight trajectories to identify subspace; measure commutator defects (non-commuting successive gradient steps) projected onto subspace; analyze curvature growth; perform causal interventions; test across learning rates, hyperparameters, seeds.
AI.result: Most variance explained by a single PC; 68-83% variance; curvature grows in directions orthogonal to subspace; curvature growth precedes generalization with power-law relation; orthogonal gradient flow necessary for grokking; boosting curvature defects alone ineffective; results robust across lr regimes and seeds; alignments differ between regimes.
AI.conclusion: Grokking corresponds to escape from metastable confinement; motion along subspace is necessary for generalization; transverse curvature accumulation drives the transition; geometric account supported; orthogonal gradient flow is necessary but not sufficient.

title: DDiT: Dynamic Patch Scheduling for Efficient Diffusion Transformers
summary: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have achieved state-of-the-art performance in image and video generation, but their success comes at the cost of heavy computation. This inefficiency is largely due to the fixed tokenization process, which uses constant-sized patches throughout the entire denoising phase, regardless of the content's complexity. We propose dynamic tokenization, an efficient test-time strategy that varies patch sizes based on content complexity and the denoising timestep. Our key insight is that early timesteps only require coarser patches to model global structure, while later iterations demand finer (smaller-sized) patches to refine local details. During inference, our method dynamically reallocates patch sizes across denoising steps for image and video generation and substantially reduces cost while preserving perceptual generation quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach: it achieves up to $3.52\times$ and $3.2\times$ speedup on FLUX-1.Dev and Wan $2.1$, respectively, without compromising the generation quality and prompt adherence.

AI.tldr: Dynamic tokenization for diffusion transformers reduces compute by adaptively varying patch sizes across timesteps, achieving up to ~3.5x speedups on image/video benchmarks while preserving quality.
AI.motivation: Address heavy compute of Diffusion Transformers caused by fixed, constant patch sizes; exploit that early steps capture global structure while later steps need detail.
AI.method: Introduce a test-time dynamic tokenization strategy that reallocates patch sizes per timestep and content complexity; apply to image and video generation; reallocation across denoising steps to balance global structure and local detail.
AI.result: Reported speedups up to 3.52x (FLUX-1.Dev) and 3.2x (Wan 2.1) with no loss in perceptual quality or prompt adherence.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic tokenization is an effective approach to reduce computation in diffusion transformers without sacrificing generation quality; extends applicability to both image and video generation.

title: MALLVI: a multi agent framework for integrated generalized robotics manipulation
summary: Task planning for robotic manipulation with large language models (LLMs) is an emerging area. Prior approaches rely on specialized models, fine tuning, or prompt tuning, and often operate in an open loop manner without robust environmental feedback, making them fragile in dynamic settings.We present MALLVi, a Multi Agent Large Language and Vision framework that enables closed loop feedback driven robotic manipulation. Given a natural language instruction and an image of the environment, MALLVi generates executable atomic actions for a robot manipulator. After action execution, a Vision Language Model (VLM) evaluates environmental feedback and decides whether to repeat the process or proceed to the next step.Rather than using a single model, MALLVi coordinates specialized agents, Decomposer, Localizer, Thinker, and Reflector, to manage perception, localization, reasoning, and high level planning. An optional Descriptor agent provides visual memory of the initial state. The Reflector supports targeted error detection and recovery by reactivating only relevant agents, avoiding full replanning.Experiments in simulation and real world settings show that iterative closed loop multi agent coordination improves generalization and increases success rates in zero shot manipulation tasks.Code available at https://github.com/iman1234ahmadi/MALLVI.

AI.tldr: MALLVi proposes a multi-agent, closed-loop framework that combines LLMs and vision to execute and iteratively refine robotic manipulation tasks via specialized agents and visual feedback.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based robotic planning often relies on open-loop strategies or fine-tuned models and struggles in dynamic environments. There is a need for robust, generalizable, feedback-driven architectures that can coordinate perception, localization, reasoning, and planning.
AI.method: MALLVi introduces a multi-agent system with Decomposer, Localizer, Thinker, Reflector (and an optional Descriptor for memory) that operate on natural language instructions and environment images. An onboard Vision-Language Model provides environmental feedback after each atomic action, guiding repetition or progression. Reflector enables targeted error detection and partial replanning instead of full replanning.
AI.result: Experiments in both simulation and real-world settings show that iterative, closed-loop multi-agent coordination improves generalization and increases success rates on zero-shot manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: MALLVi demonstrates that coordinating specialized agents with LLMs and VLM feedback enables robust, closed-loop robotic manipulation with improved generalization and resilience to errors.

title: NeuDiff Agent: A Governed AI Workflow for Single-Crystal Neutron Crystallography
summary: Large-scale facilities increasingly face analysis and reporting latency as the limiting step in scientific throughput, particularly for structurally and magnetically complex samples that require iterative reduction, integration, refinement, and validation. To improve time-to-result and analysis efficiency, NeuDiff Agent is introduced as a governed, tool-using AI workflow for TOPAZ at the Spallation Neutron Source that takes instrument data products through reduction, integration, refinement, and validation to a validated crystal structure and a publication-ready CIF. NeuDiff Agent executes this established pipeline under explicit governance by restricting actions to allowlisted tools, enforcing fail-closed verification gates at key workflow boundaries, and capturing complete provenance for inspection, auditing, and controlled replay. Performance is assessed using a fixed prompt protocol and repeated end-to-end runs with two large language model backends, with user and machine time partitioned and intervention burden and recovery behaviors quantified under gating. In a reference-case benchmark, NeuDiff Agent reduces wall time from 435 minutes (manual) to 86.5(4.7) to 94.4(3.5) minutes (4.6-5.0x faster) while producing a validated CIF with no checkCIF level A or B alerts. These results establish a practical route to deploy agentic AI in facility crystallography while preserving traceability and publication-facing validation requirements.

AI.tldr: NeuDiff Agent provides a governance-enabled AI workflow for TOPAZ crystallography that automates reduction, integration, refinement, and validation to a publication-ready CIF, with strict tool governance and complete provenance; achieves ~4.6–5× speed-ups and CIF validation without checkCIF alerts.
AI.motivation: To address mounting analysis and reporting latency in large-scale facilities for structurally and magnetically complex samples, aiming to improve time-to-result and analysis efficiency while ensuring traceability and publication-grade validation.
AI.method: Developed NeuDiff Agent that restricts actions to allowlisted tools, enforces fail-closed verification gates at workflow boundaries, captures complete provenance, and enables controlled replay. Evaluation uses a fixed prompt protocol, repeated end-to-end runs with two LLM backends, with partitioned user/machine time and quantified intervention burden and recovery under gating.
AI.result: In a reference-case benchmark, wall time is reduced from 435 minutes (manual) to 86.5(4.7) to 94.4(3.5) minutes (4.6–5.0× faster) while producing a validated CIF with no checkCIF level A or B alerts.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a practical route to deploying agentic AI in facility crystallography that preserves traceability and publication-facing validation requirements; supports rapid, auditable, and reproducible analysis pipelines.

title: LiveClin: A Live Clinical Benchmark without Leakage
summary: The reliability of medical LLM evaluation is critically undermined by data contamination and knowledge obsolescence, leading to inflated scores on static benchmarks. To address these challenges, we introduce LiveClin, a live benchmark designed for approximating real-world clinical practice. Built from contemporary, peer-reviewed case reports and updated biannually, LiveClin ensures clinical currency and resists data contamination. Using a verified AI-human workflow involving 239 physicians, we transform authentic patient cases into complex, multimodal evaluation scenarios that span the entire clinical pathway. The benchmark currently comprises 1,407 case reports and 6,605 questions. Our evaluation of 26 models on LiveClin reveals the profound difficulty of these real-world scenarios, with the top-performing model achieving a Case Accuracy of just 35.7%. In benchmarking against human experts, Chief Physicians achieved the highest accuracy, followed closely by Attending Physicians, with both surpassing most models. LiveClin thus provides a continuously evolving, clinically grounded framework to guide the development of medical LLMs towards closing this gap and achieving greater reliability and real-world utility. Our data and code are publicly available at https://github.com/AQ-MedAI/LiveClin.

AI.tldr: LiveClin is a live, contamination-resistant benchmark for medical LLMs that mirrors real-world practice by using up-to-date peer-reviewed cases and an AI-human workflow; among 26 models, the best achieved 35.7% Case Accuracy, with Chief/Attending Physicians outperforming most models; data and code are public.
AI.motivation: Static medical benchmarks suffer from data contamination and knowledge obsolescence, inflating performance; a continuously updated, clinically grounded benchmark is needed to accurately assess and guide medical LLM development.
AI.method: Construct LiveClin from contemporary peer-reviewed case reports, updated biannually; use a verified AI-human workflow involving 239 physicians to convert authentic cases into multimodal evaluation tasks spanning the clinical path; consist of 1,407 case reports and 6,605 questions; evaluate 26 models.
AI.result: Top model achieves 35.7% Case Accuracy; humans (Chief Physicians, Attending Physicians) outperform most models; demonstrates substantial gap between current models and real-world clinical performance; LiveClin proves resistant to data contamination and clinically current.
AI.conclusion: LiveClin offers a continuously evolving, clinically grounded framework to guide medical LLM development toward reliability and real-world utility; data and code are publicly available.

title: Characterizing the Predictive Impact of Modalities with Supervised Latent-Variable Modeling
summary: Despite the recent success of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), existing approaches predominantly assume the availability of multiple modalities during training and inference. In practice, multimodal data is often incomplete because modalities may be missing, collected asynchronously, or available only for a subset of examples. In this work, we propose PRIMO, a supervised latent-variable imputation model that quantifies the predictive impact of any missing modality within the multimodal learning setting. PRIMO enables the use of all available training examples, whether modalities are complete or partial. Specifically, it models the missing modality through a latent variable that captures its relationship with the observed modality in the context of prediction. During inference, we draw many samples from the learned distribution over the missing modality to both obtain the marginal predictive distribution (for the purpose of prediction) and analyze the impact of the missing modalities on the prediction for each instance. We evaluate PRIMO on a synthetic XOR dataset, Audio-Vision MNIST, and MIMIC-III for mortality and ICD-9 prediction. Across all datasets, PRIMO obtains performance comparable to unimodal baselines when a modality is fully missing and to multimodal baselines when all modalities are available. PRIMO quantifies the predictive impact of a modality at the instance level using a variance-based metric computed from predictions across latent completions. We visually demonstrate how varying completions of the missing modality result in a set of plausible labels.

AI.tldr: PRIMO is a supervised latent-variable imputation model for incomplete multimodal data that estimates marginal predictions via sampling missing modalities and provides per-instance modality impact via variance across completions; validated on XOR, Audio-Visual MNIST, and MIMIC-III with competitive performance across missing and complete modality scenarios.
AI.motivation: Multimodal models often assume complete modality availability, but real data is frequently incomplete due to missing, asynchronous, or partially labeled modalities. A method that can utilize all available data and quantify the impact of missing modalities is needed.
AI.method: Model the missing modality as a latent variable that captures its relationship with observed modalities for prediction. Train to leverage both complete and partial multimodal data. During inference, sample many completions from the learned latent distribution to obtain marginal predictive distributions and to analyze modality impact. Evaluate with synthetic XOR, Audio-Vision MNIST, and MIMIC-III for mortality and ICD-9 prediction. Use a variance-based metric across latent completions to quantify per-instance predictive impact and visually illustrate plausible label sets under varying completions.
AI.result: PRIMO achieves performance comparable to unimodal baselines when a modality is fully missing and to multimodal baselines when all modalities are available. It provides a per-instance quantify of the predictive impact of missing modalities via a variance-based metric across completions. Demonstrations show how different latent completions yield plausible label sets.
AI.conclusion: PRIMO enables effective learning with incomplete multimodal data and offers interpretability by quantifying the impact of missing modalities on predictions at the instance level, suitable for applications requiring robust handling of incomplete multimodal information.

title: SparTa: Sparse Graphical Task Models from a Handful of Demonstrations
summary: Learning long-horizon manipulation tasks efficiently is a central challenge in robot learning from demonstration. Unlike recent endeavors that focus on directly learning the task in the action domain, we focus on inferring what the robot should achieve in the task, rather than how to do so. To this end, we represent evolving scene states using a series of graphical object relationships. We propose a demonstration segmentation and pooling approach that extracts a series of manipulation graphs and estimates distributions over object states across task phases. In contrast to prior graph-based methods that capture only partial interactions or short temporal windows, our approach captures complete object interactions spanning from the onset of control to the end of the manipulation. To improve robustness when learning from multiple demonstrations, we additionally perform object matching using pre-trained visual features. In extensive experiments, we evaluate our method's demonstration segmentation accuracy and the utility of learning from multiple demonstrations for finding a desired minimal task model. Finally, we deploy the fitted models both in simulation and on a real robot, demonstrating that the resulting task representations support reliable execution across environments.

AI.tldr: A graph-based, long-horizon task representation learned from demonstrations via segmentation into manipulation graphs, pooling, and cross-demo object matching, enabling robust, transferable execution across environments.
AI.motivation: Addresses the challenge of long-horizon manipulation learning by shifting focus from how to perform actions to what the robot should achieve; aims to capture complete, temporally extended object interactions to improve robustness across demonstrations and environments.
AI.method: Represent evolving scene states as a sequence of manipulation graphs; perform demonstration segmentation and pooling to extract graphs and estimate object-state distributions across task phases; enhance robustness with object matching using pre-trained visual features to align objects across demonstrations.
AI.result: Demonstration segmentation accuracy is evaluated; learning from multiple demonstrations yields a minimal, robust task model; models are deployed in simulation and on a real robot, enabling reliable execution across environments.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a complete, graph-based representation of long-horizon manipulation tasks, enabling effective transfer and robust execution across varying environments by leveraging segmented manipulation graphs and cross-demo object matching.

title: Node Learning: A Framework for Adaptive, Decentralised and Collaborative Network Edge AI
comment: 16 pages, 3 figures, 3 tables, this paper introduces a new concept
summary: The expansion of AI toward the edge increasingly exposes the cost and fragility of cen- tralised intelligence. Data transmission, latency, energy consumption, and dependence on large data centres create bottlenecks that scale poorly across heterogeneous, mobile, and resource-constrained environments. In this paper, we introduce Node Learning, a decen- tralised learning paradigm in which intelligence resides at individual edge nodes and expands through selective peer interaction. Nodes learn continuously from local data, maintain their own model state, and exchange learned knowledge opportunistically when collaboration is beneficial. Learning propagates through overlap and diffusion rather than global synchro- nisation or central aggregation. It unifies autonomous and cooperative behaviour within a single abstraction and accommodates heterogeneity in data, hardware, objectives, and connectivity. This concept paper develops the conceptual foundations of this paradigm, contrasts it with existing decentralised approaches, and examines implications for communi- cation, hardware, trust, and governance. Node Learning does not discard existing paradigms, but places them within a broader decentralised perspective

AI.tldr: Proposes Node Learning, a decentralized edge-AI paradigm where intelligence resides on individual edge nodes and knowledge propagates through selective peer interactions, avoiding centralized aggregation.
AI.motivation: Address bottlenecks of centralized AI in edge and heterogeneous environments: data transmission costs, latency, energy consumption, and dependence on data centres; enable autonomous, cooperative, and scalable learning across resource-constrained devices with diverse objectives.
AI.method: Conceptual framework establishing Node Learning, contrasting it with existing decentralized approaches, and detailing mechanisms of learning propagation via overlap and diffusion; emphasizes local model state, opportunistic knowledge exchange, and a single abstraction that unifies autonomous and cooperative behavior; discusses implications for communication, hardware, trust, and governance.
AI.result: The paper provides a foundational theoretical framework and comparative analysis for Node Learning. It defines the paradigm, outlines how knowledge propagates through overlap/diffusion without global synchronization, and positions this approach as a unifying abstraction that encompasses heterogeneous data, hardware, objectives, and connectivity; no empirical validation is reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Node Learning is presented as a broad, integrative decentralised perspective that complements existing paradigms rather than replacing them, with implications for deployment, governance, and trust across communication and hardware landscapes.

title: Attending to Routers Aids Indoor Wireless Localization
comment: AAAI 2026 Workshop on Machine Learning for Wireless Communication and Networks (ML4Wireless)
summary: Modern machine learning-based wireless localization using Wi-Fi signals continues to face significant challenges in achieving groundbreaking performance across diverse environments. A major limitation is that most existing algorithms do not appropriately weight the information from different routers during aggregation, resulting in suboptimal convergence and reduced accuracy. Motivated by traditional weighted triangulation methods, this paper introduces the concept of attention to routers, ensuring that each router's contribution is weighted differently when aggregating information from multiple routers for triangulation. We demonstrate, by incorporating attention layers into a standard machine learning localization architecture, that emphasizing the relevance of each router can substantially improve overall performance. We have also shown through evaluation over the open-sourced datasets and demonstrate that Attention to Routers outperforms the benchmark architecture by over 30% in accuracy.

AI.tldr: Introducing router-level attention in ML-based Wi-Fi localization improves accuracy, achieving >30% gains on open datasets over a standard benchmark.
AI.motivation: Current aggregation in ML-based localization treats router contributions uniformly, leading to suboptimal convergence and accuracy; traditional weighted triangulation suggests that varying router importance should be considered.
AI.method: Incorporate attention layers to weight each router's information during aggregation within a standard ML localization architecture, effectively learning router relevance for triangulation.
AI.result: On open-source datasets, the Attention to Routers approach outperforms the benchmark by over 30% in localization accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Attention-weighted aggregation over routers is a promising method to enhance ML-based Wi-Fi localization, with substantial accuracy gains demonstrated; further work could explore generalization, ablations, and computational trade-offs.

title: Patch-Based Spatial Authorship Attribution in Human-Robot Collaborative Paintings
summary: As agentic AI becomes increasingly involved in creative production, documenting authorship has become critical for artists, collectors, and legal contexts. We present a patch-based framework for spatial authorship attribution within human-robot collaborative painting practice, demonstrated through a forensic case study of one human artist and one robotic system across 15 abstract paintings. Using commodity flatbed scanners and leave-one-painting-out cross-validation, the approach achieves 88.8% patch-level accuracy (86.7% painting-level via majority vote), outperforming texture-based and pretrained-feature baselines (68.0%-84.7%). For collaborative artworks, where ground truth is inherently ambiguous, we use conditional Shannon entropy to quantify stylistic overlap; manually annotated hybrid regions exhibit 64% higher uncertainty than pure paintings (p=0.003), suggesting the model detects mixed authorship rather than classification failure. The trained model is specific to this human-robot pair but provides a methodological grounding for sample-efficient attribution in data-scarce human-AI creative workflows that, in the future, has the potential to extend authorship attribution to any human-robot collaborative painting.

AI.tldr: Patch-based spatial authorship attribution for human-robot collaborative painting; demonstrates high patch-level and painting-level attribution accuracy on 15 abstract paintings using scanner-based data, with entropy-based overlap analysis in collaborative artworks.
AI.motivation: Address the need to document authorship in data-scarce human-AI creative workflows and to provide a methodological framework for sample-efficient attribution in human-robot collaboration where ground truth is ambiguous.
AI.method: Patch-based analysis applied to flatbed-scanned artworks with leave-one-painting-out cross-validation; comparison against texture-based and pretrained-feature baselines; use of conditional Shannon entropy to quantify stylistic overlap; manual annotation of hybrid regions to assess uncertainty.
AI.result: 88.8% patch-level accuracy and 86.7% painting-level accuracy (via majority vote); baselines achieved 68.0–84.7%; hybrid regions exhibited 64% higher uncertainty than pure paintings (p=0.003), indicating detection of mixed authorship rather than model failure; approach validated on 15 paintings.
AI.conclusion: The model is system-specific to the human-robot pair but offers a principled, sample-efficient framework for authorship attribution in data-scarce collaborative artworks and lays groundwork for extending attribution to other human-robot collaborations.

title: Benchmarking the Effects of Object Pose Estimation and Reconstruction on Robotic Grasping Success
summary: 3D reconstruction serves as the foundational layer for numerous robotic perception tasks, including 6D object pose estimation and grasp pose generation. Modern 3D reconstruction methods for objects can produce visually and geometrically impressive meshes from multi-view images, yet standard geometric evaluations do not reflect how reconstruction quality influences downstream tasks such as robotic manipulation performance. This paper addresses this gap by introducing a large-scale, physics-based benchmark that evaluates 6D pose estimators and 3D mesh models based on their functional efficacy in grasping. We analyze the impact of model fidelity by generating grasps on various reconstructed 3D meshes and executing them on the ground-truth model, simulating how grasp poses generated with an imperfect model affect interaction with the real object. This assesses the combined impact of pose error, grasp robustness, and geometric inaccuracies from 3D reconstruction. Our results show that reconstruction artifacts significantly decrease the number of grasp pose candidates but have a negligible effect on grasping performance given an accurately estimated pose. Our results also reveal that the relationship between grasp success and pose error is dominated by spatial error, and even a simple translation error provides insight into the success of the grasping pose of symmetric objects. This work provides insight into how perception systems relate to object manipulation using robots.

AI.tldr: A physics-based, large-scale benchmark assesses how 3D reconstruction quality affects 6D pose estimation and grasping; reconstruction artifacts reduce feasible grasp candidates but have limited impact on grasp success when the pose is accurate; spatial pose error dominates grasp failure, with translation errors notably affecting symmetric objects.
AI.motivation: To close the gap between synthetic/visual quality metrics for 3D reconstructions and real robotic manipulation performance, by evaluating how reconstruction fidelity translates to grasping effectiveness.
AI.method: Generate grasps on multiple reconstructed meshes; simulate execution against the ground-truth model to capture the joint effects of pose error, grasp robustness, and geometric inaccuracies; analyze how reconstruction artifacts and pose estimation errors influence grasp candidates and success.
AI.result: Reconstruction artifacts significantly reduce the number of grasp pose candidates but have negligible effect on grasping success when the pose is accurately estimated; grasp success is mainly governed by spatial errors in pose, and even simple translation errors reveal insights for symmetric objects.
AI.conclusion: The study clarifies how perception quality propagates to manipulation, highlighting the primacy of accurate pose (especially spatial alignment) for successful grasping and offering a benchmark to evaluate perception-to-action pipelines in robotics.

title: An order-oriented approach to scoring hesitant fuzzy elements
summary:
Traditional scoring approaches on hesitant fuzzy sets often lack a formal base in order theory. This paper proposes a unified framework, where each score is explicitly defined with respect to a given order. This order-oriented perspective enables more flexible and coherent scoring mechanisms. We examine several classical orders on hesitant fuzzy elements, that is, nonempty subsets in [0,1], and show that, contrary to prior claims, they do not induce lattice structures. In contrast, we prove that the scores defined with respect to the symmetric order satisfy key normative criteria for scoring functions, including strong monotonicity with respect to unions and the Gärdenfors condition.
  Following this analysis, we introduce a class of functions, called dominance functions, for ranking hesitant fuzzy elements. They aim to compare hesitant fuzzy elements relative to control sets incorporating minimum acceptability thresholds. Two concrete examples of dominance functions for finite sets are provided: the discrete dominance function and the relative dominance function. We show that these can be employed to construct fuzzy preference relations on typical hesitant fuzzy sets and support group decision-making.

AI.tldr: Unified order-based scoring for hesitant fuzzy elements; symmetric order scores satisfy key normative properties; dominance functions (discrete and relative) rank hesitating fuzzy elements with thresholds and enable group decision-making.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of a formal order-theoretic basis for scoring hesitant fuzzy elements, corrects claims about lattice structures, and provides normative scoring functions to support coherent ranking and aggregation in decision making.
AI.method: Analyze classical orders on hesitant fuzzy elements (nonempty subsets of [0,1]); prove they do not form lattice structures. Define scores with respect to the symmetric order and verify normative properties (strong monotonicity w.r.t unions, Gärdenfors condition). Introduce dominance functions to rank hesitant fuzzy elements relative to control sets with minimum acceptability thresholds. Provide two finite-set examples: discrete dominance function and relative dominance function. Demonstrate construction of fuzzy preference relations and applicability to group decision-making.
AI.result: Symmetric-order-based scoring functions satisfy key normative criteria for scoring—most notably strong monotonicity with respect to unions and the Gärdenfors condition. Dominance functions enable ranking of hesitant fuzzy elements; discrete and relative dominance functions can construct fuzzy preference relations, facilitating group decision-making.
AI.conclusion: An order-oriented framework yields coherent, normatively sound scoring for hesitant fuzzy elements. The symmetric order is preferable for normative scoring, and dominance functions offer practical tools for ranking with thresholds and for forming group decision-making structures via fuzzy preferences.

title: Machine Learning Argument of Latitude Error Model for LEO Satellite Orbit and Covariance Correction
summary: Low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites are leveraged to support new position, navigation, and timing (PNT) service alternatives to GNSS. These alternatives require accurate propagation of satellite position and velocity with a realistic quantification of uncertainty. It is commonly assumed that the propagated uncertainty distribution is Gaussian; however, the validity of this assumption can be quickly compromised by the mismodeling of atmospheric drag. We develop a machine learning approach that corrects error growth in the argument of latitude for a diverse set of LEO satellites. The improved orbit propagation accuracy extends the applicability of the Gaussian assumption and modeling of the errors with a corrected mean and covariance. We compare the performance of a time-conditioned neural network and a Gaussian Process on datasets computed with an open source orbit propagator and publicly available Vector Covariance Message (VCM) ephemerides. The learned models predict the argument of latitude error as a Gaussian distribution given parameters from a single VCM epoch and reverse propagation errors. We show that this one-dimensional model captures the effect of mismodeled drag, which can be mapped to the Cartesian state space. The correction method only updates information along the dimensions of dominant error growth, while maintaining the physics-based propagation of VCM covariance in the remaining dimensions. We therefore extend the utility of VCM ephemerides to longer time horizons without modifying the functionality of the existing propagator.

AI.tldr: A machine learning approach corrects error growth in LEO orbit propagation by modeling the argument of latitude error as Gaussian, addressing drag-mismodeling challenges. It compares a time-conditioned neural network and a Gaussian Process, trained on VCM ephemerides, and maps the 1D correction back to Cartesian state space to extend Gaussian-based uncertainty modeling for longer horizons without changing the propagator.
AI.motivation: Uncertain LEO propagation under atmospheric drag often violates Gaussian error assumptions; improving uncertainty quantification (UQ) is essential for PNT services that rely on realistic error statistics.
AI.method: Develop a one-dimensional, drag-mismodeling aware correction for the argument of latitude error using two ML approaches: a time-conditioned neural network and a Gaussian Process. Train on datasets from an open-source orbit propagator and publicly available Vector Covariance Message (VCM) ephemerides. The model predicts the ArgLat error as Gaussian conditioned on a single VCM epoch and reverse-propagation errors, capturing drag effects and mapping corrections to Cartesian state while preserving other covariance dynamics.
AI.result: The learned models produce Gaussian-distributed ArgLat errors given VCM inputs, effectively capturing mis-modeled drag effects and enabling a corrected, extended propagation horizon. The correction updates only the dominant error dimension and leaves the rest of the physics-based covariance intact, thereby enhancing VCM applicability.
AI.conclusion: The approach extends the utility of VCM ephemerides to longer time horizons without altering the underlying propagator, by providing a targeted, physics-consistent error correction that preserves overall covariance structure while improving accuracy in the dominant error mode.

title: PartRAG: Retrieval-Augmented Part-Level 3D Generation and Editing
summary: Single-image 3D generation with part-level structure remains challenging: learned priors struggle to cover the long tail of part geometries and maintain multi-view consistency, and existing systems provide limited support for precise, localized edits. We present PartRAG, a retrieval-augmented framework that integrates an external part database with a diffusion transformer to couple generation with an editable representation. To overcome the first challenge, we introduce a Hierarchical Contrastive Retrieval module that aligns dense image patches with 3D part latents at both part and object granularity, retrieving from a curated bank of 1,236 part-annotated assets to inject diverse, physically plausible exemplars into denoising. To overcome the second challenge, we add a masked, part-level editor that operates in a shared canonical space, enabling swaps, attribute refinements, and compositional updates without regenerating the whole object while preserving non-target parts and multi-view consistency. PartRAG achieves competitive results on Objaverse, ShapeNet, and ABO-reducing Chamfer Distance from 0.1726 to 0.1528 and raising F-Score from 0.7472 to 0.844 on Objaverse-with inference of 38s and interactive edits in 5-8s. Qualitatively, PartRAG produces sharper part boundaries, better thin-structure fidelity, and robust behavior on articulated objects. Code: https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/PartRAG. Website: https://aigeeksgroup.github.io/PartRAG.

AI.tldr: PartRAG uses retrieval-augmented diffusion transformer with hierarchical retrieval and part-level editing to improve single-image 3D generation, enabling editable, localized modifications while maintaining multi-view consistency.
AI.motivation: Tackle the long-tail of part geometries and limited support for precise, localized edits in single-image 3D generation, and improve multi-view consistency.
AI.method: Hierarchical Contrastive Retrieval aligns dense image patches with 3D part latents at part/object granularity, retrieving from a bank of 1,236 part-annotated assets to inject diverse, plausible exemplars into denoising. A masked, part-level editor operates in a shared canonical space to enable swaps, attribute refinements, and compositional updates without regenerating the whole object.
AI.result: On Objaverse, ShapeNet, and ABO, achieves Chamfer Distance reduction from 0.1726 to 0.1528 and F-Score increase from 0.7472 to 0.844 on Objaverse; inference time ~38s and interactive edits in 5-8s. Qualitatively, sharper part boundaries, better thin-structure fidelity, robust on articulated objects.
AI.conclusion: PartRAG demonstrates competitive 3D generation with editable, part-centric control via retrieval augmentation, improving geometry accuracy and editability while maintaining multi-view consistency.

title: Grasp Synthesis Matching From Rigid To Soft Robot Grippers Using Conditional Flow Matching
summary: A representation gap exists between grasp synthesis for rigid and soft grippers. Anygrasp [1] and many other grasp synthesis methods are designed for rigid parallel grippers, and adapting them to soft grippers often fails to capture their unique compliant behaviors, resulting in data-intensive and inaccurate models. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes a novel framework to map grasp poses from a rigid gripper model to a soft Fin-ray gripper. We utilize Conditional Flow Matching (CFM), a generative model, to learn this complex transformation. Our methodology includes a data collection pipeline to generate paired rigid-soft grasp poses. A U-Net autoencoder conditions the CFM model on the object's geometry from a depth image, allowing it to learn a continuous mapping from an initial Anygrasp pose to a stable Fin-ray gripper pose. We validate our approach on a 7-DOF robot, demonstrating that our CFM-generated poses achieve a higher overall success rate for seen and unseen objects (34% and 46% respectively) compared to the baseline rigid poses (6% and 25% respectively) when executed by the soft gripper. The model shows significant improvements, particularly for cylindrical (50% and 100% success for seen and unseen objects) and spherical objects (25% and 31% success for seen and unseen objects), and successfully generalizes to unseen objects. This work presents CFM as a data-efficient and effective method for transferring grasp strategies, offering a scalable methodology for other soft robotic systems.

AI.tldr: Maps grasp poses from rigid to soft Fin-ray grippers using Conditional Flow Matching (CFM), achieving higher data-efficient transfer and better generalization to unseen objects.
AI.motivation: Address the representation gap between rigid-gripper grasp synthesis (e.g., Anygrasp) and soft grippers, by learning a data-efficient mapping from rigid poses to soft gripper poses to improve grasp success on soft hardware.
AI.method: Collect paired rigid-soft grasp poses; train a conditional generative model (CFM) conditioned on object geometry (depth image) via a U-Net autoencoder to map an initial Anygrasp pose to a stable Fin-ray gripper pose; validate on a 7-DOF robot.
AI.result: CFM-generated poses outperform baseline rigid poses, with seen/unseen success rates of 34%/46% vs 6%/25%; cylinders and spheres show strong gains (cylinders: 50%/100%; spheres: 25%/31%); demonstrates improved generalization to unseen objects.
AI.conclusion: CFM offers a data-efficient, scalable method to transfer grasp strategies from rigid to soft robots and can generalize to unseen objects; applicable to other soft robotic systems.

title: Omitted Variable Bias in Language Models Under Distribution Shift
summary: Despite their impressive performance on a wide variety of tasks, modern language models remain susceptible to distribution shifts, exhibiting brittle behavior when evaluated on data that differs in distribution from their training data. In this paper, we describe how distribution shifts in language models can be separated into observable and unobservable components, and we discuss how established approaches for dealing with distribution shift address only the former. Importantly, we identify that the resulting omitted variable bias from unobserved variables can compromise both evaluation and optimization in language models. To address this challenge, we introduce a framework that maps the strength of the omitted variables to bounds on the worst-case generalization performance of language models under distribution shift. In empirical experiments, we show that using these bounds directly in language model evaluation and optimization provides more principled measures of out-of-distribution performance, improves true out-of-distribution performance relative to standard distribution shift adjustment methods, and further enables inference about the strength of the omitted variables when target distribution labels are available.

AI.tldr: A framework linking unobserved omitted-variable bias in distribution-shift settings to worst-case generalization bounds for language models, enabling principled OOD evaluation/optimization and inference on latent shifts.
AI.motivation: Language models are brittle under distribution shifts; existing methods address observable shifts but ignore unobserved variables that bias evaluation and optimization; need bounds to quantify worst-case impact of these omissions.
AI.method: Introduce a framework that quantifies the strength of omitted variables and translates that into bounds on worst-case generalization under distribution shift; incorporate these bounds into evaluation and optimization procedures; provide methods to infer omitted-variable strength when target-distribution labels are available.
AI.result: Bounds yield principled measures of out-of-distribution performance; they improve true OOD performance over standard shift-adjustment methods; they also enable inference about the strength of omitted variables given target distribution labels in experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed bounds enable more robust evaluation and optimization under distribution shift and offer insight into latent omitted variables, suggesting a pathway to more reliable deployment of language models in shifting environments.

title: Amber-Image: Efficient Compression of Large-Scale Diffusion Transformers
summary: Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architectures have significantly advanced Text-to-Image (T2I) generation but suffer from prohibitive computational costs and deployment barriers. To address these challenges, we propose an efficient compression framework that transforms the 60-layer dual-stream MMDiT-based Qwen-Image into lightweight models without training from scratch. Leveraging this framework, we introduce Amber-Image, a series of streamlined T2I models. We first derive Amber-Image-10B using a timestep-sensitive depth pruning strategy, where retained layers are reinitialized via local weight averaging and optimized through layer-wise distillation and full-parameter fine-tuning. Building on this, we develop Amber-Image-6B by introducing a hybrid-stream architecture that converts deep-layer dual streams into a single stream initialized from the image branch, further refined via progressive distillation and lightweight fine-tuning. Our approach reduces parameters by 70% and eliminates the need for large-scale data engineering. Notably, the entire compression and training pipeline-from the 10B to the 6B variant-requires fewer than 2,000 GPU hours, demonstrating exceptional cost-efficiency compared to training from scratch. Extensive evaluations on benchmarks like DPG-Bench and LongText-Bench show that Amber-Image achieves high-fidelity synthesis and superior text rendering, matching much larger models.

AI.tldr: Efficiently compresses 60-layer dual-stream MMDiT-based Qwen-Image into Amber-Image 10B and 6B, achieving ~70% parameter reduction with under 2k GPU hours; preserves high fidelity and text rendering on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational cost and deployment barriers of diffusion-transformer-based text-to-image models.
AI.method: Timestep-sensitive depth pruning with local weight averaging reinitialization, followed by layer-wise distillation and full-parameter fine-tuning; introduces a hybrid-stream 6B variant by converting dual streams to a single stream initialized from the image branch, refined via progressive distillation and lightweight fine-tuning.
AI.result: Amber-Image-10B and Amber-Image-6B achieve high-fidelity synthesis and superior text rendering, matching larger models while using ~70% fewer parameters and without large-scale data engineering; compression pipeline requires <2000 GPU hours.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a cost-efficient compression pipeline from 10B to 6B that enables deployment of competitive T2I models without training from scratch.

title: Physical Human-Robot Interaction for Grasping in Augmented Reality via Rigid-Soft Robot Synergy
comment: Camera-ready version for RoboSoft 2026. 8 pages, 6 figures
summary: Hybrid rigid-soft robots combine the precision of rigid manipulators with the compliance and adaptability of soft arms, offering a promising approach for versatile grasping in unstructured environments. However, coordinating hybrid robots remains challenging, due to difficulties in modeling, perception, and cross-domain kinematics. In this work, we present a novel augmented reality (AR)-based physical human-robot interaction framework that enables direct teleoperation of a hybrid rigid-soft robot for simple reaching and grasping tasks. Using an AR headset, users can interact with a simulated model of the robotic system integrated into a general-purpose physics engine, which is superimposed on the real system, allowing simulated execution prior to real-world deployment. To ensure consistent behavior between the virtual and physical robots, we introduce a real-to-simulation parameter identification pipeline that leverages the inherent geometric properties of the soft robot, enabling accurate modeling of its static and dynamic behavior as well as the control system's response.

AI.tldr: AR-based teleoperation framework for hybrid rigid-soft robots enabling virtual simulation and real-to-simulation parameter identification for consistent virtual-physical behavior.
AI.motivation: Coordination of hybrid rigid-soft robots is difficult due to modeling, perception, and cross-domain kinematics; AR teleoperation could simplify control in unstructured environments.
AI.method: Use an AR headset to interact with a simulated robotic model embedded in a general-purpose physics engine, superimposed onto the real robot to allow simulated execution before real deployment. Introduce a real-to-simulation parameter identification pipeline that uses the soft robot's geometry to model static/dynamic behavior and the control system's response.
AI.result: A novel AR-based physical human-robot interaction framework enabling direct teleoperation of the hybrid robot; simulated execution precedes real-world deployment; parameter identification yields accurate modeling of static/dynamic behavior and the control loop.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables simple reaching and grasping tasks with consistent virtual-physical behavior, highlighting AR-HRI as a viable pathway for controlling hybrid rigid-soft robots in unstructured environments.

title: Mobile-Agent-v3.5: Multi-platform Fundamental GUI Agents
summary: The paper introduces GUI-Owl-1.5, the latest native GUI agent model that features instruct/thinking variants in multiple sizes (2B/4B/8B/32B/235B) and supports a range of platforms (desktop, mobile, browser, and more) to enable cloud-edge collaboration and real-time interaction. GUI-Owl-1.5 achieves state-of-the-art results on more than 20+ GUI benchmarks on open-source models: (1) on GUI automation tasks, it obtains 56.5 on OSWorld, 71.6 on AndroidWorld, and 48.4 on WebArena; (2) on grounding tasks, it obtains 80.3 on ScreenSpotPro; (3) on tool-calling tasks, it obtains 47.6 on OSWorld-MCP, and 46.8 on MobileWorld; (4) on memory and knowledge tasks, it obtains 75.5 on GUI-Knowledge Bench. GUI-Owl-1.5 incorporates several key innovations: (1) Hybird Data Flywheel: we construct the data pipeline for UI understanding and trajectory generation based on a combination of simulated environments and cloud-based sandbox environments, in order to improve the efficiency and quality of data collection. (2) Unified Enhancement of Agent Capabilities: we use a unified thought-synthesis pipeline to enhance the model's reasoning capabilities, while placing particular emphasis on improving key agent abilities, including Tool/MCP use, memory and multi-agent adaptation; (3) Multi-platform Environment RL Scaling: We propose a new environment RL algorithm, MRPO, to address the challenges of multi-platform conflicts and the low training efficiency of long-horizon tasks. The GUI-Owl-1.5 models are open-sourced, and an online cloud-sandbox demo is available at https://github.com/X-PLUG/MobileAgent.

AI.tldr: GUI-Owl-1.5 is a native GUI agent model with multi-size variants (2B/4B/8B/32B/235B) and multi-platform support, achieving state-of-the-art on 20+ GUI benchmarks; introduces Hybrid Data Flywheel, Unified Enhancement of Agent Capabilities, and MRPO for multi-platform RL; open-sourced with cloud-sandbox demo.
AI.motivation: Need for robust, scalable GUI agents that operate across devices and enable real-time cloud-edge collaboration, with improved data efficiency, reasoning, tool use, memory, and cross-platform long-horizon task handling.
AI.method: Hybrid Data Flywheel: data pipeline combining simulated environments and cloud sandbox environments; Unified thought-synthesis pipeline to enhance reasoning and agent abilities (tool/MCP use, memory, multi-agent adaptation); Multi-platform Environment RL Scaling using MRPO to address cross-platform conflicts and long-horizon task inefficiency.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on 20+ GUI benchmarks: OSWorld 56.5, AndroidWorld 71.6, WebArena 48.4; ScreenSpotPro 80.3; OSWorld-MCP 47.6, MobileWorld 46.8; GUI-Knowledge Bench 75.5. Model is open-sourced and accompanied by a cloud-sandbox online demo at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: GUI-Owl-1.5 advances GUI agent capabilities across platforms through data-efficient training, unified reasoning enhancements, and scalable multi-platform RL, with open-source release and a cloud-sandbox demo enabling reproducibility and practical evaluation.

title: Better Think Thrice: Learning to Reason Causally with Double Counterfactual Consistency
summary: Despite their strong performance on reasoning benchmarks, large language models (LLMs) have proven brittle when presented with counterfactual questions, suggesting weaknesses in their causal reasoning ability. While recent work has demonstrated that labeled counterfactual tasks can be useful benchmarks of LLMs' causal reasoning, producing such data at the scale required to cover the vast potential space of counterfactuals is limited. In this work, we introduce double counterfactual consistency (DCC), a lightweight inference-time method for measuring and guiding the ability of LLMs to reason causally. Without requiring labeled counterfactual data, DCC verifies a model's ability to execute two important elements of causal reasoning: causal intervention and counterfactual prediction. Using DCC, we evaluate the causal reasoning abilities of various leading LLMs across a range of reasoning tasks and interventions. Moreover, we demonstrate the effectiveness of DCC as a training-free test-time rejection sampling criterion and show that it can directly improve performance on reasoning tasks across multiple model families.

AI.tldr: Introduce double counterfactual consistency (DCC) as an inference-time method to assess and guide LLMs' causal reasoning without labeled data; it checks causal intervention and counterfactual prediction, enabling test-time rejection sampling and improving reasoning performance across models.
AI.motivation: Evaluate causal reasoning in LLMs without large labeled counterfactual datasets, addressing brittleness to counterfactual questions and data-scale limitations.
AI.method: DCC operates at inference time to verify two causal-reasoning elements: causal intervention (manipulate variables and observe effects) and counterfactual prediction (predict outcomes under alternative scenarios). It can be used as a rejection-sampling criterion.
AI.result: Applied across several leading LLMs and reasoning tasks; DCC serves as an effective, training-free test-time criterion and can directly improve performance across multiple model families.
AI.conclusion: DCC offers a scalable, data-efficient tool to measure and improve causal reasoning in LLMs, linking evaluation with practical performance without requiring labeled data.

title: StructCore: Structure-Aware Image-Level Scoring for Training-Free Unsupervised Anomaly Detection
summary:
Max pooling is the de facto standard for converting anomaly score maps into image-level decisions in memory-bank-based unsupervised anomaly detection (UAD). However, because it relies on a single extreme response, it discards most information about how anomaly evidence is distributed and structured across the image, often causing normal and anomalous scores to overlap.
  We propose StructCore, a training-free, structure-aware image-level scoring method that goes beyond max pooling. Given an anomaly score map, StructCore computes a low-dimensional structural descriptor phi(S) that captures distributional and spatial characteristics, and refines image-level scoring via a diagonal Mahalanobis calibration estimated from train-good samples, without modifying pixel-level localization.
  StructCore achieves image-level AUROC scores of 99.6% on MVTec AD and 98.4% on VisA, demonstrating robust image-level anomaly detection by exploiting structural signatures missed by max pooling.

AI.tldr: StructCore is a training-free, structure-aware image-level scoring method for unsupervised anomaly detection that uses a low-dimensional structural descriptor phi(S) and diagonal Mahalanobis calibration to improve image-level scoring beyond max pooling.
AI.motivation: Max pooling discards distributional and spatial information in anomaly score maps, causing overlap between normal and anomalous scores and reducing detection effectiveness; a structure-aware, calibration-based approach can exploit spatial patterns without altering pixel-level localization.
AI.method: From an anomaly score map, compute a low-dimensional structural descriptor phi(S) capturing distributional and spatial characteristics; refine image-level score via a diagonal Mahalanobis distance calibrated on train-good samples; no changes to pixel-level localization or training required.
AI.result: Achieves image-level AUROC of 99.6% on MVTec AD and 98.4% on VisA, indicating robust detection by leveraging structural signatures overlooked by max pooling.
AI.conclusion: StructCore enables robust, structure-aware image-level anomaly detection without retraining or affecting pixel-level localization, outperforming max-pooling baselines by exploiting structural cues in score maps.

title: Geometric Inverse Flight Dynamics on SO(3) and Application to Tethered Fixed-Wing Aircraft
summary: We present a robotics-oriented, coordinate-free formulation of inverse flight dynamics for fixed-wing aircraft on SO(3). Translational force balance is written in the world frame and rotational dynamics in the body frame; aerodynamic directions (drag, lift, side) are defined geometrically, avoiding local attitude coordinates. Enforcing coordinated flight (no sideslip), we derive a closed-form trajectory-to-input map yielding the attitude, angular velocity, and thrust-angle-of-attack pair, and we recover the aerodynamic moment coefficients component-wise. Applying such a map to tethered flight on spherical parallels, we obtain analytic expressions for the required bank angle and identify a specific zero-bank locus where the tether tension exactly balances centrifugal effects, highlighting the decoupling between aerodynamic coordination and the apparent gravity vector. Under a simple lift/drag law, the minimal-thrust angle of attack admits a closed form. These pointwise quasi-steady inversion solutions become steady-flight trim when the trajectory and rotational dynamics are time-invariant. The framework bridges inverse simulation in aeronautics with geometric modeling in robotics, providing a rigorous building block for trajectory design and feasibility checks.

AI.tldr: Coordinate-free geometric inverse flight dynamics on SO(3) with a closed-form trajectory-to-input map, applied to tethered and steady flight, enabling analytic bank/AoA expressions and a lift/drag-based minimal-thrust result.
AI.motivation: Bridge inverse simulation in aeronautics with geometric robotics, enabling rigorous, coordinate-free trajectory design and feasibility checks without relying on local attitude coordinates.
AI.method: World-frame translational dynamics and body-frame rotational dynamics are written with drag/lift/side defined geometrically. Coordinated flight (no sideslip) yields a closed-form map from a state/trajectory to inputs (attitude, angular velocity, thrust-angle-of-attack). Aerodynamic moments are recovered component-wise. Application to tethered flight on spherical parallels derives bank-angle expressions and a zero-bank locus where tether tension balances centrifugal effects; a simple lift/drag law yields a closed-form minimal-thrust angle of attack. Pointwise quasi-steady inversions become steady trim when trajectory and rotation are time-invariant.
AI.result: A closed-form trajectory-to-input inversion, analytic bank-angle expressions, a zero-bank locus, and a minimal-thrust AoA under a simple lift/drag law. The approach provides explicit, coordinate-free building blocks for trajectory design and feasibility checks in UAV robotics contexts.
AI.conclusion: The framework unifies inverse aerodynamics with geometric modeling, offering a rigorous, coordinate-free tool for trajectory planning and feasibility assessment, and clarifying the separation between aerodynamic coordination and the apparent gravity vector.

title: OpenSage: Self-programming Agent Generation Engine
summary: Agent development kits (ADKs) provide effective platforms and tooling for constructing agents, and their designs are critical to the constructed agents' performance, especially the functionality for agent topology, tools, and memory. However, current ADKs either lack sufficient functional support or rely on humans to manually design these components, limiting agents' generalizability and overall performance. We propose OpenSage, the first ADK that enables LLMs to automatically create agents with self-generated topology and toolsets while providing comprehensive and structured memory support. OpenSage offers effective functionality for agents to create and manage their own sub-agents and toolkits. It also features a hierarchical, graph-based memory system for efficient management and a specialized toolkit tailored to software engineering tasks. Extensive experiments across three state-of-the-art benchmarks with various backbone models demonstrate the advantages of OpenSage over existing ADKs. We also conduct rigorous ablation studies to demonstrate the effectiveness of our design for each component. We believe OpenSage can pave the way for the next generation of agent development, shifting the focus from human-centered to AI-centered paradigms.

AI.tldr: OpenSage is the first ADK that enables LLMs to automatically generate agent topology and toolsets with structured memory, including a hierarchical graph-based memory and a software-engineering toolkit, yielding superior performance versus existing ADKs across several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current agent development kits rely on human-designed topology, tools, and memory, which limits generalizability, scalability, and performance; a fully AI-centered approach that allows LLMs to self-generate agents could advance the field.
AI.method: Propose OpenSage: an ADK where LLMs autonomously create agents with self-generated topology and toolsets, manage sub-agents and toolkits, and leverage a hierarchical graph-based memory system; includes a specialized toolkit for software engineering tasks; evaluated on three state-of-the-art benchmarks with multiple backbone models and through ablation studies.
AI.result: OpenSage demonstrates advantages over existing ADKs across benchmarks and backbones; ablations validate the contribution of each component, indicating improved agent performance, organization, and memory management.
AI.conclusion: OpenSage advances AI-centered agent development by enabling fully autonomous agent construction within an ADK, setting a path for broader, more capable, and generalizable AI agents; further work could explore broader toolkits, robustness, and scaling.

title: Escaping the Cognitive Well: Efficient Competition Math with Off-the-Shelf Models
summary: In the past year, custom and unreleased math reasoning models reached gold medal performance on the International Mathematical Olympiad (IMO). Similar performance was then reported using large-scale inference on publicly available models but at prohibitive costs (e.g., 3000 USD per problem). In this work, we present an inference pipeline that attains best-in-class performance on IMO-style math problems at an average inference cost orders of magnitude below competing methods while using only general-purpose off-the-shelf models. Our method relies on insights about grader failure in solver-grader pipelines, which we call the Cognitive Well (iterative refinement converging to a wrong solution that the solver as well as the pipeline's internal grader consider to be basically correct). Our pipeline addresses these failure modes through conjecture extraction, wherein candidate lemmas are isolated from generated solutions and independently verified alongside their negations in a fresh environment (context detachment). On IMO-ProofBench Advanced (PB-Adv), our pipeline achieves 67.1 percent performance using Gemini 3.0 Pro with an average cost per question of approximately 31 USD. At the time of evaluation, this represented the state-of-the-art on PB-Adv among both public and unreleased models, and more than doubles the success rate of the next best publicly accessible pipeline, all at a fraction of the cost.

AI.tldr: A cost-efficient inference pipeline using conjecture extraction and context detachment achieves state-of-the-art performance on IMO-style problems with off-the-shelf models at a fraction of the cost.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational and monetary barriers for solving IMO-style math problems and address solver-grader failure modes (Cognitive Well) in solver-grader pipelines.
AI.method: Iterative refinement with conjecture extraction: isolate candidate lemmas from generated solutions and independently verify them and their negations in a fresh environment (context detachment). The approach uses general-purpose off-the-shelf models to perform reasoning.
AI.result: On IMO-ProofBench Advanced, the pipeline achieves 67.1% performance using Gemini 3.0 Pro with an average cost of about $31 per question, representing state-of-the-art among public and unreleased models and more than doubling the success rate of the next best publicly accessible pipeline.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that cost-effective math reasoning is achievable with lemma-based verification and context detachment, enabling strong performance with standard models and broad accessibility.

title: Cholec80-port: A Geometrically Consistent Trocar Port Segmentation Dataset for Robust Surgical Scene Understanding
summary: Trocar ports are camera-fixed, pseudo-static structures that can persistently occlude laparoscopic views and attract disproportionate feature points due to specular, textured surfaces. This makes ports particularly detrimental to geometry-based downstream pipelines such as image stitching, 3D reconstruction, and visual SLAM, where dynamic or non-anatomical outliers degrade alignment and tracking stability. Despite this practical importance, explicit port labels are rare in public surgical datasets, and existing annotations often violate geometric consistency by masking the central lumen (opening), even when anatomical regions are visible through it. We present Cholec80-port, a high-fidelity trocar port segmentation dataset derived from Cholec80, together with a rigorous standard operating procedure (SOP) that defines a port-sleeve mask excluding the central opening. We additionally cleanse and unify existing public datasets under the same SOP. Experiments demonstrate that geometrically consistent annotations substantially improve cross-dataset robustness beyond what dataset size alone provides.

AI.tldr: Introduces Cholec80-port: a high-fidelity trocar port segmentation dataset with an SOP that excludes the central lumen, plus unified annotations across public datasets; demonstrates improved cross-dataset robustness for vision pipelines.
AI.motivation: Trocar ports occlude laparoscopic views and attract disproportionate feature points due to specular/textured surfaces, harming geometry-based pipelines (e.g., stitching, 3D reconstruction, SLAM). Public datasets lack consistent port labels and often mask the central lumen, breaking geometric consistency.
AI.method: Derive Cholec80-port from the Cholec80 dataset and establish a rigorous SOP that defines a port-sleeve mask excluding the central opening. Cleanse and unify existing public datasets under the same SOP, providing consistent, geometry-preserving annotations.
AI.result: Geometrically consistent annotations yield improved cross-dataset robustness beyond what increasing dataset size alone achieves.
AI.conclusion: Geometric consistency in annotations is crucial for robust cross-dataset performance in port-aware surgical vision; the SOP-based unification and the resulting Cholec80-port dataset offer a practical benchmark for downstream geometry-based pipelines.

title: Nonlinear Predictive Control of the Continuum and Hybrid Dynamics of a Suspended Deformable Cable for Aerial Pick and Place
comment: Accepted to the IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA) 2026
summary: This paper presents a framework for aerial manipulation of an extensible cable that combines a high-fidelity model based on partial differential equations (PDEs) with a reduced-order representation suitable for real-time control. The PDEs are discretised using a finite-difference method, and proper orthogonal decomposition is employed to extract a reduced-order model (ROM) that retains the dominant deformation modes while significantly reducing computational complexity. Based on this ROM, a nonlinear model predictive control scheme is formulated, capable of stabilizing cable oscillations and handling hybrid transitions such as payload attachment and detachment. Simulation results confirm the stability, efficiency, and robustness of the ROM, as well as the effectiveness of the controller in regulating cable dynamics under a range of operating conditions. Additional simulations illustrate the application of the ROM for trajectory planning in constrained environments, demonstrating the versatility of the proposed approach. Overall, the framework enables real-time, dynamics-aware control of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) carrying suspended flexible cables.

AI.tldr: A UAV with a suspended extensible cable is controlled via a PDE-based cable model reduced by POD into a ROM for real-time control; nonlinear MPC stabilizes the cable, enabling payload attachment/detachment and trajectory planning in constrained environments.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of real-time, dynamics-aware control of flexible cables in aerial manipulation by marrying high-fidelity dynamics with a real-time capable reduced-order model, enabling stable payload handling and planning.
AI.method: Develop a PDE-based cable model discretized by finite differences; apply proper orthogonal decomposition (POD) to derive a reduced-order model retaining dominant deformation modes; formulate a nonlinear model predictive control (MPC) scheme using the ROM to stabilize oscillations and manage hybrid transitions; validate via simulations.
AI.result: ROM preserves the dominant deformation dynamics while significantly reducing computation; nonlinear MPC stabilizes cable oscillations and handles payload attachment/detachment; simulations demonstrate stability, efficiency, robustness, and applicability to trajectory planning in constrained environments.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables real-time, dynamics-aware UAV control with suspended flexible cables, combining high-fidelity modeling with ROM-based control and planning for versatile aerial manipulation.

title: AgentLAB: Benchmarking LLM Agents against Long-Horizon Attacks
summary: LLM agents are increasingly deployed in long-horizon, complex environments to solve challenging problems, but this expansion exposes them to long-horizon attacks that exploit multi-turn user-agent-environment interactions to achieve objectives infeasible in single-turn settings. To measure agent vulnerabilities to such risks, we present AgentLAB, the first benchmark dedicated to evaluating LLM agent susceptibility to adaptive, long-horizon attacks. Currently, AgentLAB supports five novel attack types including intent hijacking, tool chaining, task injection, objective drifting, and memory poisoning, spanning 28 realistic agentic environments, and 644 security test cases. Leveraging AgentLAB, we evaluate representative LLM agents and find that they remain highly susceptible to long-horizon attacks; moreover, defenses designed for single-turn interactions fail to reliably mitigate long-horizon threats. We anticipate that AgentLAB will serve as a valuable benchmark for tracking progress on securing LLM agents in practical settings. The benchmark is publicly available at https://tanqiujiang.github.io/AgentLAB_main.

AI.tldr: AgentLAB is a new benchmark to evaluate LLM agents against adaptive, long-horizon attacks, revealing high vulnerability to such threats and showing single-turn defenses are insufficient; it covers five attack types, 28 environments, and 644 test cases, with public availability.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of benchmarks for assessing LLM agents in long-horizon, adaptive, multi-turn settings where attacker and environment evolve over time.
AI.method: Introduce AgentLAB with five attack types (intent hijacking, tool chaining, task injection, objective drifting, memory poisoning), spanning 28 realistic agentic environments and 644 security test cases; evaluate representative LLM agents; analyze defense effectiveness against long-horizon threats; make benchmark publicly available.
AI.result: LLM agents remain highly susceptible to long-horizon attacks; single-turn defenses fail to reliably mitigate long-horizon threats; AgentLAB serves as a baseline for tracking progress in securing LLM agents.
AI.conclusion: AgentLAB provides a valuable benchmark for evaluating and advancing the security of LLM agents in practical, long-horizon settings and enables reproducible progress tracking.

title: Efficient Tail-Aware Generative Optimization via Flow Model Fine-Tuning
summary: Fine-tuning pre-trained diffusion and flow models to optimize downstream utilities is central to real-world deployment. Existing entropy-regularized methods primarily maximize expected reward, providing no mechanism to shape tail behavior. However, tail control is often essential: the lower tail determines reliability by limiting low-reward failures, while the upper tail enables discovery by prioritizing rare, high-reward outcomes. In this work, we present Tail-aware Flow Fine-Tuning (TFFT), a principled and efficient distributional fine-tuning algorithm based on the Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR). We address two distinct tail-shaping goals: right-CVaR for seeking novel samples in the high-reward tail and left-CVaR for controlling worst-case samples in the low-reward tail. Unlike prior approaches that rely on non-linear optimization, we leverage the variational dual formulation of CVaR to decompose it into a decoupled two-stage procedure: a lightweight one-dimensional threshold optimization step, and a single entropy-regularized fine-tuning process via a specific pseudo-reward. This decomposition achieves CVaR fine-tuning efficiently with computational cost comparable to standard expected fine-tuning methods. We demonstrate the effectiveness of TFFT across illustrative experiments, high-dimensional text-to-image generation, and molecular design.

AI.tldr: Tail-aware Flow Fine-Tuning (TFFT) uses CVaR to shape distribution tails during fine-tuning of diffusion/flow models, via a two-stage approach (threshold optimization + entropy-regularized fine-tuning) enabling both high-reward discovery (right-CVaR) and worst-case reliability (left-CVaR) with computational cost comparable to standard methods.
AI.motivation: Existing entropy-regularized fine-tuning focuses on expected reward and neglects tail behavior, which is critical for reliability (lower tail) and discovery (upper tail) in real-world deployment.
AI.method: Leverage the variational dual form of CVaR to decompose optimization into a one-dimensional threshold search and a single entropy-regularized fine-tuning step using a carefully designed pseudo-reward. Apply both right-CVaR and left-CVaR to target high-reward and low-reward tails, respectively, with cost comparable to standard fine-tuning.
AI.result: Empirical demonstrations across illustrative experiments, high-dimensional text-to-image generation, and molecular design show effective tail control without incurring extra computational burden relative to standard expected fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: TFFT offers a principled, efficient framework for distributional fine-tuning with tail control in diffusion/flow models, enabling targeted improvements in reliability and discovery without higher training costs.

title: Cross Pseudo Labeling For Weakly Supervised Video Anomaly Detection
summary: Weakly supervised video anomaly detection aims to detect anomalies and identify abnormal categories with only video-level labels. We propose CPL-VAD, a dual-branch framework with cross pseudo labeling. The binary anomaly detection branch focuses on snippet-level anomaly localization, while the category classification branch leverages vision-language alignment to recognize abnormal event categories. By exchanging pseudo labels, the two branches transfer complementary strengths, combining temporal precision with semantic discrimination. Experiments on XD-Violence and UCF-Crime demonstrate that CPL-VAD achieves state-of-the-art performance in both anomaly detection and abnormal category classification.

AI.tldr: Proposes CPL-VAD, a dual-branch weakly supervised video anomaly detection framework with cross pseudo labeling that merges snippet-level localization and vision-language category recognition, achieving state-of-the-art performance on XD-Violence and UCF-Crime.
AI.motivation: Tackle weakly supervised video anomaly detection by enabling both precise temporal localization and semantic abnormal-category discrimination under only video-level labels, via cross-pseudo-label transfers.
AI.method: A dual-branch architecture: (1) binary anomaly detection branch performs snippet-level localization; (2) category classification branch uses vision-language alignment to recognize abnormal event categories. The branches exchange pseudo labels, enabling cross-branch knowledge transfer and combining temporal precision with semantic discrimination.
AI.result: Empirical results on XD-Violence and UCF-Crime show state-of-the-art performance in both anomaly detection and abnormal category classification.
AI.conclusion: Cross pseudo labeling between a temporal localization branch and a semantic category branch improves weakly supervised VAD by jointly optimizing temporal precision and category semantics, validating CPL-VAD's effectiveness.

title: Multi-session Localization and Mapping Exploiting Topological Information
