AI.method: Identify Entropy Collapse Layer (ECL) via matrix entropy where information content drops sharply. Propose EntropyPrune to quantify information value of individual tokens and prune redundancies without relying on attention maps. Improve efficiency by exploiting spectral equivalence of dual Gram matrices to speed up entropy computation, achieving up to 64x theoretical speedup.
AI.result: EntroyPrune yields up to 68.2% FLOPs reduction on LLaVA-1.5-7B while preserving 96.0% of original accuracy. Demonstrates strong performance across high-resolution and video-based models, outperforming state-of-the-art pruning methods in accuracy and efficiency. Code to be released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: A principled, transferable token-pruning framework for MLLMs that achieves substantial efficiency gains with minimal accuracy loss, supported by a robust entropy-based criterion and scalable entropy computations.

title: Proximal powered knee placement: a case study
comment: Submitted to IEEE RAS/EMBS 11th International Conference on Biomedical Robotics and Biomechatronics (BioRob 2026)
summary: Lower limb amputation affects millions worldwide, leading to impaired mobility, reduced walking speed, and limited participation in daily and social activities. Powered prosthetic knees can partially restore mobility by actively assisting knee joint torque, improving gait symmetry, sit-to-stand transitions, and walking speed. However, added mass from powered components may diminish these benefits, negatively affecting gait mechanics and increasing metabolic cost. Consequently, optimizing mass distribution, rather than simply minimizing total mass, may provide a more effective and practical solution. In this exploratory study, we evaluated the feasibility of above-knee powertrain placement for a powered prosthetic knee in a small cohort. Compared to below-knee placement, the above-knee configuration demonstrated improved walking speed (+9.2% for one participant) and cadence (+3.6%), with mixed effects on gait symmetry. Kinematic measures indicated similar knee range of motion and peak velocity across configurations. Additional testing on ramps and stairs confirmed the robustness of the control strategy across multiple locomotion tasks. These preliminary findings suggest that above-knee placement is functionally feasible and that careful mass distribution can preserve the benefits of powered assistance while mitigating adverse effects of added weight. Further studies are needed to confirm these trends and guide design and clinical recommendations.

AI.tldr: Above-knee powertrain placement for a powered knee is feasible and can preserve benefits of powered assistance, showing improved gait performance in a small exploratory study, with similar knee kinematics to below-knee placement; requires larger studies for confirmation.
AI.motivation: Powered knee prostheses can restore mobility but added mass may negate benefits; optimizing mass distribution (placement) may enhance performance without incurring higher metabolic cost.
AI.method: Exploratory, within-subject comparison in a small cohort comparing above-knee vs below-knee placement of the powertrain. Measured walking speed, cadence, gait symmetry, knee ROM, and peak knee velocity. Also tested ramps and stairs to assess control robustness.
AI.result: Above-knee placement yielded higher walking speed (+9.2% in one participant) and cadence (+3.6%), with mixed effects on gait symmetry. Knee ROM and peak velocity were similar across configurations. Ramp and stairs testing supported robustness of the control strategy.
AI.conclusion: Above-knee placement is functionally feasible. Careful mass distribution can preserve benefits of powered assistance while reducing adverse effects of added weight. Larger studies are needed to confirm trends and inform design and clinical practice.

title: HQFS: Hybrid Quantum Classical Financial Security with VQC Forecasting, QUBO Annealing, and Audit-Ready Post-Quantum Signing
summary:
Here's the corrected paragraph with all punctuation and formatting issues fixed:
  Financial risk systems usually follow a two-step routine: a model predicts return or risk, and then an optimizer makes a decision such as a portfolio rebalance. In practice, this split can break under real constraints. The prediction model may look good, but the final decision can be unstable when the market shifts, when discrete constraints are added (lot sizes, caps), or when the optimization becomes slow for larger asset sets. Also, regulated settings need a clear audit trail that links each decision to the exact model state and inputs. We present HQFS, a practical hybrid pipeline that connects forecasting, discrete risk optimization, and auditability in one flow. First, HQFS learns next-step return and a volatility proxy using a variational quantum circuit (VQC) with a small classical head. Second, HQFS converts the risk-return objective and constraints into a QUBO and solves it with quantum annealing when available, while keeping a compatible classical QUBO solver as a fallback for deployment. Third, HQFS signs each rebalance output using a post-quantum signature so the allocation can be verified later without trusting the runtime environment. On our market dataset study, HQFS reduces return prediction error by 7.8% and volatility prediction error by 6.1% versus a tuned classical baseline. For the decision layer, HQFS improves out-of-sample Sharpe by 9.4% and lowers maximum drawdown by 11.7%. The QUBO solve stage also cuts average solve time by 28% compared to a mixed-integer baseline under the same constraints, while producing fully traceable, signed allocation records.

AI.tldr: HQFS is a hybrid quantum-classical pipeline that links forecasting, discrete risk optimization, and auditability to improve predictive accuracy, optimization performance, and traceable decision records in financial risk management.
AI.motivation: Bridge the forecasting-to-optimization gap under real-world constraints (discrete asset Rules, slow solvers) while ensuring regulatory auditability and verifiability of decisions.
AI.method: 1) Forecast: next-step return and volatility proxy via a variational quantum circuit with a small classical head. 2) Optimization: convert risk-return objectives and constraints to a QUBO and solve via quantum annealing when available; provide a classical QUBO solver fallback. 3) Auditability: sign each rebalance output with a post-quantum signature for verifiable allocations regardless of runtime environment.
AI.result: Market data study shows: return prediction error reduced by 7.8%, volatility error reduced by 6.1%. Decision layer improves out-of-sample Sharpe by 9.4% and lowers max drawdown by 11.7%. QUBO solve time reduced by 28% versus a tuned mixed-integer baseline; allocations are fully traceable and signed.
AI.conclusion: HQFS demonstrates a practical, auditable, hybrid quantum-classical workflow that improves predictive accuracy, decision quality, and computational efficiency while maintaining deterministic traceability for regulated deployments.

title: What is the Value of Censored Data? An Exact Analysis for the Data-driven Newsvendor
summary: We study the offline data-driven newsvendor problem with censored demand data. In contrast to prior works where demand is fully observed, we consider the setting where demand is censored at the inventory level and only sales are observed; sales match demand when there is sufficient inventory, and equal the available inventory otherwise. We provide a general procedure to compute the exact worst-case regret of classical data-driven inventory policies, evaluated over all demand distributions. Our main technical result shows that this infinite-dimensional, non-convex optimization problem can be reduced to a finite-dimensional one, enabling an exact characterization of the performance of policies for any sample size and censoring levels. We leverage this reduction to derive sharp insights on the achievable performance of standard inventory policies under demand censoring. In particular, our analysis of the Kaplan-Meier policy shows that while demand censoring fundamentally limits what can be learned from passive sales data, just a small amount of targeted exploration at high inventory levels can substantially improve worst-case guarantees, enabling near-optimal performance even under heavy censoring. In contrast, when the point-of-sale system does not record stockout events and only reports realized sales, a natural and commonly used approach is to treat sales as demand. Our results show that policies based on this sales-as-demand heuristic can suffer severe performance degradation as censored data accumulates, highlighting how the quality of point-of-sale information critically shapes what can, and cannot, be learned offline.

AI.tldr: Exact finite-dimensional reduction enables exact worst-case regret analysis for censored-demand offline newsvendor; censoring limits learning from passive sales data, but targeted exploration at high inventory improves guarantees; sales-as-demand heuristics degrade with censoring, highlighting the importance of POS data quality.
AI.motivation: Understand how censored demand data (observed sales vs. true demand) affects offline data-driven inventory policies, and quantify the best possible guarantees across all demand distributions.
AI.method: Derive a finite-dimensional reformulation of an infinite-dimensional, non-convex worst-case regret problem. Analyze the Kaplan-Meier policy and compare it to policies based on treating sales as demand, using exploration strategies at high inventory levels to mitigate censoring.
AI.result: Exact characterization of policy performance for any sample size and censoring level. Demonstrates that censoring fundamentally limits learning from passive sales data, but even limited targeted exploration can yield near-optimal performance under heavy censoring. Shows that sales-as-demand heuristics can perform severely poorly as censoring grows.
AI.conclusion: POS data quality critically shapes what can be learned offline; a small amount of targeted exploration at high inventory can substantially improve worst-case guarantees under censoring.

title: GASS: Geometry-Aware Spherical Sampling for Disentangled Diversity Enhancement in Text-to-Image Generation
comment: Preprint. Code will be available at https://github.com/L-YeZhu/GASS_T2I
summary: Despite high semantic alignment, modern text-to-image (T2I) generative models still struggle to synthesize diverse images from a given prompt. This lack of diversity not only restricts user choice, but also risks amplifying societal biases. In this work, we enhance the T2I diversity through a geometric lens. Unlike most existing methods that rely primarily on entropy-based guidance to increase sample dissimilarity, we introduce Geometry-Aware Spherical Sampling (GASS) to enhance diversity by explicitly controlling both prompt-dependent and prompt-independent sources of variation. Specifically, we decompose the diversity measure in CLIP embeddings using two orthogonal directions: the text embedding, which captures semantic variation related to the prompt, and an identified orthogonal direction that captures prompt-independent variation (e.g., backgrounds). Based on this decomposition, GASS increases the geometric projection spread of generated image embeddings along both axes and guides the T2I sampling process via expanded predictions along the generation trajectory. Our experiments on different frozen T2I backbones (U-Net and DiT, diffusion and flow) and benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of disentangled diversity enhancement with minimal impact on image fidelity and semantic alignment.

AI.tldr: GASS enhances T2I diversity by geometrically expanding CLIP embedding projections along two orthogonal axes—prompt-dependent text and prompt-independent background—leading to richer outputs with minimal fidelity/semantic loss across backbones (U-Net, DiT) and diffusion/flow-based models.
AI.motivation: Despite strong semantic alignment, modern T2I models struggle to produce diverse images for a given prompt, risking limited user choice and potential bias amplification. Entropy-based methods alone fail to disentangle prompt-related variation from prompt-independent factors, hindering controlled diversity.
AI.method: Propose Geometry-Aware Spherical Sampling (GASS). Decompose diversity in CLIP embeddings into two orthogonal directions: (1) the text embedding capturing prompt-related semantic variation, and (2) an orthogonal direction capturing prompt-independent variation (e.g., backgrounds). Increase geometric projection spread along both axes and guide sampling via expanded predictions along the generation trajectory.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across frozen T2I backbones (U-Net and DiT; diffusion and flow) and benchmarks shows that disentangled diversity enhancement is effective, achieving higher diversity while maintaining image fidelity and semantic alignment. The method works across multiple architectures without significant degradation of quality.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-aware, orthogonal decomposition enables effective, controlled diversification in text-to-image generation, compatible with various backbones and generation paradigms, with minimal impact on fidelity and semantic alignment.

title: RA-Nav: A Risk-Aware Navigation System Based on Semantic Segmentation for Aerial Robots in Unpredictable Environments
summary: Existing aerial robot navigation systems typically plan paths around static and dynamic obstacles, but fail to adapt when a static obstacle suddenly moves. Integrating environmental semantic awareness enables estimation of potential risks posed by suddenly moving obstacles. In this paper, we propose RA- Nav, a risk-aware navigation framework based on semantic segmentation. A lightweight multi-scale semantic segmentation network identifies obstacle categories in real time. These obstacles are further classified into three types: stationary, temporarily static, and dynamic. For each type, corresponding risk estimation functions are designed to enable real-time risk prediction, based on which a complete local risk map is constructed. Based on this map, the risk-informed path search algorithm is designed to guarantee planning that balances path efficiency and safety. Trajectory optimization is then applied to generate trajectories that are safe, smooth, and dynamically feasible. Comparative simulations demonstrate that RA-Nav achieves higher success rates than baselines in sudden obstacle state transition scenarios. Its effectiveness is further validated in simulations using real- world data.

AI.tldr: RA-Nav: a risk-aware, semantic-segmentation-based navigation framework for aerial robots that anticipates sudden obstacle state changes by classifying obstacles into stationary, temporarily static, and dynamic, constructing a local risk map, and performing risk-informed planning plus trajectory optimization, outperforming baselines in simulations.
AI.motivation: To address failures of traditional path planning when static obstacles suddenly move; to incorporate environmental semantics for real-time risk estimation and robust navigation.
AI.method: 1) A lightweight multi-scale semantic segmentation network for real-time obstacle categorization; 2) classify obstacles into stationary, temporarily static, dynamic; 3) design type-specific risk estimation functions; 4) build a local risk map; 5) develop a risk-informed path search algorithm; 6) apply trajectory optimization for safe, smooth, dynamically feasible trajectories.
AI.result: Simulations show higher success rates vs baselines in sudden obstacle state transitions; validated with real-world data simulations.
AI.conclusion: RA-Nav demonstrates effective risk-aware navigation balancing safety and efficiency with real-time performance, offering improved resilience to sudden obstacle behavior changes.

title: Fundamental Limits of Black-Box Safety Evaluation: Information-Theoretic and Computational Barriers from Latent Context Conditioning
summary: Black-box safety evaluation of AI systems assumes model behavior on test distributions reliably predicts deployment performance. We formalize and challenge this assumption through latent context-conditioned policies -- models whose outputs depend on unobserved internal variables that are rare under evaluation but prevalent under deployment. We establish fundamental limits showing that no black-box evaluator can reliably estimate deployment risk for such models. (1) Passive evaluation: For evaluators sampling i.i.d. from D_eval, we prove minimax lower bounds via Le Cam's method: any estimator incurs expected absolute error >= (5/24)*delta*L approximately 0.208*delta*L, where delta is trigger probability under deployment and L is the loss gap. (2) Adaptive evaluation: Using a hash-based trigger construction and Yao's minimax principle, worst-case error remains >= delta*L/16 even for fully adaptive querying when D_dep is supported over a sufficiently large domain; detection requires Theta(1/epsilon) queries. (3) Computational separation: Under trapdoor one-way function assumptions, deployment environments possessing privileged information can activate unsafe behaviors that any polynomial-time evaluator without the trapdoor cannot distinguish. For white-box probing, estimating deployment risk to accuracy epsilon_R requires O(1/(gamma^2 * epsilon_R^2)) samples, where gamma = alpha_0 + alpha_1 - 1 measures probe quality, and we provide explicit bias correction under probe error. Our results quantify when black-box testing is statistically underdetermined and provide explicit criteria for when additional safeguards -- architectural constraints, training-time guarantees, interpretability, and deployment monitoring -- are mathematically necessary for worst-case safety assurance.

AI.tldr: Black-box safety testing cannot reliably estimate deployment risk for latent context-conditioned AI policies; fundamental limits require safeguards beyond testing in many settings.
AI.motivation: Deployment risk under distribution shifts and unseen latent contexts can be triggered by rare internal variables; formal limits justify why black-box evaluation may fail to guarantee safety and motivate structural safeguards.
AI.method: (1) Passive evaluation: Le Cam minimax lower bounds on i.i.d. samples show a non-negligible estimation error; (2) Adaptive evaluation: Hash-based trigger construction with Yao's principle yields a persistent worst-case error and shows detection needs Theta(1/epsilon) queries; (3) Computational separation: Trapdoor one-way function assumptions imply privileged environments reveal unsafe behaviors unidentifiable by polynomial-time evaluators; White-box: sample complexity O(1/(gamma^2 * epsilon_R^2)) with bias correction.
AI.result: Key lower bounds: passive error >= (5/24)*delta*L ≈ 0.208 delta L; adaptive error >= delta*L/16 with domain-size assumptions; detection demands Theta(1/epsilon) queries; computational separation under trapdoors; white-box probing achieves estimation with sample complexity O(1/(gamma^2 epsilon_R^2)) and bias-corrected estimates.
AI.conclusion: Black-box testing is statistically underdetermined in critical regimes; safeguarding strategies (architectural constraints, training-time guarantees, interpretability, deployment monitoring) are mathematically necessary for worst-case safety, and results provide criteria for when perception-based testing suffices versus when structural safeguards must be employed.

title: On the Mechanism and Dynamics of Modular Addition: Fourier Features, Lottery Ticket, and Grokking
summary: We present a comprehensive analysis of how two-layer neural networks learn features to solve the modular addition task. Our work provides a full mechanistic interpretation of the learned model and a theoretical explanation of its training dynamics. While prior work has identified that individual neurons learn single-frequency Fourier features and phase alignment, it does not fully explain how these features combine into a global solution. We bridge this gap by formalizing a diversification condition that emerges during training when overparametrized, consisting of two parts: phase symmetry and frequency diversification. We prove that these properties allow the network to collectively approximate a flawed indicator function on the correct logic for the modular addition task. While individual neurons produce noisy signals, the phase symmetry enables a majority-voting scheme that cancels out noise, allowing the network to robustly identify the correct sum. Furthermore, we explain the emergence of these features under random initialization via a lottery ticket mechanism. Our gradient flow analysis proves that frequencies compete within each neuron, with the "winner" determined by its initial spectral magnitude and phase alignment. From a technical standpoint, we provide a rigorous characterization of the layer-wise phase coupling dynamics and formalize the competitive landscape using the ODE comparison lemma. Finally, we use these insights to demystify grokking, characterizing it as a three-stage process involving memorization followed by two generalization phases, driven by the competition between loss minimization and weight decay.

AI.tldr: Two-layer networks learn modular addition by combining per-neuron Fourier features via phase symmetry and frequency diversification, enabling a majority-voting mechanism that cancels noise; training dynamics are governed by competition among frequencies and lottery-ticket initialization, with grokking explained as memorization followed by two generalization phases.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between single-neuron Fourier feature learning and global solution for modular addition; provide a mechanistic, dynamical understanding of training in overparameterized networks.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis introducing a diversification condition (phase symmetry + frequency diversification) under overparameterization; gradient-flow/ODE analysis showing intra-neuron frequency competition with winner determined by initial spectral magnitude and phase alignment; characterization of layer-wise phase coupling dynamics; formalization of competitive landscape via the ODE comparison lemma; explanation via lottery-ticket mechanism; three-stage grokking model (memorization, first generalization, second generalization) driven by loss minimization vs weight decay.
AI.result: Demonstrates that collectively the network approximates a flawed indicator function for the modular addition task; phase symmetry enables a majority voting scheme to suppress per-neuron noise and recover the correct sum; provides a mechanistic account for the emergence of features and dynamics under random initialization and overparameterization; offers a rigorous description of feature competition and phase coupling that underpins grokking.
AI.conclusion: Provides a rigorous, mechanistic framework for feature learning in two-layer nets on modular addition, clarifying how initialization and competitive dynamics yield global solutions and explain grokking as a three-stage process involving memorization and two generalization phases.

title: HiMAP: History-aware Map-occupancy Prediction with Fallback
comment: Accepted in 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation
summary: Accurate motion forecasting is critical for autonomous driving, yet most predictors rely on multi-object tracking (MOT) with identity association, assuming that objects are correctly and continuously tracked. When tracking fails due to, e.g., occlusion, identity switches, or missed detections, prediction quality degrades and safety risks increase. We present \textbf{HiMAP}, a tracking-free, trajectory prediction framework that remains reliable under MOT failures. HiMAP converts past detections into spatiotemporally invariant historical occupancy maps and introduces a historical query module that conditions on the current agent state to iteratively retrieve agent-specific history from unlabeled occupancy representations. The retrieved history is summarized by a temporal map embedding and, together with the final query and map context, drives a DETR-style decoder to produce multi-modal future trajectories. This design lifts identity reliance, supports streaming inference via reusable encodings, and serves as a robust fallback when tracking is unavailable. On Argoverse~2, HiMAP achieves performance comparable to tracking-based methods while operating without IDs, and it substantially outperforms strong baselines in the no-tracking setting, yielding relative gains of 11\% in FDE, 12\% in ADE, and a 4\% reduction in MR over a fine-tuned QCNet. Beyond aggregate metrics, HiMAP delivers stable forecasts for all agents simultaneously without waiting for tracking to recover, highlighting its practical value for safety-critical autonomy. The code is available under: https://github.com/XuYiMing83/HiMAP.

AI.tldr: HiMAP is a tracking-free trajectory predictor that uses historical occupancy maps and a historical query to recover agent-specific pasts, feeding a DETR-style decoder to generate multi-modal futures, matching tracking-based methods without IDs and excelling in no-tracking scenarios.
AI.motivation: MOT-based predictors rely on continuous identity tracking; occlusions, switches, or missed detections break identity tracking, degrading prediction quality and safety. A tracking-free, streaming-friendly alternative reduces reliance on IDs and provides robust fallbacks when tracking fails.
AI.method: Past detections are converted into spatiotemporally invariant occupancy maps. A historical query conditioned on the current agent state retrieves agent-specific history from unlabeled occupancy representations. The retrieved history is summarized into a temporal map embedding. Together with the final query and map context, a DETR-style decoder predicts multi-modal future trajectories. The system supports streaming inference with reusable encodings and avoids identity dependence.
AI.result: On Argoverse 2, HiMAP achieves performance comparable to tracking-based methods without IDs. In no-tracking settings, it substantially outperforms strong baselines, with relative gains of 11% in FDE, 12% in ADE, and 4% MR over a fine-tuned QCNet. HiMAP provides stable forecasts for all agents simultaneously without waiting for tracking to recover.
AI.conclusion: HiMAP demonstrates that reliable, multi-agent trajectory forecasting is achievable without MOT IDs, offering a practical fallback for safety-critical autonomous systems and a foundation for streaming, identity-agnostic prediction.

title: IRIS: Learning-Driven Task-Specific Cinema Robot Arm for Visuomotor Motion Control
summary: Robotic camera systems enable dynamic, repeatable motion beyond human capabilities, yet their adoption remains limited by the high cost and operational complexity of industrial-grade platforms. We present the Intelligent Robotic Imaging System (IRIS), a task-specific 6-DOF manipulator designed for autonomous, learning-driven cinematic motion control. IRIS integrates a lightweight, fully 3D-printed hardware design with a goal-conditioned visuomotor imitation learning framework based on Action Chunking with Transformers (ACT). The system learns object-aware and perceptually smooth camera trajectories directly from human demonstrations, eliminating the need for explicit geometric programming. The complete platform costs under $1,000 USD, supports a 1.5 kg payload, and achieves approximately 1 mm repeatability. Real-world experiments demonstrate accurate trajectory tracking, reliable autonomous execution, and generalization across diverse cinematic motions.

AI.tldr: A low-cost, 6-DOF robotic camera system (IRIS) uses a 3D-printed design and Action Chunking with Transformers (ACT) to learn goal-conditioned camera trajectories from human demonstrations, enabling autonomous, perceptually smooth cinematography with ~1 mm repeatability and a payload of 1.5 kg at under $1,000.
AI.motivation: To lower the barrier to adopting robotic camera systems by reducing cost and operational complexity, enabling autonomous, learning-driven motion control for cinematic applications.
AI.method: Hardware: a lightweight, fully 3D-printed 6-DOF manipulator. Control: a goal-conditioned visuomotor imitation learning framework based on Action Chunking with Transformers (ACT). The system learns object-aware, perceptually smooth camera trajectories directly from human demonstrations without explicit geometric programming, supporting a payload of 1.5 kg and achieving ~1 mm repeatability.
AI.result: Real-world experiments show accurate trajectory tracking, reliable autonomous execution, and generalization across diverse cinematic motions. The platform cost is under $1,000, with a 1.5 kg payload and ~1 mm repeatability.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates a cost-effective, learning-driven solution for autonomous cinematic camera control, potentially democratizing robotic cinematography by combining accessible hardware with data-driven trajectory learning and avoiding traditional, expert-heavy geometric programming.

title: Conv-FinRe: A Conversational and Longitudinal Benchmark for Utility-Grounded Financial Recommendation
summary: Most recommendation benchmarks evaluate how well a model imitates user behavior. In financial advisory, however, observed actions can be noisy or short-sighted under market volatility and may conflict with a user's long-term goals. Treating what users chose as the sole ground truth, therefore, conflates behavioral imitation with decision quality. We introduce Conv-FinRe, a conversational and longitudinal benchmark for stock recommendation that evaluates LLMs beyond behavior matching. Given an onboarding interview, step-wise market context, and advisory dialogues, models must generate rankings over a fixed investment horizon. Crucially, Conv-FinRe provides multi-view references that distinguish descriptive behavior from normative utility grounded in investor-specific risk preferences, enabling diagnosis of whether an LLM follows rational analysis, mimics user noise, or is driven by market momentum. We build the benchmark from real market data and human decision trajectories, instantiate controlled advisory conversations, and evaluate a suite of state-of-the-art LLMs. Results reveal a persistent tension between rational decision quality and behavioral alignment: models that perform well on utility-based ranking often fail to match user choices, whereas behaviorally aligned models can overfit short-term noise. The dataset is publicly released on Hugging Face, and the codebase is available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: Conv-FinRe is a conversational-longitudinal benchmark for stock recommendation that evaluates LLMs beyond behavior imitation, using multi-view references to separate rational utility from descriptive user behavior, revealing a tension between decision quality and behavioral alignment.
AI.motivation: Traditional recommendation benchmarks favor imitation; in finance, observed actions can be noisy, short-sighted, and misaligned with long-term goals. There is a need to distinguish normative utility from descriptive behavior.
AI.method: Construct Conv-FinRe from real market data and human decision trajectories; use onboarding interviews, step-wise market context, and advisory dialogues to produce rankings over a fixed investment horizon. Provide multi-view references to separate descriptive behavior from investor-specific utility. Instantiate controlled advisory conversations and evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs across the benchmark; dataset released on HuggingFace and code on GitHub.
AI.result: Results show a persistent tension: models with strong utility-based ranking often fail to match user choices, while behaviorally aligned models may overfit short-term noise.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark enables diagnosing whether LLMs follow rational analysis, imitate user noise, or are driven by market momentum; it promotes evaluation beyond behavior matching and provides publicly available resources for replication.

title: Position: Why a Dynamical Systems Perspective is Needed to Advance Time Series Modeling
summary: Time series (TS) modeling has come a long way from early statistical, mainly linear, approaches to the current trend in TS foundation models. With a lot of hype and industrial demand in this field, it is not always clear how much progress there really is. To advance TS forecasting and analysis to the next level, here we argue that the field needs a dynamical systems (DS) perspective. TS of observations from natural or engineered systems almost always originate from some underlying DS, and arguably access to its governing equations would yield theoretically optimal forecasts. This is the promise of DS reconstruction (DSR), a class of ML/AI approaches that aim to infer surrogate models of the underlying DS from data. But models based on DS principles offer other profound advantages: Beyond short-term forecasts, they enable to predict the long-term statistics of an observed system, which in many practical scenarios may be the more relevant quantities. DS theory furthermore provides domain-independent theoretical insight into mechanisms underlying TS generation, and thereby will inform us, e.g., about upper bounds on performance of any TS model, generalization into unseen regimes as in tipping points, or potential control strategies. After reviewing some of the central concepts, methods, measures, and models in DS theory and DSR, we will discuss how insights from this field can advance TS modeling in crucial ways, enabling better forecasting with much lower computational and memory footprints. We conclude with a number of specific suggestions for translating insights from DSR into TS modeling.

AI.tldr: Argues for a dynamical-systems (DS) perspective in time-series modeling, advocating DS reconstruction (DSR) to infer governing equations from data, enabling long-term statistics, theoretical insights, and efficient forecasting; provides review and practical suggestions to translate DS insights into TS modeling.
AI.motivation: Time-series forecasting often relies on data-driven, often linear or non-D S-based models with limited interpretability and theoretical guarantees. Incorporating DS theory and DSR aims to leverage underlying governing equations to improve forecasting, understand mechanisms, estimate long-term statistics, and establish bounds and control strategies.
AI.method: A narrative review and synthesis of dynamical-systems theory and DS reconstruction (DSR). It discusses central DS concepts, methods, measures, and models, and argues for integrating DS insights into TS modeling; outlines how to translate DSR into practical TS forecasting improvements and provides concrete suggestions.
AI.result: The paper outlines central concepts and methods in DS theory and DSR, argues for their advantages in TS forecasting, and offers specific recommendations for translating DS insights into practical TS modeling, including improved long-term statistics and reduced computation/memory requirements.
AI.conclusion: A DS perspective and DSR can advance TS modeling by yielding better forecasts, long-run statistics, domain-independent insights, and potential control strategies, with practical suggestions to integrate these ideas into TS workflows.

title: FR-GESTURE: An RGBD Dataset For Gesture-based Human-Robot Interaction In First Responder Operations
summary: The ever increasing intensity and number of disasters make even more difficult the work of First Responders (FRs). Artificial intelligence and robotics solutions could facilitate their operations, compensating these difficulties. To this end, we propose a dataset for gesture-based UGV control by FRs, introducing a set of 12 commands, drawing inspiration from existing gestures used by FRs and tactical hand signals and refined after incorporating feedback from experienced FRs. Then we proceed with the data collection itself, resulting in 3312 RGBD pairs captured from 2 viewpoints and 7 distances. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first dataset especially intended for gesture-based UGV guidance by FRs. Finally we define evaluation protocols for our RGBD dataset, termed FR-GESTURE, and we perform baseline experiments, which are put forward for improvement. We have made data publicly available to promote future research on the domain: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18131333.

AI.tldr: Dataset FR-GESTURE for gesture-based UGV control by first responders (FRs). 12 commands, 3312 RGBD pairs from 2 viewpoints and 7 distances. First FR-specific gesture dataset for UGV guidance; includes evaluation protocols and baseline experiments. Data publicly available.
AI.motivation: Facilitate disaster response operations by FRs through AI/robotics; fill the gap of FR-specific gesture datasets and control signals for UGVs; incorporate real FR feedback to reflect practical hand signals and tacit gestures.
AI.method: Data collection of 3312 RGBD gesture frames/pairs collected from 2 viewpoints and 7 distances; 12 command gestures derived from FRs’ existing gestures and tactical signals, refined with experienced FR feedback. Definition of evaluation protocols for RGBD FR-GESTURE dataset. Baseline experiments performed to establish reference performance. Data released publicly (FR-GESTURE).
AI.result: Introduction of FR-GESTURE dataset as a first resource targeting FR gesture-based UGV control; number of samples and setup detailed; baselines established; protocol for evaluation provided; data publicly accessible.
AI.conclusion: FR-GESTURE provides a new benchmark enabling research on gesture-based UGV guidance by FRs, supporting faster development of assistive perception-action loops in disaster-response scenarios; invites further improvement and broader validation.

title: Sonar-TS: Search-Then-Verify Natural Language Querying for Time Series Databases
summary: Natural Language Querying for Time Series Databases (NLQ4TSDB) aims to assist non-expert users retrieve meaningful events, intervals, and summaries from massive temporal records. However, existing Text-to-SQL methods are not designed for continuous morphological intents such as shapes or anomalies, while time series models struggle to handle ultra-long histories. To address these challenges, we propose Sonar-TS, a neuro-symbolic framework that tackles NLQ4TSDB via a Search-Then-Verify pipeline. Analogous to active sonar, it utilizes a feature index to ping candidate windows via SQL, followed by generated Python programs to lock on and verify candidates against raw signals. To enable effective evaluation, we introduce NLQTSBench, the first large-scale benchmark designed for NLQ over TSDB-scale histories. Our experiments highlight the unique challenges within this domain and demonstrate that Sonar-TS effectively navigates complex temporal queries where traditional methods fail. This work presents the first systematic study of NLQ4TSDB, offering a general framework and evaluation standard to facilitate future research.

AI.tldr: Neuro-symbolic NLQ over time-series DBs using a Search-Then-Verify pipeline (Sonar-TS) with a feature-index ping of candidate windows via SQL and Python-based verification, plus NLQTSBench benchmark; first systematic NLQ4TSDB study.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch of Text-to-SQL methods for continuous temporal morphologies (shapes, anomalies) and the difficulty of modeling ultra-long histories in TSDBs.
AI.method: Sonar-TS employs a Search-Then-Verify pipeline: a feature index is used to ping candidate windows through SQL queries, then generated Python programs lock on and verify candidates against raw signals. NLQTSBench provides a large-scale benchmark for NLQ over TSDB-scale histories.
AI.result: Experiments show that Sonar-TS can navigate complex temporal queries that traditional methods fail to handle, and establish a baseline and evaluation standard for NLQ4TSDB.
AI.conclusion: Presents the first systematic study of NLQ4TSDB, offering a general framework and evaluation standard to facilitate future NLQ4TSDB research.

title: ML-driven detection and reduction of ballast information in multi-modal datasets
summary: Modern datasets often contain ballast as redundant or low-utility information that increases dimensionality, storage requirements, and computational cost without contributing meaningful analytical value. This study introduces a generalized, multimodal framework for ballast detection and reduction across structured, semi-structured, unstructured, and sparse data types. Using diverse datasets, entropy, mutual information, Lasso, SHAP, PCA, topic modelling, and embedding analysis are applied to identify and eliminate ballast features. A novel Ballast Score is proposed to integrate these signals into a unified, cross-modal pruning strategy. Experimental results demonstrate that significant portions of the feature space as often exceeding 70% in sparse or semi-structured data, can be pruned with minimal or even improved classification performance, along with substantial reductions in training time and memory footprint. The framework reveals distinct ballast typologies (e.g. statistical, semantic, infrastructural), and offers practical guidance for leaner, more efficient machine learning pipelines.

AI.tldr: A cross-modal ballast-detection framework reduces feature space by identifying and pruning ballast features (e.g., irrelevant or low-utility features) across structured, semi-structured, unstructured, and sparse data using a Ballast Score that integrates entropy, mutual information, Lasso, SHAP, PCA, topic modeling, and embeddings. Pruning up to ~70% of features often maintains or improves performance, with significant gains in training time and memory use.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional ballast features inflate storage, computation, and diminish interpretability without improving analytical value. There is a need for a generalized, multi-modal method to identify and prune such features across data types.
AI.method: Proposes a generalized multimodal framework. Applies diverse analyses (entropy, mutual information, Lasso, SHAP, PCA, topic modeling, embeddings) to assess features, combines signals into a unified Ballast Score for cross-modal pruning, and tests on diverse datasets across structured, semi-structured, unstructured, and sparse data. Reports pruning rates, classification performance, training time, and memory usage.
AI.result: Significant portions of the feature space (often exceeding 70% in sparse or semi-structured data) can be pruned with minimal or improved classification performance, along with substantial reductions in training time and memory footprint. The framework identifies ballast typologies (statistical, semantic, infrastructural) and provides practical guidance for leaner ML pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Ballast-aware, cross-modal pruning is effective across data types, enabling leaner, more efficient ML pipelines. The approach yields actionable insights into ballast typologies and offers practical guidelines for real-world deployment.

title: A Multi-modal Detection System for Infrastructure-based Freight Signal Priority
comment: 12 pages, 15 figures. Accepted at ICTD 2026. Final version to appear in ASCE Proceedings
summary: Freight vehicles approaching signalized intersections require reliable detection and motion estimation to support infrastructure-based Freight Signal Priority (FSP). Accurate and timely perception of vehicle type, position, and speed is essential for enabling effective priority control strategies. This paper presents the design, deployment, and evaluation of an infrastructure-based multi-modal freight vehicle detection system integrating LiDAR and camera sensors. A hybrid sensing architecture is adopted, consisting of an intersection-mounted subsystem and a midblock subsystem, connected via wireless communication for synchronized data transmission. The perception pipeline incorporates both clustering-based and deep learning-based detection methods with Kalman filter tracking to achieve stable real-time performance. LiDAR measurements are registered into geodetic reference frames to support lane-level localization and consistent vehicle tracking. Field evaluations demonstrate that the system can reliably monitor freight vehicle movements at high spatio-temporal resolution. The design and deployment provide practical insights for developing infrastructure-based sensing systems to support FSP applications.

AI.tldr: An infrastructure-based, multi-modal sensing system using LiDAR and camera sensors to detect, localize, and track freight vehicles at signalized intersections for Freight Signal Priority (FSP).
AI.motivation: To enable reliable, high-resolution perception of freight vehicle type, position, and speed for effective priority control at intersections, addressing limitations of current perception approaches.
AI.method: Hybrid sensing architecture with intersection-mounted and midblock subsystems connected via wireless communication. Perception pipeline combines clustering-based and deep learning-based detections with Kalman filter tracking. LiDAR data are registered to geodetic frames for lane-level localization, enabling consistent multi-sensor fusion and tracking.
AI.result: Field evaluations demonstrate reliable monitoring of freight vehicle movements with high spatio-temporal resolution, validating the practicality of infrastructure-based sensing for FSP applications.
AI.conclusion: The study provides practical guidance for designing and deploying infrastructure-based sensing systems to support Freight Signal Priority, including sensor fusion, data synchronization, and georeferenced tracking.

title: Hybrid System Planning using a Mixed-Integer ADMM Heuristic and Hybrid Zonotopes
summary: Embedded optimization-based planning for hybrid systems is challenging due to the use of mixed-integer programming, which is computationally intensive and often sensitive to the specific numerical formulation. To address that challenge, this article proposes a framework for motion planning of hybrid systems that pairs hybrid zonotopes - an advanced set representation - with a new alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) mixed-integer programming heuristic. A general treatment of piecewise affine (PWA) system reachability analysis using hybrid zonotopes is presented and extended to formulate optimal planning problems. Sets produced using the proposed identities have lower memory complexity and tighter convex relaxations than equivalent sets produced from preexisting techniques. The proposed ADMM heuristic makes efficient use of the hybrid zonotope structure. For planning problems formulated as hybrid zonotopes, the proposed heuristic achieves improved convergence rates as compared to state-of-the-art mixed-integer programming heuristics. The proposed methods for hybrid system planning on embedded hardware are experimentally applied in a combined behavior and motion planning scenario for autonomous driving.

AI.tldr: Hybrid zonotopes paired with an ADMM-based mixed-integer programming (MIP) heuristic for embedded planning in hybrid systems; claims lower memory, tighter relaxations, faster convergence, and successful autonomous-driving experiments.
AI.motivation: Overcome computational burden and sensitivity of mixed-integer formulations in planning for hybrid systems; enable efficient embedded hardware implementation.
AI.method: Introduce hybrid zonotopes as a set representation; derive identities and a general PWA reachability analysis framework; extend to optimal planning; develop an ADMM-based MIP heuristic leveraging the zonotope structure.
AI.result: Lower memory complexity and tighter convex relaxations relative to existing methods; faster convergence of the ADMM heuristic for planning problems cast as hybrid zonotopes; effective on embedded hardware; validated in a combined behavior and motion planning setup for autonomous driving.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid zonotopes with an ADMM-MIP heuristic provide scalable, efficient planning for hybrid systems on embedded platforms, with improved relaxation quality and convergence and demonstrated applicability to autonomous driving scenarios.

title: Cinder: A fast and fair matchmaking system
summary: A fair and fast matchmaking system is an important component of modern multiplayer online games, directly impacting player retention and satisfaction. However, creating fair matches between lobbies (pre-made teams) of heterogeneous skill levels presents a significant challenge. Matching based simply on average team skill metrics, such as mean or median rating or rank, often results in unbalanced and one-sided games, particularly when skill distributions are wide or skewed. This paper introduces Cinder, a two-stage matchmaking system designed to provide fast and fair matches. Cinder first employs a rapid preliminary filter by comparing the "non-outlier" skill range of lobbies using the Ruzicka similarity index. Lobbies that pass this initial check are then evaluated using a more precise fairness metric. This second stage involves mapping player ranks to a non-linear set of skill buckets, generated from an inverted normal distribution, to provide higher granularity at average skill levels. The fairness of a potential match is then quantified using the Kantorovich distance on the lobbies' sorted bucket indices, producing a "Sanction Score." We demonstrate the system's viability by analyzing the distribution of Sanction Scores from 140 million simulated lobby pairings, providing a robust foundation for fair matchmaking thresholds.

AI.tldr: Two-stage, fast and fair matchmaking (Cinder) using Ruzicka similarity on non-outlier skill ranges, followed by Kantorovich distance on nonlinearly bucketed ranks; validated on 140M simulated lobby pairings.
AI.motivation: Address unfairness from simple mean/median-based matching when lobbies have heterogeneous or skewed skill distributions; need fast pre-filter and precise fairness metric.
AI.method: Stage 1: rapid pre-filter comparing non-outlier skill range via Ruzicka index. Stage 2: map ranks to buckets generated from inverted normal distribution to get finer granularity around average skill, then compute Kantorovich distance between lobbies' sorted bucket indices to obtain Sanction Score.
AI.result: Simulated 140 million lobby-pairings show distribution of Sanction Scores; demonstrates viability and provides basis for fair thresholds.
AI.conclusion: Cinder offers a scalable fairness-aware matchmaking approach, balancing speed and fairness; the methodology can inform thresholding and deployment in real systems.

title: Construction of a classification model for dementia among Brazilian adults aged 50 and over
summary: To build a dementia classification model for middle-aged and elderly Brazilians, implemented in Python, combining variable selection and multivariable analysis, using low-cost variables with modification potential. Observational study with a predictive modeling approach using a cross-sectional design, aimed at estimating the chances of developing dementia, using data from the Brazilian Longitudinal Study of Aging (ELSI-Brazil), involving 9,412 participants. Dementia was determined based on neuropsychological assessment and informant-based cognitive function. Analyses were performed using Random Forest (RF) and multivariable logistic regression to estimate the risk of dementia in the middle-aged and elderly populations of Brazil. The prevalence of dementia was 9.6%. The highest odds of dementia were observed in illiterate individuals (Odds Ratio (OR) = 7.42), individuals aged 90 years or older (OR = 11.00), low weight (OR = 2.11), low handgrip strength (OR = 2.50), self-reported black skin color (OR = 1.47), physical inactivity (OR = 1.61), self-reported hearing loss (OR = 1.65), and presence of depressive symptoms (OR = 1.72). Higher education (OR=0.44), greater life satisfaction (OR=0.72), and being employed (OR=0.78) were protective factors. The RF model outperformed logistic regression, achieving an area under the ROC curve of 0.776, with a sensitivity of 0.708, a specificity of 0.702, an F1-score of 0.311, a G-means of 0.705, and an accuracy of 0.703. Conclusion: The findings reinforce the multidimensional nature of dementia and the importance of accessible factors for identifying vulnerable individuals. Strengthening public policies focused on promoting brain health can contribute significantly to the efficient allocation of resources in primary care and dementia prevention in Brazil

AI.tldr: Cross-sectional Brazilian dementia risk model using RF and logistic regression; AUROC 0.776; 9.6% prevalence; key risk factors include illiteracy, advanced age, low weight, weak grip, hearing loss, depressive symptoms; RF superior.
AI.motivation: Identify accessible and modifiable factors to predict dementia in mid-aged and elderly Brazilians and to inform public health resource allocation in Brazil.
AI.method: Observational cross-sectional study using data from ELSI-Brazil (n=9,412). Variable selection and multivariable analysis with Random Forest and multivariable logistic regression to estimate dementia risk; dementia determined by neuropsychological and informant-based assessment.
AI.result: Dementia prevalence 9.6%. High odds associated with illiteracy (OR 7.42), age ≥90 (OR 11.00), low weight (OR 2.11), low handgrip strength (OR 2.50), self-reported black skin color (OR 1.47), physical inactivity (OR 1.61), hearing loss (OR 1.65), depressive symptoms (OR 1.72); higher education (OR 0.44), life satisfaction (OR 0.72), employment (OR 0.78) as protective. RF AUC 0.776; sensitivity 0.708; specificity 0.702; F1 0.311; G-mean 0.705; accuracy 0.703.
AI.conclusion: Dementia is multidimensional; accessible factors can help identify vulnerable individuals. Public policies promoting brain health could improve primary care and dementia prevention in Brazil.

title: EA-Swin: An Embedding-Agnostic Swin Transformer for AI-Generated Video Detection
summary: Recent advances in foundation video generators such as Sora2, Veo3, and other commercial systems have produced highly realistic synthetic videos, exposing the limitations of existing detection methods that rely on shallow embedding trajectories, image-based adaptation, or computationally heavy MLLMs. We propose EA-Swin, an Embedding-Agnostic Swin Transformer that models spatiotemporal dependencies directly on pretrained video embeddings via a factorized windowed attention design, making it compatible with generic ViT-style patch-based encoders. Alongside the model, we construct the EA-Video dataset, a benchmark dataset comprising 130K videos that integrates newly collected samples with curated existing datasets, covering diverse commercial and open-source generators and including unseen-generator splits for rigorous cross-distribution evaluation. Extensive experiments show that EA-Swin achieves 0.97-0.99 accuracy across major generators, outperforming prior SoTA methods (typically 0.8-0.9) by a margin of 5-20%, while maintaining strong generalization to unseen distributions, establishing a scalable and robust solution for modern AI-generated video detection.

AI.tldr: EA-Swin: a patch-based, embedding-agnostic Swin Transformer for video-embedding detection, coupled with a large EA-Video benchmark for cross-generator robustness.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of detection methods that rely on shallow embedding trajectories, image adaptation, or heavy LLMs, and to provide a scalable, generalizable detector for AI-generated videos across diverse generators and unseen distributions.
AI.method: Introduce EA-Swin, an Embedding-Agnostic Swin Transformer that processes spatiotemporal dependencies directly on pretrained video embeddings using a factorized windowed attention design, making it compatible with generic ViT-style patch encoders. Construct EA-Video, a 130K-video benchmark combining new data with curated existing datasets, including unseen-generator splits for rigorous cross-distribution evaluation.
AI.result: EA-Swin achieves 0.97–0.99 accuracy across major generators, outperforming prior state-of-the-art methods (typical 0.8–0.9) by 5–20%, and shows strong generalization to unseen distributions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Embedding-Agnostic Swin Transformer offers a scalable, robust solution for modern AI-generated video detection, with broad compatibility to ViT-style encoders and strong cross-distribution performance on the EA-Video benchmark.

title: Conditional Flow Matching for Continuous Anomaly Detection in Autonomous Driving on a Manifold-Aware Spectral Space
summary: Safety validation for Level 4 autonomous vehicles (AVs) is currently bottlenecked by the inability to scale the detection of rare, high-risk long-tail scenarios using traditional rule-based heuristics. We present Deep-Flow, an unsupervised framework for safety-critical anomaly detection that utilizes Optimal Transport Conditional Flow Matching (OT-CFM) to characterize the continuous probability density of expert human driving behavior. Unlike standard generative approaches that operate in unstable, high-dimensional coordinate spaces, Deep-Flow constrains the generative process to a low-rank spectral manifold via a Principal Component Analysis (PCA) bottleneck. This ensures kinematic smoothness by design and enables the computation of the exact Jacobian trace for numerically stable, deterministic log-likelihood estimation. To resolve multi-modal ambiguity at complex junctions, we utilize an Early Fusion Transformer encoder with lane-aware goal conditioning, featuring a direct skip-connection to the flow head to maintain intent-integrity throughout the network. We introduce a kinematic complexity weighting scheme that prioritizes high-energy maneuvers (quantified via path tortuosity and jerk) during the simulation-free training process. Evaluated on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset (WOMD), our framework achieves an AUC-ROC of 0.766 against a heuristic golden set of safety-critical events. More significantly, our analysis reveals a fundamental distinction between kinematic danger and semantic non-compliance. Deep-Flow identifies a critical predictability gap by surfacing out-of-distribution behaviors, such as lane-boundary violations and non-normative junction maneuvers, that traditional safety filters overlook. This work provides a mathematically rigorous foundation for defining statistical safety gates, enabling objective, data-driven validation for the safe deployment of autonomous fleets.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised Deep-Flow uses OT-CFM with a PCA bottleneck to model expert driving density for safety anomaly detection, enabling stable log-likelihood estimation and OOD detection; achieves 0.766 AUC-ROC on WOMD, with lane-aware transformer conditioning and kinematic complexity weighting. Provides a data-driven safety gate distinguishing kinematic danger from semantic non-compliance.
AI.motivation: Scale safety validation for Level 4 AVs by capturing rare, high-risk long-tail scenarios beyond rule-based heuristics; standard generative models struggle in high-dimensional spaces and with unstable log-likelihood estimation.
AI.method: OT-CFM to model continuous density of expert driving; constrains generation to low-rank spectral manifold via PCA bottleneck; exact Jacobian trace for deterministic log-likelihood; Early Fusion Transformer encoder with lane-aware goal conditioning and skip-connection; kinematic complexity weighting focusing on high-energy maneuvers; simulation-free training; evaluated on WOMD.
AI.result: AUC-ROC 0.766 against a heuristic safety-critical events set; reveals a predictability gap by surfacing out-of-distribution behaviors (lane-boundary violations, non-normative junction maneuvers) not captured by traditional safety filters.
AI.conclusion: Provides a mathematically rigorous foundation for data-driven safety gates and objective safety validation for autonomous fleets; emphasizes distinction between kinematic danger and semantic non-compliance.

title: M2F: Automated Formalization of Mathematical Literature at Scale
summary: Automated formalization of mathematics enables mechanical verification but remains limited to isolated theorems and short snippets. Scaling to textbooks and research papers is largely unaddressed, as it requires managing cross-file dependencies, resolving imports, and ensuring that entire projects compile end-to-end. We present M2F (Math-to-Formal), the first agentic framework for end-to-end, project-scale autoformalization in Lean. The framework operates in two stages. The statement compilation stage splits the document into atomic blocks, orders them via inferred dependencies, and repairs declaration skeletons until the project compiles, allowing placeholders in proofs. The proof repair stage closes these holes under fixed signatures using goal-conditioned local edits. Throughout both stages, M2F keeps the verifier in the loop, committing edits only when toolchain feedback confirms improvement. In approximately three weeks, M2F converts long-form mathematical sources into a project-scale Lean library of 153,853 lines from 479 pages textbooks on real analysis and convex analysis, fully formalized as Lean declarations with accompanying proofs. This represents textbook-scale formalization at a pace that would typically require months or years of expert effort. On FATE-H, we achieve $96\%$ proof success (vs.\ $80\%$ for a strong baseline). Together, these results demonstrate that practical, large-scale automated formalization of mathematical literature is within reach. The full generated Lean code from our runs is available at https://github.com/optsuite/ReasBook.git.

AI.tldr: M2F (Math-to-Formal) enables end-to-end, project-scale autoformalization in Lean via a two-stage pipeline: statement compilation and proof repair, with a verifier loop. It achieves textbook-scale formalization (~153,853 Lean lines from 479 pages) in ~3 weeks and 96% proof success on FATE-H (vs. 80% baseline).
AI.motivation: Scale automated formalization from isolated theorems to entire mathematical textbooks by handling cross-file dependencies, imports, and end-to-end compilation, enabling practical, large-scale formal verification of mathematical literature.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) statement compilation — split documents into atomic blocks, infer dependencies, repair declaration skeletons allowing placeholders while ensuring compilation; (2) proof repair — fill holes under fixed signatures using goal-conditioned local edits. A verifier loop commits edits only if toolchain feedback indicates improvement.
AI.result: Produced a Lean library of 153,853 lines from 479-page textbooks on real analysis and convex analysis, fully formalized with Lean declarations and proofs; achieved 96% proof success on FATE-H (vs. 80% baseline); results and full generated Lean code are available at the linked repository.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the practicality of large-scale automated formalization for mathematical literature, showing that project-scale autoformalization is achievable and significantly faster than expert-only efforts; supports future scaling to broader curricula and domains.

title: Exact Certification of Data-Poisoning Attacks Using Mixed-Integer Programming
comment: Accepted to the 23rd International Conference on the Integration of Constraint Programming, Artificial Intelligence, and Operations Research (CPAIOR)
summary: This work introduces a verification framework that provides both sound and complete guarantees for data poisoning attacks during neural network training. We formulate adversarial data manipulation, model training, and test-time evaluation in a single mixed-integer quadratic programming (MIQCP) problem. Finding the global optimum of the proposed formulation provably yields worst-case poisoning attacks, while simultaneously bounding the effectiveness of all possible attacks on the given training pipeline. Our framework encodes both the gradient-based training dynamics and model evaluation at test time, enabling the first exact certification of training-time robustness. Experimental evaluation on small models confirms that our approach delivers a complete characterization of robustness against data poisoning.

AI.tldr: A mixed-integer quadratic program yields sound and complete certification of training-time robustness against data poisoning by computing the worst-case poisoning and bounding all attacks, validated on small models.
AI.motivation: To provide provable, complete guarantees against data-poisoning attacks during neural network training by unifying data manipulation, training dynamics, and evaluation into a single optimization framework.
AI.method: Formulate adversarial data manipulation, training dynamics, and test-time evaluation as a single MIQCP. The global optimum yields the worst-case poisoning and bounds all possible attacks; the framework encodes gradient-based training and test-time evaluation to certify robustness.
AI.result: The approach offers a complete characterization of robustness against data poisoning; experiments on small models demonstrate the method’s ability to certify training-time robustness.
AI.conclusion: This work delivers the first exact certification of training-time robustness to data-poisoning, enabling sound and complete guarantees for defending neural networks during training.

title: Physics Encoded Spatial and Temporal Generative Adversarial Network for Tropical Cyclone Image Super-resolution
summary: High-resolution satellite imagery is indispensable for tracking the genesis, intensification, and trajectory of tropical cyclones (TCs). However, existing deep learning-based super-resolution (SR) methods often treat satellite image sequences as generic videos, neglecting the underlying atmospheric physical laws governing cloud motion. To address this, we propose a Physics Encoded Spatial and Temporal Generative Adversarial Network (PESTGAN) for TC image super-resolution. Specifically, we design a disentangled generator architecture incorporating a PhyCell module, which approximates the vorticity equation via constrained convolutions and encodes the resulting approximate physical dynamics as implicit latent representations to separate physical dynamics from visual textures. Furthermore, a dual-discriminator framework is introduced, employing a temporal discriminator to enforce motion consistency alongside spatial realism. Experiments on the Digital Typhoon dataset for 4$\times$ upscaling demonstrate that PESTGAN establishes a better performance in structural fidelity and perceptual quality. While maintaining competitive pixel-wise accuracy compared to existing approaches, our method significantly excels in reconstructing meteorologically plausible cloud structures with superior physical fidelity.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed SR for tropical cyclone imagery: a disentangled GAN with a PhyCell that encodes vorticity dynamics, plus temporal and spatial discriminators, achieving superior structural fidelity and meteorological plausibility on 4× Digital Typhoon data.
AI.motivation: Traditional SR treats satellite sequences as generic videos, ignoring atmospheric physics. Incorporating governing fluid dynamics aims to produce physically plausible cloud motion and more useful meteorological representations.
AI.method: Disentangled generator with a PhyCell module approximating the vorticity equation via constrained convolutions to encode physical dynamics as latent representations; a dual-discriminator framework with a temporal discriminator enforcing motion consistency and a spatial discriminator enforcing realism.
AI.result: On Digital Typhoon dataset for 4× upscaling, PESTGAN achieves better structural fidelity and perceptual quality, with competitive pixel-wise accuracy, and produces meteorologically plausible cloud structures with higher physical fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Physics-encoded SR via PESTGAN improves physical plausibility of TC imagery, suggesting physics-informed generative models enhance meteorological data products beyond conventional SR methods.

title: Graph Neural Model Predictive Control for High-Dimensional Systems
summary: The control of high-dimensional systems, such as soft robots, requires models that faithfully capture complex dynamics while remaining computationally tractable. This work presents a framework that integrates Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based dynamics models with structure-exploiting Model Predictive Control to enable real-time control of high-dimensional systems. By representing the system as a graph with localized interactions, the GNN preserves sparsity, while a tailored condensing algorithm eliminates state variables from the control problem, ensuring efficient computation. The complexity of our condensing algorithm scales linearly with the number of system nodes, and leverages Graphics Processing Unit (GPU) parallelization to achieve real-time performance. The proposed approach is validated in simulation and experimentally on a physical soft robotic trunk. Results show that our method scales to systems with up to 1,000 nodes at 100 Hz in closed-loop, and demonstrates real-time reference tracking on hardware with sub-centimeter accuracy, outperforming baselines by 63.6%. Finally, we show the capability of our method to achieve effective full-body obstacle avoidance.

AI.tldr: GNN-based dynamics with structure-exploiting MPC for real-time control of high-dimensional systems; scalable to 1,000 nodes at 100 Hz, enabling sub-centimeter tracking and obstacle avoidance.
AI.motivation: Need scalable, accurate real-time control for high-dimensional systems (e.g., soft robots). Dense dynamics and classic MPC struggle with computation; exploiting sparsity via graph-based models and condensing enables real-time performance.
AI.method: Model the system as a graph with localized interactions using a Graph Neural Network for dynamics; apply a structure-exploiting MPC with a tailored condensing algorithm that eliminates state variables; implement GPU-accelerated parallel computation to achieve real-time performance; validate on simulations and a soft robotic trunk.
AI.result: Demonstrates real-time closed-loop control up to ~1,000 nodes at 100 Hz; sub-centimeter positioning accuracy; outperforms baselines by 63.6%; shows full-body obstacle avoidance in simulation and hardware.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework achieves scalable, real-time control for high-dimensional systems while preserving accuracy, leveraging graph sparsity and condensing to enable practical deployment on soft robots and similar systems.

title: Sales Research Agent and Sales Research Bench
comment: Technical report. 2 figures. Microsoft Dynamics 365 Sales
summary: Enterprises increasingly need AI systems that can answer sales-leader questions over live, customized CRM data, but most available models do not expose transparent, repeatable evidence of quality. This paper describes the Sales Research Agent in Microsoft Dynamics 365 Sales, an AI-first application that connects to live CRM and related data, reasons over complex schemas, and produces decision-ready insights through text and chart outputs. To make quality observable, we introduce the Sales Research Bench, a purpose-built benchmark that scores systems on eight customer-weighted dimensions, including text and chart groundedness, relevance, explainability, schema accuracy, and chart quality. In a 200-question run on a customized enterprise schema on October 19, 2025, the Sales Research Agent outperformed Claude Sonnet 4.5 by 13 points and ChatGPT-5 by 24.1 points on the 100-point composite score, giving customers a repeatable way to compare AI solutions.

AI.tldr: Sales Research Agent integrated with live CRM exposes observable quality through a dedicated benchmark; in a 200-question enterprise-schema evaluation it outperformed Claude Sonnet 4.5 by 13 points and ChatGPT-5 by 24.1 points on a 100-point composite score, enabling repeatable AI-solution comparison.
AI.motivation: Addresses the need for transparent, repeatable evidence of AI quality when answering sales-leader questions from live CRM data; existing models lack observable quality metrics.
AI.method: Develop Sales Research Agent connected to live CRM and related data; reason over complex schemas; introduce the Sales Research Bench with eight customer-weighted dimensions (including text and chart groundedness, relevance, explainability, schema accuracy, and chart quality); evaluate on a customized enterprise schema via a 200-question run (Oct 19, 2025).
AI.result: The agent outperformed Claude Sonnet 4.5 by 13 points and ChatGPT-5 by 24.1 points on the 100-point composite score in a 200-question enterprise-scope evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Provides customers a repeatable way to compare AI solutions; demonstrates a measurable, observable-quality benchmark for enterprise CRM AI assistants.

title: Beyond Message Passing: A Symbolic Alternative for Expressive and Interpretable Graph Learning
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have become essential in high-stakes domains such as drug discovery, yet their black-box nature remains a significant barrier to trustworthiness. While self-explainable GNNs attempt to bridge this gap, they often rely on standard message-passing backbones that inherit fundamental limitations, including the 1-Weisfeiler-Lehman (1-WL) expressivity barrier and a lack of fine-grained interpretability. To address these challenges, we propose SymGraph, a symbolic framework designed to transcend these constraints. By replacing continuous message passing with discrete structural hashing and topological role-based aggregation, our architecture theoretically surpasses the 1-WL barrier, achieving superior expressiveness without the overhead of differentiable optimization. Extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that SymGraph achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing self-explainable GNNs. Notably, SymGraph delivers 10x to 100x speedups in training time using only CPU execution. Furthermore, SymGraph generates rules with superior semantic granularity compared to existing rule-based methods, offering great potential for scientific discovery and explainable AI.

AI.tldr: SymGraph is a symbolic GNN framework that replaces differentiable message passing with discrete hashing and topological role-based aggregation to surpass 1-WL, achieving state-of-the-art results among self-explainable GNNs with significant CPU-based speedups and semantically rich rules.
AI.motivation: To overcome the 1-WL expressivity limit and lack of fine-grained interpretability in self-explainable GNNs, enabling trustworthy AI in high-stakes domains and efficient training.
AI.method: Replace continuous message passing with discrete structural hashing and topological role-based aggregation; theoretical argument for surpassing 1-WL; non-differentiable/symbolic computation; CPU-friendly; rule extraction for interpretability.
AI.result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance among self-explainable GNNs; 10x-100x training speedups on CPU; superior semantic granularity in extracted rules.
AI.conclusion: SymGraph provides enhanced expressivity beyond 1-WL, improved interpretability and efficiency, with potential for scientific discovery; promising direction for explainable AI in graphs.

title: Attachment Anchors: A Novel Framework for Laparoscopic Grasping Point Prediction in Colorectal Surgery
summary: Accurate grasping point prediction is a key challenge for autonomous tissue manipulation in minimally invasive surgery, particularly in complex and variable procedures such as colorectal interventions. Due to their complexity and prolonged duration, colorectal procedures have been underrepresented in current research. At the same time, they pose a particularly interesting learning environment due to repetitive tissue manipulation, making them a promising entry point for autonomous, machine learning-driven support. Therefore, in this work, we introduce attachment anchors, a structured representation that encodes the local geometric and mechanical relationships between tissue and its anatomical attachments in colorectal surgery. This representation reduces uncertainty in grasping point prediction by normalizing surgical scenes into a consistent local reference frame. We demonstrate that attachment anchors can be predicted from laparoscopic images and incorporated into a grasping framework based on machine learning. Experiments on a dataset of 90 colorectal surgeries demonstrate that attachment anchors improve grasping point prediction compared to image-only baselines. There are particularly strong gains in out-of-distribution settings, including unseen procedures and operating surgeons. These results suggest that attachment anchors are an effective intermediate representation for learning-based tissue manipulation in colorectal surgery.

AI.tldr: Attachment anchors provide a structured, local reference frame encoding tissue–attachment geometry to improve grasping point prediction in colorectal surgery, with stronger generalization to unseen procedures and surgeons.
AI.motivation: Autonomous tissue manipulation in minimally invasive colorectal surgery requires robust grasping point prediction. Colorectal procedures are underrepresented in ML-based surgical manipulation research, yet involve repetitive tissue manipulation making them a suitable domain for learning-based support. A normalized local reference frame could reduce scene variability and uncertainty.
AI.method: Introduce attachment anchors as a structured representation encoding local geometric and mechanical relationships between tissue and anatomical attachments. Predict anchors from laparoscopic images and integrate them into a ML-based grasping framework using this normalized reference frame.
AI.result: On a dataset of 90 colorectal surgeries, attachment anchors improve grasping point prediction over image-only baselines, with particularly strong gains in out-of-distribution settings such as unseen procedures and surgeons.
AI.conclusion: Attachment anchors are an effective intermediate representation to enhance learning-based tissue manipulation in colorectal surgery by reducing scene variability and improving generalization for grasping.

title: Phase-Aware Mixture of Experts for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has equipped LLM agents with a strong ability to solve complex tasks. However, existing RL methods normally use a \emph{single} policy network, causing \emph{simplicity bias} where simple tasks occupy most parameters and dominate gradient updates, leaving insufficient capacity for complex tasks. A plausible remedy could be employing the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture in the policy network, as MoE allows different parameters (experts) to specialize in different tasks, preventing simple tasks from dominating all parameters. However, a key limitation of traditional MoE is its token-level routing, where the router assigns each token to specialized experts, which fragments phase-consistent patterns into scattered expert assignments and thus undermines expert specialization. In this paper, we propose \textbf{Phase-Aware Mixture of Experts (PA-MoE)}.
