AI.motivation: Existing SSL view-level contrastive methods fail to capture fine-grained correspondences under sophisticated edits, due to lack of explicit pixel-level mappings and high supervision noise.
AI.method: PixTrace: a pixel coordinate tracking module preserving explicit spatial mappings across editing transformations. CopyNCE: a geometrically-guided contrastive loss that regularizes patch affinity using overlap ratios derived from PixTrace’s verified mappings, bridging pixel-level traceability with patch-level similarity learning.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on DISC21: matcher 88.7% uAP / 83.9% RP90; descriptor 72.6% uAP / 68.4% RP90, with improved interpretability over prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Integrating pixel-level traceability into SSL training reduces supervision noise and yields both stronger detection/descriptor performance and better interpretability for image copy detection.

title: Instructor-Aligned Knowledge Graphs for Personalized Learning
summary: Mastering educational concepts requires understanding both their prerequisites (e.g., recursion before merge sort) and sub-concepts (e.g., merge sort as part of sorting algorithms). Capturing these dependencies is critical for identifying students' knowledge gaps and enabling targeted intervention for personalized learning. This is especially challenging in large-scale courses, where instructors cannot feasibly diagnose individual misunderstanding or determine which concepts need reinforcement. While knowledge graphs offer a natural representation for capturing these conceptual relationships at scale, existing approaches are either surface-level (focusing on course-level concepts like "Algorithms" or logistical relationships such as course enrollment), or disregard the rich pedagogical signals embedded in instructional materials. We propose InstructKG, a framework for automatically constructing instructor-aligned knowledge graphs that capture a course's intended learning progression. Given a course's lecture materials (slides, notes, etc.), InstructKG extracts significant concepts as nodes and infers learning dependencies as directed edges (e.g., "part-of" or "depends-on" relationships). The framework synergizes the rich temporal and semantic signals unique to educational materials (e.g., "recursion" is taught before "mergesort"; "recursion" is mentioned in the definition of "merge sort") with the generalizability of large language models. Through experiments on real-world, diverse lecture materials across multiple courses and human-based evaluation, we demonstrate that InstructKG captures rich, instructor-aligned learning progressions.

AI.tldr: InstructKG automatically constructs course-specific knowledge graphs from lecture materials to map learning progressions and prerequisites, using pedagogical signals and LLMs, validated with real courses.
AI.motivation: Capture prerequisite and sub-concept relationships at scale to identify knowledge gaps and enable targeted intervention in large courses; existing knowledge graphs are too coarse and ignore instructional signals.
AI.method: Extract significant concepts from lecture materials (slides, notes), create concept nodes, infer directed edges (depends-on, part-of) using temporal/semantic cues (e.g., recursion before mergesort; recursion mentioned in merge sort definition) and large language models to generalize across courses.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world, diverse lecture materials and human evaluation show that InstructKG captures rich, instructor-aligned learning progressions.
AI.conclusion: InstructKG provides an automated, scalable framework for constructing instructor-aligned knowledge graphs that reflect a course's intended learning progression, enabling targeted interventions and improved personalization at scale.

title: WS-GRPO: Weakly-Supervised Group-Relative Policy Optimization for Rollout-Efficient Reasoning
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is effective for training language models on complex reasoning. However, since the objective is defined relative to a group of sampled trajectories, extended deliberation can create more chances to realize relative gains, leading to inefficient reasoning and overthinking, and complicating the trade-off between correctness and rollout efficiency. Controlling this behavior is difficult in practice, considering (i) Length penalties are hard to calibrate because longer rollouts may reflect harder problems that require longer reasoning, penalizing tokens risks truncating useful reasoning along with redundant continuation; and (ii) supervision that directly indicates when to continue or stop is typically unavailable beyond final answer correctness. We propose Weakly Supervised GRPO (WS-GRPO), which improves rollout efficiency by converting terminal rewards into correctness-aware guidance over partial trajectories. Unlike global length penalties that are hard to calibrate, WS-GRPO trains a preference model from outcome-only correctness to produce prefix-level signals that indicate when additional continuation is beneficial. Thus, WS-GRPO supplies outcome-derived continue/stop guidance, reducing redundant deliberation while maintaining accuracy. We provide theoretical results and empirically show on reasoning benchmarks that WS-GRPO substantially reduces rollout length while remaining competitive with GRPO baselines.

AI.tldr: WS-GRPO converts terminal rewards into prefix-level, correctness-aware guidance to decide when to continue or stop deliberation, reducing rollout length while staying competitive with GRPO.
AI.motivation: GRPO’s objective relative to sampled trajectory groups encourages extended deliberation; longer rollouts can inflate perceived gains and hamper efficiency. Length penalties are hard to calibrate and supervision for continuation is often unavailable beyond final correctness.
AI.method: Introduce WS-GRPO by learning a preference model from outcome-only correctness to generate prefix-level signals indicating when additional continuation is beneficial, providing continue/stop guidance without global length penalties.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees and empirical results on reasoning benchmarks show WS-GRPO substantially reduces rollout length while remaining competitive with GRPO baselines.
AI.conclusion: WS-GRPO offers outcome-derived guidance to curb redundant deliberation, improving rollout efficiency without sacrificing accuracy, addressing practical calibration issues inherent in length-penalty approaches.

title: FoundationPose-Initialized 3D-2D Liver Registration for Surgical Augmented Reality
summary: Augmented reality can improve tumor localization in laparoscopic liver surgery. Existing registration pipelines typically depend on organ contours; deformable (non-rigid) alignment is often handled with finite-element (FE) models coupled to dimensionality-reduction or machine-learning components. We integrate laparoscopic depth maps with a foundation pose estimator for camera-liver pose estimation and replace FE-based deformation with non-rigid iterative closest point (NICP) to lower engineering/modeling complexity and expertise requirements. On real patient data, the depth-augmented foundation pose approach achieved 9.91 mm mean registration error in 3 cases. Combined rigid-NICP registration outperformed rigid-only registration, demonstrating NICP as an efficient substitute for finite-element deformable models. This pipeline achieves clinically relevant accuracy while offering a lightweight, engineering-friendly alternative to FE-based deformation.

AI.tldr: Depth maps + foundation pose estimator enable non-rigid registration using NICP instead of FE-based models; achieved 9.91 mm mean registration error on 3 patient cases; rigid-NICP outperforms rigid-only, offering a lightweight, engineering-friendly alternative.
AI.motivation: Improve laparoscopic liver tumor localization while reducing reliance on organ contours and complex FE-based deformation; simplify deployment without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: Integrate laparoscopic depth maps with a foundation pose estimator for camera–liver pose estimation and replace FE-based deformation with non-rigid ICP (NICP); evaluate a combined rigid–NICP registration on real patient data (3 cases).
AI.result: 9.91 mm mean registration error across 3 cases; rigid–NICP outperforms rigid-only; demonstrates NICP as an efficient substitute for finite-element deformable models.
AI.conclusion: Depth-augmented foundation pose with NICP yields clinically relevant accuracy and a lightweight alternative to FE-based deformation, reducing engineering complexity.

title: Epistemology of Generative AI: The Geometry of Knowing
summary: Generative AI presents an unprecedented challenge to our understanding of knowledge and its production. Unlike previous technological transformations, where engineering understanding preceded or accompanied deployment, generative AI operates through mechanisms whose epistemic character remains obscure, and without such understanding, its responsible integration into science, education, and institutional life cannot proceed on a principled basis. This paper argues that the missing account must begin with a paradigmatic break that has not yet received adequate philosophical attention. In the Turing-Shannon-von Neumann tradition, information enters the machine as encoded binary vectors, and semantics remains external to the process. Neural network architectures rupture this regime: symbolic input is instantly projected into a high-dimensional space where coordinates correspond to semantic parameters, transforming binary code into a position in a geometric space of meanings. It is this space that constitutes the active epistemic condition shaping generative production. Drawing on four structural properties of high-dimensional geometry concentration of measure, near-orthogonality, exponential directional capacity, and manifold regularity the paper develops an Indexical Epistemology of High-Dimensional Spaces. Building on Peirce semiotics and Papert constructionism, it reconceptualizes generative models as navigators of learned manifolds and proposes navigational knowledge as a third mode of knowledge production, distinct from both symbolic reasoning and statistical recombination.

AI.tldr: Proposes Indexical Epistemology of High-Dimensional Spaces; navigational knowledge within learned manifolds as a third mode of knowledge production in generative AI.
AI.motivation: Address the epistemic nature of generative AI and its divergence from classical information-processing frameworks; provide a principled basis for responsible integration into science, education, and institutions.
AI.method: Theoretical synthesis drawing on high-dimensional geometry (concentration of measure, near-orthogonality, exponential directional capacity, manifold regularity) and reconstruction of Peirce semiotics and Papert constructionism to conceptualize generative models as navigators of learned manifolds.
AI.result: A new epistemic framework—Indexical Epistemology of High-Dimensional Spaces—that identifies navigational knowledge as distinct from symbolic reasoning and statistical recombination and treats generative models as agents navigating learned manifolds.
AI.conclusion: If adopted, this framework offers a principled lens for evaluating and guiding the use of generative AI, with implications for research, pedagogy, and policy; it invites empirical validation of the navigational knowledge concept.

title: Transforming Behavioral Neuroscience Discovery with In-Context Learning and AI-Enhanced Tensor Methods
summary: Scientific discovery pipelines typically involve complex, rigid, and time-consuming processes, from data preparation to analyzing and interpreting findings. Recent advances in AI have the potential to transform such pipelines in a way that domain experts can focus on interpreting and understanding findings, rather than debugging rigid pipelines or manually annotating data. As part of an active collaboration between data science/AI researchers and behavioral neuroscientists, we showcase an example AI-enhanced pipeline, specifically designed to transform and accelerate the way that the domain experts in the team are able to gain insights out of experimental data. The application at hand is in the domain of behavioral neuroscience, studying fear generalization in mice, an important problem whose progress can advance our understanding of clinically significant and often debilitating conditions such as PTSD (Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder). We identify the emerging paradigm of "In-Context Learning" (ICL) as a suitable interface for domain experts to automate parts of their pipeline without the need for or familiarity with AI model training and fine-tuning, and showcase its remarkable efficacy in data preparation and pattern interpretation. Also, we introduce novel AI-enhancements to tensor decomposition model, which allows for more seamless pattern discovery from the heterogeneous data in our application. We thoroughly evaluate our proposed pipeline experimentally, showcasing its superior performance compared to what is standard practice in the domain, as well as against reasonable ML baselines that do not fall under the ICL paradigm, to ensure that we are not compromising performance in our quest for a seamless and easy-to-use interface for domain experts. Finally, we demonstrate effective discovery, with results validated by the domain experts in the team.

AI.tldr: An AI-enhanced behavioral neuroscience pipeline uses In-Context Learning (ICL) and tensor-decomposition improvements to accelerate data preparation and pattern discovery in fear generalization studies in mice; evaluated against standard practice and ML baselines, with domain-expert validation.
AI.motivation: Address the bottlenecks of rigid, time-consuming scientific pipelines and free domain experts to interpret findings by leveraging AI via ICL, enabling automated data preparation and pattern interpretation in a heterogeneous, neuroscience dataset.
AI.method: Develop an AI-enhanced pipeline that employs In-Context Learning as the user-facing interface for automation, alongside novel enhancements to a tensor decomposition model to facilitate pattern discovery from heterogeneous data; compare performance against standard domain practices and ML baselines lacking ICL; include experimental evaluation and domain-expert validation.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior performance over standard practices and reasonable baselines; effective discovery validated by domain experts in the team.
AI.conclusion: ICL-enabled AI-enhanced pipelines can streamline behavioral neuroscience analyses, accelerate insights, and may generalize to other domains with heterogeneous data, supporting faster progress toward understanding fear generalization and related conditions.

title: LATA: Laplacian-Assisted Transductive Adaptation for Conformal Uncertainty in Medical VLMs
summary: Medical vision-language models (VLMs) are strong zero-shot recognizers for medical imaging, but their reliability under domain shift hinges on calibrated uncertainty with guarantees. Split conformal prediction (SCP) offers finite-sample coverage, yet prediction sets often become large (low efficiency) and class-wise coverage unbalanced-high class-conditioned coverage gap (CCV), especially in few-shot, imbalanced regimes; moreover, naively adapting to calibration labels breaks exchangeability and voids guarantees. We propose \texttt{\textbf{LATA}} (Laplacian-Assisted Transductive Adaptation), a \textit{training- and label-free} refinement that operates on the joint calibration and test pool by smoothing zero-shot probabilities over an image-image k-NN graph using a small number of CCCP mean-field updates, preserving SCP validity via a deterministic transform. We further introduce a \textit{failure-aware} conformal score that plugs into the vision-language uncertainty (ViLU) framework, providing instance-level difficulty and label plausibility to improve prediction set efficiency and class-wise balance at fixed coverage. \texttt{\textbf{LATA}} is black-box (no VLM updates), compute-light (windowed transduction, no backprop), and includes an optional prior knob that can run strictly label-free or, if desired, in a label-informed variant using calibration marginals once. Across \textbf{three} medical VLMs and \textbf{nine} downstream tasks, \texttt{\textbf{LATA}} consistently reduces set size and CCV while matching or tightening target coverage, outperforming prior transductive baselines and narrowing the gap to label-using methods, while using far less compute. Comprehensive ablations and qualitative analyses show that \texttt{\textbf{LATA}} sharpens zero-shot predictions without compromising exchangeability.

AI.tldr: LATA is a training- and label-free transductive refinement for medical vision-language models that uses Laplacian smoothing on a joint calibration/test graph to tighten conformal prediction sets, reduce class-conditional coverage gaps, and maintain finite-sample coverage. It plugs a failure-aware conformal score into ViLU, supports an optional label-informed variant, and is black-box with low compute. Empirically, it reduces set size and CCV across three VLMs and nine tasks while preserving coverage, outperforming prior strategies and narrowing the gap to label-using methods.
AI.motivation: Calibrated uncertainty under domain shift remains challenging for medical VLMs. Split conformal prediction (SCP) ensures finite-sample coverage but yields large, inefficient prediction sets and unbalanced class-wise coverage (CCV), especially in few-shot, imbalanced settings. Naively adapting calibration labels breaks exchangeability and guarantees. A lightweight, training- and label-free refinement that preserves SCP validity is needed to improve reliability and efficiency without heavy model updates.
AI.method: LATA refines predictions on the joint calibration and test pool using image-image k-NN graph smoothing of zero-shot probabilities and a small number of CCCP (concave-convex procedure) mean-field updates. This preserves SCP validity via a deterministic transform. A failure-aware conformal score is introduced to assess instance difficulty and label plausibility, improving set efficiency and CCV within ViLU. An optional prior knob allows strictly label-free use or a label-informed variant using calibration marginals. The approach is black-box (no VLM updates), compute-light (windowed transduction, no backprop), and can operate with or without calibration labels.
AI.result: Across three medical VLMs and nine downstream tasks, LATA consistently reduces prediction set size and class-conditional coverage gap while matching or tightening the target coverage. It outperforms prior transductive baselines and narrows the gap to label-using methods, with substantially lower compute. Ablations and qualitative analyses show sharper zero-shot predictions without compromising exchangeability.
AI.conclusion: LATA provides an effective, efficient, training- and label-free transductive refinement for medical VLM uncertainty, improving set efficiency and CCV under domain shift, while preserving finite-sample coverage and exchangeability. It can operate in strict label-free mode or with optional calibration marginals, and demonstrates robust gains across multiple models and tasks.

title: Efficient Parallel Algorithm for Decomposing Hard CircuitSAT Instances
summary: We propose a novel parallel algorithm for decomposing hard CircuitSAT instances. The technique employs specialized constraints to partition an original SAT instance into a family of weakened formulas. Our approach is implemented as a parameterized parallel algorithm, where adjusting the parameters allows efficient identification of high-quality decompositions, guided by hardness estimations computed in parallel. We demonstrate the algorithm's practical efficacy on challenging CircuitSAT instances, including those encoding Logical Equivalence Checking of Boolean circuits and preimage attacks on cryptographic hash functions.

AI.tldr: A parallel, parameterized SAT-decomposition method that partitions hard CircuitSAT problems into weaker subproblems via specialized constraints, using parallel hardness estimates to guide selection of high-quality decompositions; demonstrated on logical-equivalence checking and hash-preimage tasks.
AI.motivation: Hard CircuitSAT instances arise in circuit verification (e.g., equivalence checking) and cryptanalytic scenarios (e.g., preimage attacks). Conventional SAT solving struggles on such large, structured problems; a scalable decomposition approach aims to exploit parallelism and improve tractability.
AI.method: Introduce specialized constraints to generate a family of weakened formulas from the original SAT instance. Implement as a parameterized parallel algorithm; tune parameters to efficiently identify high-quality decompositions; compute hardness estimations in parallel to guide the decomposition process.
AI.result: Empirically demonstrates practical effectiveness on challenging CircuitSAT instances, including boolean circuit logical equivalence checking and preimage attacks on cryptographic hash functions.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a scalable, parallelizable framework for decomposing hard CircuitSAT problems, with potential applicability to verification and cryptanalysis tasks; future work could include formal hardness metrics and broader empirical validation.

title: Forecasting Anomaly Precursors via Uncertainty-Aware Time-Series Ensembles
comment: This manuscript contains 14 pages and 8 figures. It is currently under review at IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems (TNNLS)
summary: Detecting anomalies in time-series data is critical in domains such as industrial operations, finance, and cybersecurity, where early identification of abnormal patterns is essential for ensuring system reliability and enabling preventive maintenance. However, most existing methods are reactive: they detect anomalies only after they occur and lack the capability to provide proactive early warning signals. In this paper, we propose FATE (Forecasting Anomalies with Time-series Ensembles), a novel unsupervised framework for detecting Precursors-of-Anomaly (PoA) by quantifying predictive uncertainty from a diverse ensemble of time-series forecasting models. Unlike prior approaches that rely on reconstruction errors or require ground-truth labels, FATE anticipates future values and leverages ensemble disagreement to signal early signs of potential anomalies without access to target values at inference time. To rigorously evaluate PoA detection, we introduce Precursor Time-series Aware Precision and Recall (PTaPR), a new metric that extends the traditional Time-series Aware Precision and Recall (TaPR) by jointly assessing segment-level accuracy, within-segment coverage, and temporal promptness of early predictions. This enables a more holistic assessment of early warning capabilities that existing metrics overlook. Experiments on five real-world benchmark datasets show that FATE achieves an average improvement of 19.9 percentage points in PTaPR AUC and 20.02 percentage points in early detection F1 score, outperforming baselines while requiring no anomaly labels. These results demonstrate the effectiveness and practicality of FATE for real-time unsupervised early warning in complex time-series environments.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised time-series anomaly precursor detection via ensemble forecasting; introduces PTaPR metric and demonstrates strong early-warning performance without anomaly labels.
AI.motivation: Existing anomaly detection methods are largely reactive and depend on labeled data or reconstruction errors, limiting proactive, label-free early warning in complex time-series.
AI.method: FATE constructs a diverse ensemble of forecasting models to forecast future values and quantify predictive uncertainty via ensemble disagreement. It detects Precursors-of-Anomaly (PoA) without access to true targets at inference. It also introduces PTaPR (Precursor Time-series Aware Precision and Recall), extending TaPR to evaluate segment-level accuracy, within-segment coverage, and temporal promptness.
AI.result: On five real-world datasets, FATE improves PTaPR AUC by about 19.9 percentage points and early detection F1 by about 20.02 points over baselines, without requiring anomaly labels.
AI.conclusion: FATE is effective and practical for real-time unsupervised early warning in complex time-series settings, enabling proactive detection of potential anomalies without ground-truth labels.

title: GraphThinker: Reinforcing Video Reasoning with Event Graph Thinking
summary: Video reasoning requires understanding the causal relationships between events in a video. However, such relationships are often implicit and costly to annotate manually. While existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often infer event relations through dense captions or video summaries for video reasoning, such modeling still lacks causal understanding. Without explicit causal structure modeling within and across video events, these models suffer from hallucinations during the video reasoning. In this work, we propose GraphThinker, a reinforcement finetuning-based method that constructs structural event-level scene graphs and enhances visual grounding to jointly reduce hallucinations in video reasoning. Specifically, we first employ an MLLM to construct an event-based video scene graph (EVSG) that explicitly models both intra- and inter-event relations, and incorporate these formed scene graphs into the MLLM as an intermediate thinking process. We also introduce a visual attention reward during reinforcement finetuning, which strengthens video grounding and further mitigates hallucinations. We evaluate GraphThinker on two datasets, RexTime and VidHalluc, where it shows superior ability to capture object and event relations with more precise event localization, reducing hallucinations in video reasoning compared to prior methods.

AI.tldr: GraphThinker introduces an event-based scene graph (EVSG) and a visual-attention reward in reinforcement finetuning to reduce hallucinations in video reasoning by explicitly modeling intra- and inter-event relations and grounding.
AI.motivation: Video reasoning suffers from implicit causal relations and hallucinations due to lack of explicit causal structure; current MLLMs rely on dense captions but miss causal reasoning.
AI.method: An MLLM constructs an EVSG capturing intra- and inter-event relations. The EVSG is incorporated as an intermediate thinking step. A visual attention reward is used during reinforcement finetuning to strengthen video grounding and further mitigate hallucinations.
AI.result: On RexTime and VidHalluc datasets, GraphThinker demonstrates improved capture of object and event relations and more precise event localization, with reduced hallucinations compared with prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Explicit event-level graph reasoning plus grounding-focused reinforcement learning can substantially reduce hallucinations and improve video reasoning performance, suggesting the value of integrated structural and grounding modules.

title: Bonsai: A Framework for Convolutional Neural Network Acceleration Using Criterion-Based Pruning
summary: As the need for more accurate and powerful Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) increases, so too does the size, execution time, memory footprint, and power consumption. To overcome this, solutions such as pruning have been proposed with their own metrics and methodologies, or criteria, for how weights should be removed. These solutions do not share a common implementation and are difficult to implement and compare. In this work, we introduce Combine, a criterion- based pruning solution and demonstrate that it is fast and effective framework for iterative pruning, demonstrate that criterion have differing effects on different models, create a standard language for comparing criterion functions, and propose a few novel criterion functions. We show the capacity of these criterion functions and the framework on VGG inspired models, pruning up to 79\% of filters while retaining or improving accuracy, and reducing the computations needed by the network by up to 68\%.

AI.tldr: Combine is a criterion-based pruning framework enabling fast, standardized iterative pruning; it demonstrates strong pruning performance across VGG-inspired models, achieving substantial filter pruning and computation reductions while preserving or improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Pruning literature lacks a unified, easily comparable set of criteria and frameworks. The lack of standardization makes implementation, benchmarking, and cross-model comparisons difficult, hindering progress.
AI.method: Introduce Combine as a criterion-based pruning solution that defines a standard language for comparing pruning criteria, proposes novel criterion functions, and applies iterative pruning to VGG-inspired models to assess performance.
AI.result: Achieves up to 79% pruning of convolutional filters and up to 68% reduction in computational load, with accuracy retention or improvement. Demonstrates that different criteria have varying effects across models.
AI.conclusion: Provides a unified framework and language for criterion-based pruning, enabling easier comparison, faster experimentation, and potential for greater compression without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Multi-Probe Zero Collision Hash (MPZCH): Mitigating Embedding Collisions and Enhancing Model Freshness in Large-Scale Recommenders
summary: Embedding tables are critical components of large-scale recommendation systems, facilitating the efficient mapping of high-cardinality categorical features into dense vector representations. However, as the volume of unique IDs expands, traditional hash-based indexing methods suffer from collisions that degrade model performance and personalization quality. We present Multi-Probe Zero Collision Hash (MPZCH), a novel indexing mechanism based on linear probing that effectively mitigates embedding collisions. With reasonable table sizing, it often eliminates these collisions entirely while maintaining production-scale efficiency. MPZCH utilizes auxiliary tensors and high-performance CUDA kernels to implement configurable probing and active eviction policies. By retiring obsolete IDs and resetting reassigned slots, MPZCH prevents the stale embedding inheritance typical of hash-based methods, ensuring new features learn effectively from scratch. Despite its collision-mitigation overhead, the system maintains training QPS and inference latency comparable to existing methods. Rigorous online experiments demonstrate that MPZCH achieves zero collisions for user embeddings and significantly improves item embedding freshness and quality. The solution has been released within the open-source TorchRec library for the broader community.

AI.tldr: MPZCH is a multi-probe linear-probing hash for embedding tables that eliminates collisions (notably for user embeddings) while preserving production-scale speed; released in TorchRec.
AI.motivation: Embedding tables suffer collisions with high-cardinality IDs, harming personalization quality; need scalable, collision-free or collision-minimized indexing.
AI.method: Use linear probing with multiple probes, auxiliary tensors, and CUDA kernels; configurable probing and active eviction; retire obsolete IDs and reset reassigned slots to avoid stale inheritance.
AI.result: Online experiments show zero collisions for user embeddings; improved freshness/quality of item embeddings; training QPS and inference latency comparable to baselines.
AI.conclusion: MPZCH is a practical, scalable collision-mitigating approach for embeddings, suitable for production; open-source in TorchRec.

title: RetouchIQ: MLLM Agents for Instruction-Based Image Retouching with Generalist Reward
summary: Recent advances in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown great potential for extending vision-language reasoning to professional tool-based image editing, enabling intuitive and creative editing. A promising direction is to use reinforcement learning (RL) to enable MLLMs to reason about and execute optimal tool-use plans within professional image-editing software. However, training remains challenging due to the lack of reliable, verifiable reward signals that can reflect the inherently subjective nature of creative editing. In this work, we introduce RetouchIQ, a framework that performs instruction-based executable image editing through MLLM agents guided by a generalist reward model. RetouchIQ interprets user-specified editing intentions and generates corresponding, executable image adjustments, bridging high-level aesthetic goals with precise parameter control. To move beyond conventional, rule-based rewards that compute similarity against a fixed reference image using handcrafted metrics, we propose a generalist reward model, an RL fine-tuned MLLM that evaluates edited results through a set of generated metrics on a case-by-case basis. Then, the reward model provides scalar feedback through multimodal reasoning, enabling reinforcement learning with high-quality, instruction-consistent gradients. We curate an extended dataset with 190k instruction-reasoning pairs and establish a new benchmark for instruction-based image editing. Experiments show that RetouchIQ substantially improves both semantic consistency and perceptual quality over previous MLLM-based and diffusion-based editing systems. Our findings demonstrate the potential of generalist reward-driven MLLM agents as flexible, explainable, and executable assistants for professional image editing.

AI.tldr: RetouchIQ uses a generalist reward-driven RL framework for instruction-based image editing with MLLMs, leveraging a reward model trained to generate case-specific metrics to guide editing, achieving better semantic consistency and perceptual quality than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of reliable, verifiable reward signals for creative image editing and connect high-level user intents with precise, executable editing parameters via an interpretable, multimodal reward model.
AI.method: Introduce RetouchIQ: an RL-based framework where an MLLM agent performs image editing in professional software guided by a generalist reward model. Train on 190k instruction-reasoning pairs for instruction-based editing. The reward model is an RL-finetuned MLLM that evaluates edits using generated, case-specific metrics and provides scalar feedback to optimize the agent. Establish a new benchmark for instruction-based editing.
AI.result: RetouchIQ yields substantial improvements in semantic consistency and perceptual quality over prior MLLM-based and diffusion-based editing systems on the new benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Generalist reward-driven MLLM agents offer flexible, explainable, and executable assistance for professional image editing, enabling instruction-consistent optimization in creative tasks.

title: JEPA-DNA: Grounding Genomic Foundation Models through Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures
summary: Genomic Foundation Models (GFMs) have largely relied on Masked Language Modeling (MLM) or Next Token Prediction (NTP) to learn the language of life. While these paradigms excel at capturing local genomic syntax and fine-grained motif patterns, they often fail to capture the broader functional context, resulting in representations that lack a global biological perspective. We introduce JEPA-DNA, a novel pre-training framework that integrates the Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) with traditional generative objectives. JEPA-DNA introduces latent grounding by coupling token-level recovery with a predictive objective in the latent space by supervising a CLS token. This forces the model to predict the high-level functional embeddings of masked genomic segments rather than focusing solely on individual nucleotides. JEPA-DNA extends both NTP and MLM paradigms and can be deployed either as a standalone from-scratch objective or as a continual pre-training enhancement for existing GFMs. Our evaluations across a diverse suite of genomic benchmarks demonstrate that JEPA-DNA consistently yields superior performance in supervised and zero-shot tasks compared to generative-only baselines. By providing a more robust and biologically grounded representation, JEPA-DNA offers a scalable path toward foundation models that understand not only the genomic alphabet, but also the underlying functional logic of the sequence.

AI.tldr: JEPA-DNA combines latent-space predictive grounding with traditional MLM/NTP objectives to learn high-level functional embeddings in genomic data, yielding superior performance on supervised and zero-shot tasks versus generative-only baselines.
AI.motivation: GFMs trained with MLM/NTP capture local sequence motifs but struggle with global functional context; a biologically grounded, global representation is needed for robust downstream performance and generalization.
AI.method: Extend the Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) to DNA by coupling token-level recovery (MLM/NTP-like objective) with a latent-space predictive objective supervised via a CLS token. This latent prediction forces the model to recover high-level functional embeddings of masked genomic segments rather than individual nucleotides. Can be used as a standalone pre-training objective or as continual pre-training to enhance existing GFMs.
AI.result: Across diverse genomic benchmarks, JEPA-DNA yields superior performance in both supervised and zero-shot tasks compared with generative-only baselines, demonstrating improved alignment with functional genomic signals and more robust representations.
AI.conclusion: JEPA-DNA offers a scalable, biologically grounded path for foundation models that understand not only the genomic alphabet but also the underlying functional logic, by grounding token recovery in latent, functionally meaningful embeddings.

title: Sign Lock-In: Randomly Initialized Weight Signs Persist and Bottleneck Sub-Bit Model Compression
summary: Sub-bit model compression seeks storage below one bit per weight; as magnitudes are aggressively compressed, the sign bit becomes a fixed-cost bottleneck. Across Transformers, CNNs, and MLPs, learned sign matrices resist low-rank approximation and are spectrally indistinguishable from an i.i.d. Rademacher baseline. Despite this apparent randomness, most weights retain their initialization signs; flips primarily occur via rare near-zero boundary crossings, suggesting that sign-pattern randomness is largely inherited from initialization. We formalize this behavior with sign lock-in theory, a stopping-time analysis of sign flips under SGD noise. Under bounded updates and a rare re-entry condition into a small neighborhood around zero, the number of effective sign flips exhibits a geometric tail. Building on this mechanism, we introduce a gap-based initialization and a lightweight outward-drift regularizer, reducing the effective flip rate to approximately $10^{-3}$ with only about a one-point increase in perplexity.

AI.tldr: Sub-bit compression is bottlenecked by fixed sign bits; learned sign matrices appear random and resist low-rank compression; most weight signs stay from initialization; flips occur mainly near-zero and are governed by sign-lock-in dynamics; a gap-based initialization and outward-drift regularizer reduce sign flips with modest perplexity impact.
AI.motivation: Explain why sign patterns in aggressively quantized models behave as if random, quantify flip dynamics under SGD noise, and derive practical methods to reduce sign flips without large performance loss.
AI.method: Introduce sign lock-in theory: a stopping-time analysis of SGD-induced sign flips under bounded updates and a rare re-entry condition into a zero-neighborhood; show the flips have a geometric tail. Build method from this insight with gap-based initialization and outward-drift regularizer.
AI.result: The number of effective sign flips follows a geometric distribution with a tail, yielding a very low flip rate (~10^-3). The proposed gap-based initialization plus outward-drift regularizer achieves this low flip rate and incurs only about a one-point increase in perplexity.
AI.conclusion: Sign-pattern randomness largely originates from initialization; sign-lock-in provides a principled explanation for rare flips. The proposed initialization and regularization reduce flip rate with minimal performance cost, and the findings apply across architectures (Transformers, CNNs, MLPs).

title: Art2Mus: Artwork-to-Music Generation via Visual Conditioning and Large-Scale Cross-Modal Alignment
summary: Music generation has advanced markedly through multimodal deep learning, enabling models to synthesize audio from text and, more recently, from images. However, existing image-conditioned systems suffer from two fundamental limitations: (i) they are typically trained on natural photographs, limiting their ability to capture the richer semantic, stylistic, and cultural content of artworks; and (ii) most rely on an image-to-text conversion stage, using language as a semantic shortcut that simplifies conditioning but prevents direct visual-to-audio learning. Motivated by these gaps, we introduce ArtSound, a large-scale multimodal dataset of 105,884 artwork-music pairs enriched with dual-modality captions, obtained by extending ArtGraph and the Free Music Archive. We further propose ArtToMus, the first framework explicitly designed for direct artwork-to-music generation, which maps digitized artworks to music without image-to-text translation or language-based semantic supervision. The framework projects visual embeddings into the conditioning space of a latent diffusion model, enabling music synthesis guided solely by visual information. Experimental results show that ArtToMus generates musically coherent and stylistically consistent outputs that reflect salient visual cues of the source artworks. While absolute alignment scores remain lower than those of text-conditioned systems-as expected given the substantially increased difficulty of removing linguistic supervision-ArtToMus achieves competitive perceptual quality and meaningful cross-modal correspondence. This work establishes direct visual-to-music generation as a distinct and challenging research direction, and provides resources that support applications in multimedia art, cultural heritage, and AI-assisted creative practice. Code and dataset will be publicly released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Introduces ArtSound, a large-scale artwork–music dataset (105,884 pairs) and ArtToMus, a direct artwork-to-music generation framework that uses visual embeddings within a latent diffusion model without image-to-text translation or linguistic supervision.
AI.motivation: Current image-conditioned music generation often relies on natural photographs and an image-to-text preprocessing step, which limits grounding in the semantic, stylistic, and cultural content of artworks and obstructs direct visual-to-audio learning.
AI.method: Construct ArtSound by extending ArtGraph and Free Music Archive with dual-modality captions; propose ArtToMus that maps visual embeddings into the conditioning space of a latent diffusion model to synthesize music from artwork visuals without linguistic supervision.
AI.result: ArtToMus produces musically coherent, stylistically consistent outputs that reflect salient visual cues; alignment with text-conditioned baselines is lower but perceptual quality and cross-modal correspondence are competitive.
AI.conclusion: Direct visual-to-music generation is viable and distinct; the dataset and framework enable applications in multimedia art and AI-assisted creativity, with code and data to be released upon acceptance.

title: Texo: Formula Recognition within 20M Parameters
summary: In this paper we present Texo, a minimalist yet highperformance formula recognition model that contains only 20 million parameters. By attentive design, distillation and transfer of the vocabulary and the tokenizer, Texo achieves comparable performance to state-of-the-art models such as UniMERNet-T and PPFormulaNet-S, while reducing the model size by 80% and 65%, respectively. This enables real-time inference on consumer-grade hardware and even in-browser deployment. We also developed a web application to demonstrate the model capabilities and facilitate its usage for end users.

AI.tldr: Texo is a compact, 20M-parameter formula recognition model that matches state-of-the-art performance while drastically reducing size, enabling real-time and in-browser use; accompanied by a web app.
AI.motivation: The need for high-performance formula recognition that runs efficiently on consumer hardware and supports in-browser deployment, reducing latency and resource demands compared to larger models.
AI.method: A minimalist, high-performance architecture using attentive design, model distillation, and transfer of vocabulary and tokenizer to preserve accuracy with a much smaller parameter count.
AI.result: Texo achieves comparable performance to UniMERNet-T and PPFormulaNet-S while reducing model size by ~80% and ~65% respectively; enables real-time inference on consumer hardware and in-browser deployment.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that compact, well-engineered models with transfer learning strategies can deliver state-of-the-art-style results in formula recognition while significantly improving accessibility via web apps and client-side inference.

title: Spatio-temporal dual-stage hypergraph MARL for human-centric multimodal corridor traffic signal control
summary: Human-centric traffic signal control in corridor networks must increasingly account for multimodal travelers, particularly high-occupancy public transportation, rather than focusing solely on vehicle-centric performance. This paper proposes STDSH-MARL (Spatio-Temporal Dual-Stage Hypergraph based Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning), a scalable multi-agent deep reinforcement learning framework that follows a centralized training and decentralized execution paradigm. The proposed method captures spatio-temporal dependencies through a novel dual-stage hypergraph attention mechanism that models interactions across both spatial and temporal hyperedges. In addition, a hybrid discrete action space is introduced to jointly determine the next signal phase configuration and its corresponding green duration, enabling more adaptive signal timing decisions. Experiments conducted on a corridor network under five traffic scenarios demonstrate that STDSH-MARL consistently improves multimodal performance and provides clear benefits for public transportation priority. Compared with state-of-the-art baseline methods, the proposed approach achieves superior overall performance. Further ablation studies confirm the contribution of each component of STDSH-MARL, with temporal hyperedges identified as the most influential factor driving the observed performance gains.

AI.tldr: Introduces STDSH-MARL, a scalable multi-agent DRL framework using dual-stage hypergraph attention and a hybrid action space to optimize corridor traffic with multimodal emphasis; achieves superior multimodal performance and public-transport priority gains across five scenarios.
AI.motivation: Address multimodal traffic, especially high-occupancy public transit, in corridor networks rather than vehicle-centric optimization; need scalable MA RL with spatio-temporal modeling.
AI.method: Centralized training, decentralized execution; dual-stage hypergraph attention modeling interactions via spatial and temporal hyperedges; hybrid discrete action space to pick signal phase and green duration; evaluated on corridor network under five scenarios; ablation studies.
AI.result: Consistent performance improvements for multimodal metrics; clear public transport priority benefits; outperforms state-of-the-art baselines; ablation identifies temporal hyperedges as the most influential factor.
AI.conclusion: STDSH-MARL effectively models spatio-temporal dependencies and enables adaptive signaling with a hybrid action space; temporal hyperedges drive the gains; scalable for corridor networks with multimodal traffic.

title: Adapting Actively on the Fly: Relevance-Guided Online Meta-Learning with Latent Concepts for Geospatial Discovery
summary: In many real-world settings, such as environmental monitoring, disaster response, or public health, with costly and difficult data collection and dynamic environments, strategically sampling from unobserved regions is essential for efficiently uncovering hidden targets under tight resource constraints. Yet, sparse and biased geospatial ground truth limits the applicability of existing learning-based methods, such as reinforcement learning. To address this, we propose a unified geospatial discovery framework that integrates active learning, online meta-learning, and concept-guided reasoning. Our approach introduces two key innovations built on a shared notion of *concept relevance*, which captures how domain-specific factors influence target presence: a *concept-weighted uncertainty sampling strategy*, where uncertainty is modulated by learned relevance based on readily-available domain-specific concepts (e.g., land cover, source proximity); and a *relevance-aware meta-batch formation strategy* that promotes semantic diversity during online-meta updates, improving generalization in dynamic environments. Our experiments include testing on a real-world dataset of cancer-causing PFAS (Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) contamination, showcasing our method's reliability at uncovering targets with limited data and a varying environment.

AI.tldr: A unified geospatial discovery framework that fuses active learning, online meta-learning, and concept-guided reasoning to efficiently uncover hidden targets under sparse data, demonstrated on PFAS contamination.
AI.motivation: In resource-constrained, dynamic geospatial settings, sparse and biased ground truth hinders learning-based methods. There is a need for sample-efficient discovery that leverages domain knowledge to guide exploration and maintain generalization.
AI.method: Propose two innovations built on the concept relevance idea: (1) concept-weighted uncertainty sampling where uncertainty is modulated by learned relevance from domain concepts (e.g., land cover, source proximity); (2) relevance-aware meta-batch formation that enhances semantic diversity during online-meta updates to improve generalization in dynamic environments.
AI.result: Experiments on a real-world PFAS contamination dataset show reliable target discovery under limited data and varying environments, indicating robustness of the approach in sparse settings.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a unified, concept-guided approach to geospatial discovery that improves data-efficiency and generalization in dynamic contexts, with potential applicability to other environmental and public-health discovery problems beyond PFAS.

authors: Telmo de Menezes e Silva Filho
title: Continual learning and refinement of causal models through dynamic predicate invention
summary: Efficiently navigating complex environments requires agents to internalize the underlying logic of their world, yet standard world modelling methods often struggle with sample inefficiency, lack of transparency, and poor scalability. We propose a framework for constructing symbolic causal world models entirely online by integrating continuous model learning and repair into the agent's decision loop, by leveraging the power of Meta-Interpretive Learning and predicate invention to find semantically meaningful and reusable abstractions, allowing an agent to construct a hierarchy of disentangled, high-quality concepts from its observations. We demonstrate that our lifted inference approach scales to domains with complex relational dynamics, where propositional methods suffer from combinatorial explosion, while achieving sample-efficiency orders of magnitude higher than the established PPO neural-network-based baseline.

AI.tldr: Online symbolic causal world models using Meta-Interpretive Learning and predicate invention to build reusable, hierarchical abstractions; scalable lifted inference reduces sample complexity by orders of magnitude over PPO baselines.
AI.motivation: Address sample inefficiency, lack of transparency, and poor scalability in standard world models for complex, relational environments by learning semantically meaningful abstractions online.
AI.method: Integrate continuous model learning and repair into the agent's decision loop; apply Meta-Interpretive Learning (MIL) and predicate invention to discover high-level, reusable concepts; employ lifted (first-order) inference to handle relational dynamics and construct a hierarchy of disentangled concepts.
AI.result: Demonstrates that lifted inference scales to complex relational domains where propositional methods fail; achieves sample-efficiency orders of magnitude higher than a PPO neural-network baseline.
AI.conclusion: Online, symbolic causal world models learned via MIL and predicate invention yield transparent, scalable, and highly sample-efficient planning in complex environments, bridging symbolic reasoning with model-based learning.

title: AdvSynGNN: Structure-Adaptive Graph Neural Nets via Adversarial Synthesis and Self-Corrective Propagation
summary: Graph neural networks frequently encounter significant performance degradation when confronted with structural noise or non-homophilous topologies. To address these systemic vulnerabilities, we present AdvSynGNN, a comprehensive architecture designed for resilient node-level representation learning. The proposed framework orchestrates multi-resolution structural synthesis alongside contrastive objectives to establish geometry-sensitive initializations. We develop a transformer backbone that adaptively accommodates heterophily by modulating attention mechanisms through learned topological signals. Central to our contribution is an integrated adversarial propagation engine, where a generative component identifies potential connectivity alterations while a discriminator enforces global coherence. Furthermore, label refinement is achieved through a residual correction scheme guided by per-node confidence metrics, which facilitates precise control over iterative stability. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that this synergistic approach effectively optimizes predictive accuracy across diverse graph distributions while maintaining computational efficiency. The study concludes with practical implementation protocols to ensure the robust deployment of the AdvSynGNN system in large-scale environments.

AI.tldr: AdvSynGNN integrates multi-resolution structural synthesis, contrastive learning, transformer-based heterophily-aware attention, and adversarial propagation with residual label refinement to achieve robust, scalable node-level representations under noisy and non-homophilous graphs.
AI.motivation: Graph neural networks degrade under structural noise and non-homophilous topologies; there is a need for resilient, geometry-aware initialization and propagation mechanisms to maintain performance across diverse graph distributions.
AI.method: AdvSynGNN architecture combining: (1) multi-resolution structural synthesis with contrastive objectives; (2) a transformer backbone that adapts to heterophily via learned topological signals; (3) an adversarial propagation engine with a generator identifying potential connectivity changes and a discriminator enforcing global coherence; (4) label refinement via a residual correction scheme guided by per-node confidence metrics to stabilize iterative updates.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show improved predictive accuracy across diverse graph distributions while maintaining computational efficiency, with practical deployment protocols for large-scale environments.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields robust, scalable node-level representations under noisy and heterophilous conditions, and provides a practical framework for deploying resilient GNN systems in large-scale settings.

title: CORAL: Correspondence Alignment for Improved Virtual Try-On
summary: Existing methods for Virtual Try-On (VTON) often struggle to preserve fine garment details, especially in unpaired settings where accurate person-garment correspondence is required. These methods do not explicitly enforce person-garment alignment and fail to explain how correspondence emerges within Diffusion Transformers (DiTs). In this paper, we first analyze full 3D attention in DiT-based architecture and reveal that the person-garment correspondence critically depends on precise person-garment query-key matching within the full 3D attention. Building on this insight, we then introduce CORrespondence ALignment (CORAL), a DiT-based framework that explicitly aligns query-key matching with robust external correspondences. CORAL integrates two complementary components: a correspondence distillation loss that aligns reliable matches with person-garment attention, and an entropy minimization loss that sharpens the attention distribution. We further propose a VLM-based evaluation protocol to better reflect human preference. CORAL consistently improves over the baseline, enhancing both global shape transfer and local detail preservation. Extensive ablations validate our design choices.

AI.tldr: CORAL introduces explicit person-garment alignment in a Diffusion Transformer-based virtual try-on (VTON) framework by enforcing correspondence with a correspondence distillation loss and sharpening attention via entropy minimization, plus a VLM-based evaluation protocol. It analyzes full 3D attention to show that accurate query–key matching underpins person–garment alignment, and demonstrates consistent improvements over baselines with ablations.
AI.motivation: Unpaired VTON often fails to preserve fine garment details due to missing explicit person–garment alignment; understanding how correspondence emerges in diffusion transformer architectures is essential for reliable alignment.
AI.method: Analyze full 3D attention in a DiT-based VTON model and introduce CORAL (CORrespondence ALignment). CORAL adds two components: (1) a correspondence distillation loss that aligns reliable person–garment matches with the model's attention, and (2) an entropy minimization loss to sharpen the attention distribution. It leverages robust external correspondences and incorporates a VLM-based evaluation protocol to better reflect human preferences.
AI.result: CORAL consistently improves over the baseline in both global shape transfer and local detail preservation. Extensive ablations validate the design choices, demonstrating the effectiveness of explicit correspondence alignment and attention sharpening in diffusion-transformer-based VTON.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly aligning query–key matching with external correspondences in a DiT-based VTON framework yields better person–garment alignment, improved detail preservation, and more faithful shape transfer, with validated gains across ablations and supported by a VLM-based evaluation protocol.

title: From Labor to Collaboration: A Methodological Experiment Using AI Agents to Augment Research Perspectives in Taiwan's Humanities and Social Sciences
summary:
Generative AI is reshaping knowledge work, yet existing research focuses predominantly on software engineering and the natural sciences, with limited methodological exploration for the humanities and social sciences. Positioned as a "methodological experiment," this study proposes an AI Agent-based collaborative research workflow (Agentic Workflow) for humanities and social science research. Taiwan's Claude.ai usage data (N = 7,729 conversations, November 2025) from the Anthropic Economic Index (AEI) serves as the empirical vehicle for validating the feasibility of this methodology.
  This study operates on two levels: the primary level is the design and validation of a methodological framework - a seven-stage modular workflow grounded in three principles: task modularization, human-AI division of labor, and verifiability, with each stage delineating clear roles for human researchers (research judgment and ethical decisions) and AI Agents (information retrieval and text generation); the secondary level is the empirical analysis of AEI Taiwan data - serving as an operational demonstration of the workflow's application to secondary data research, showcasing both the process and output quality (see Appendix A).
  This study contributes by proposing a replicable AI collaboration framework for humanities and social science researchers, and identifying three operational modes of human-AI collaboration - direct execution, iterative refinement, and human-led - through reflexive documentation of the operational process. This taxonomy reveals the irreplaceability of human judgment in research question formulation, theoretical interpretation, contextualized reasoning, and ethical reflection. Limitations including single-platform data, cross-sectional design, and AI reliability risks are acknowledged.

AI.tldr: Proposes and validates a seven-stage AI Agent-based workflow for humanities/social sciences, using AEI Taiwan data to demonstrate three human-AI collaboration modes and the enduring role of human judgment.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of AI methodological experimentation in humanities/social sciences and develop a replicable collaborative framework that goes beyond software engineering and natural sciences.
AI.method: Design and validation of a seven-stage modular workflow grounded in task modularization, human-AI division of labor, and verifiability; delineates roles for humans (research judgment and ethics) and AI Agents (information retrieval and text generation). Empirical demonstration via AEI Taiwan data (N=7,729 conversations, Nov 2025). Reflexive documentation to identify three collaboration modes.
AI.result: A replicable AI collaboration framework for humanities/social sciences; identifies three operational modes (direct execution, iterative refinement, human-led); shows human judgment remains irreplaceable in question formulation, theoretical interpretation, contextualized reasoning, and ethical reflection; acknowledges limitations (single-platform data, cross-sectional design, AI reliability risks).
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a replicable blueprint for AI-assisted humanities/social science research, with implications for improving research quality and transparency, while highlighting the need for broader validation and robustness against AI reliability concerns.

title: Adam Improves Muon: Adaptive Moment Estimation with Orthogonalized Momentum
summary: Efficient stochastic optimization typically integrates an update direction that performs well in the deterministic regime with a mechanism adapting to stochastic perturbations. While Adam uses adaptive moment estimates to promote stability, Muon utilizes the weight layers' matrix structure via orthogonalized momentum, showing superior performance in large language model training. We propose a new optimizer and a diagonal extension, NAMO and NAMO-D, providing the first principled integration of orthogonalized momentum with norm-based Adam-type noise adaptation. NAMO scales orthogonalized momentum using a single adaptive stepsize, preserving orthogonality while improving upon Muon at negligible additional cost. NAMO-D instead right-multiplies orthogonalized momentum by a diagonal matrix with clamped entries. This design enables neuron-wise noise adaptation and aligns with the common near block-diagonal Hessian structure. Under standard assumptions, we establish optimal convergence rates for both algorithms in the deterministic setting and show that, in the stochastic setting, their convergence guarantees adapt to the noise level of stochastic gradients. Experiments on pretraining GPT-2 models demonstrate improved performance of both NAMO and NAMO-D compared to the AdamW and Muon baselines, with NAMO-D achieving further gains over NAMO via an additional clamping hyperparameter that balances the competing goals of maintaining a well-conditioned update direction and leveraging fine-grained noise adaptation.

AI.tldr: NAMO and NAMO-D fuse orthogonalized momentum with norm-based Adam-style noise adaptation. They achieve favorable convergence guarantees and show empirical gains over AdamW and Muon in GPT-2 pretraining, with NAMO-D offering additional gains via a clamped diagonal handoff.
AI.motivation: Address the need for an optimizer that combines a principled, well-conditioned update direction (orthogonalized momentum) with robust adaptation to stochastic gradient noise (norm-based Adam-type). Existing approaches (AdamW, Muon) offer either adaptive moments or layer-structured momentum; a principled integration promises better stability and sample efficiency, especially in large-scale LM training.
AI.method: NAMO scales orthogonalized momentum with a single adaptive stepsize to preserve orthogonality while improving stability; NAMO-D right-multiplies orthogonalized momentum by a diagonal matrix with clamped entries to enable neuron-wise noise adaptation aligned with a near block-diagonal Hessian. The authors present theoretical convergence results: optimal rates in the deterministic setting and stochastic convergence that adapts to gradient noise. They also provide empirical validation on pretraining GPT-2 models, comparing against AdamW and Muon.
AI.result: Deterministic setting: optimal convergence rates established for both NAMO and NAMO-D. Stochastic setting: convergence guarantees adapt to the noise level of stochastic gradients. Experiments on GPT-2 pretraining show improved performance for both NAMO and NAMO-D relative to AdamW and Muon; NAMO-D yields further gains due to the additional clamping hyperparameter balancing conditioning and noise adaptation.
AI.conclusion: NAMO and NAMO-D provide the first principled integration of orthogonalized momentum with norm-based Adam-type noise adaptation, achieving improved convergence properties with negligible extra cost. NAMO-D’s diagonal clamping enables fine-grained, neuron-wise noise adaptation and leverages near block-diagonal Hessian structure, with practical gains in large-scale LM pretraining.

title: IntRec: Intent-based Retrieval with Contrastive Refinement
summary: Retrieving user-specified objects from complex scenes remains a challenging task, especially when queries are ambiguous or involve multiple similar objects. Existing open-vocabulary detectors operate in a one-shot manner, lacking the ability to refine predictions based on user feedback. To address this, we propose IntRec, an interactive object retrieval framework that refines predictions based on user feedback. At its core is an Intent State (IS) that maintains dual memory sets for positive anchors (confirmed cues) and negative constraints (rejected hypotheses). A contrastive alignment function ranks candidate objects by maximizing similarity to positive cues while penalizing rejected ones, enabling fine-grained disambiguation in cluttered scenes. Our interactive framework provides substantial improvements in retrieval accuracy without additional supervision. On LVIS, IntRec achieves 35.4 AP, outperforming OVMR, CoDet, and CAKE by +2.3, +3.7, and +0.5, respectively. On the challenging LVIS-Ambiguous benchmark, it improves performance by +7.9 AP over its one-shot baseline after a single corrective feedback, with less than 30 ms of added latency per interaction.

AI.tldr: Interactive open-vocabulary object retrieval with an Intent State; user feedback refines predictions via positive/negative cues, improving AP on LVIS and LVIS-Ambiguous with minimal latency.
AI.motivation: Ambiguity and clutter in complex scenes; existing one-shot detectors cannot leverage user feedback to disambiguate similar objects.
AI.method: IntRec maintains an Intent State with dual memory: positive anchors (confirmed cues) and negative constraints (rejected hypotheses). A contrastive alignment function ranks candidates by maximizing similarity to positives while penalizing negatives, enabling iterative refinement via user feedback with no extra supervision.
AI.result: On LVIS, IntRec attains 35.4 AP, outperforming OVMR, CoDet, and CAKE by +2.3, +3.7, and +0.5 respectively. On LVIS-Ambiguous, it achieves +7.9 AP improvement over its one-shot baseline after a single corrective feedback, with <30 ms latency per interaction.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates effective interactive refinement for open-vocabulary object retrieval, yielding significant accuracy gains and low interaction latency without additional supervision.

title: Decoding the Human Factor: High Fidelity Behavioral Prediction for Strategic Foresight
summary: Predicting human decision-making in high-stakes environments remains a central challenge for artificial intelligence. While large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong general reasoning, they often struggle to generate consistent, individual-specific behavior, particularly when accurate prediction depends on complex interactions between psychological traits and situational constraints. Prompting-based approaches can be brittle in this setting, exhibiting identity drift and limited ability to leverage increasingly detailed persona descriptions. To address these limitations, we introduce the Large Behavioral Model (LBM), a behavioral foundation model fine-tuned to predict individual strategic choices with high fidelity. LBM shifts from transient persona prompting to behavioral embedding by conditioning on a structured, high-dimensional trait profile derived from a comprehensive psychometric battery. Trained on a proprietary dataset linking stable dispositions, motivational states, and situational constraints to observed choices, LBM learns to map rich psychological profiles to discrete actions across diverse strategic dilemmas. In a held-out scenario evaluation, LBM fine-tuning improves behavioral prediction relative to the unadapted Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct backbone and performs comparably to frontier baselines when conditioned on Big Five traits. Moreover, we find that while prompting-based baselines exhibit a complexity ceiling, LBM continues to benefit from increasingly dense trait profiles, with performance improving as additional trait dimensions are provided. Together, these results establish LBM as a scalable approach for high-fidelity behavioral simulation, enabling applications in strategic foresight, negotiation analysis, cognitive security, and decision support.

AI.tldr: Introduces Large Behavioral Model (LBM), a behavioral foundation model fine-tuned to predict individual strategic choices from rich psychometric profiles, outperforming prompting baselines and rivaling frontier models when conditioned on trait data; demonstrates scalable high-fidelity behavioral simulation with growing performance as trait detail increases.
AI.motivation: Address brittleness of prompting-based methods and identity drift in modeling individual-specific decision-making. Leverage structured, high-dimensional psychometric profiles to predict decisions in high-stakes, strategic scenarios.
AI.method: Fine-tune a base LLM (LBM) on a proprietary dataset mapping stable dispositions, motivational states, and situational constraints to observed choices. Use structured trait profiles (e.g., Big Five and more dimensions) to condition predictions across diverse strategic dilemmas. Evaluate on held-out scenarios, comparing against unadapted backbone (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) and frontier baselines, with analyses of how trait-density affects performance.
AI.result: LBM improves behavioral prediction relative to the unadapted backbone and matches frontier baselines when conditioned on Big Five traits. Unlike prompting baselines, LBM benefits from denser trait profiles, with performance continuing to improve as more trait dimensions are provided.
AI.conclusion: LBM offers a scalable, high-fidelity approach to behavioral simulation, with potential applications in strategic foresight, negotiation analysis, cognitive security, and decision support. The work underscores the value of structured behavioral embeddings over transient persona prompts.

title: MeGU: Machine-Guided Unlearning with Target Feature Disentanglement
summary: The growing concern over training data privacy has elevated the "Right to be Forgotten" into a critical requirement, thereby raising the demand for effective Machine Unlearning. However, existing unlearning approaches commonly suffer from a fundamental trade-off: aggressively erasing the influence of target data often degrades model utility on retained data, while conservative strategies leave residual target information intact. In this work, the intrinsic representation properties learned during model pretraining are analyzed. It is demonstrated that semantic class concepts are entangled at the feature-pattern level, sharing associated features while preserving concept-specific discriminative components. This entanglement fundamentally limits the effectiveness of existing unlearning paradigms. Motivated by this insight, we propose Machine-Guided Unlearning (MeGU), a novel framework that guides unlearning through concept-aware re-alignment. Specifically, Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are leveraged to explicitly determine re-alignment directions for target samples by assigning semantically meaningful perturbing labels. To improve efficiency, inter-class conceptual similarities estimated by the MLLM are encoded into a lightweight transition matrix. Furthermore, MeGU introduces a positive-negative feature noise pair to explicitly disentangle target concept influence. During finetuning, the negative noise suppresses target-specific feature patterns, while the positive noise reinforces remaining associated features and aligns them with perturbing concepts. This coordinated design enables selective disruption of target-specific representations while preserving shared semantic structures. As a result, MeGU enables controlled and selective forgetting, effectively mitigating both under-unlearning and over-unlearning.

AI.tldr: MeGU uses concept-aware re-alignment guided by MLLMs and a positive/negative feature-noise scheme to achieve selective forgetting, balancing unlearning with retention of shared semantics.
AI.motivation: Address the unlearning trade-off under data privacy demands by analyzing entangled representation of semantic concepts in pretrained models and proposing a framework that uncouples target-specific from shared features.
AI.method: 1) Analyze representation entanglement; 2) Use Multi-modal LLMs to determine re-alignment directions by assigning perturbing labels; 3) Encode inter-class concept similarities into a light transition matrix; 4) Introduce positive-negative feature noise pairs during finetuning; 5) Negative noise suppresses target-specific features; 6) Positive noise reinforces remaining features aligned with perturbing concepts.
AI.result: Selective disruption of target-specific representations with preservation of shared semantic structures, mitigating both under-unlearning and over-unlearning, enabling controlled and selective forgetting.
AI.conclusion: MeGU offers an effective, efficient unlearning framework guided by cross-modal semantic alignment and noise-based disentanglement, advancing practical rights-to-forget in ML systems.

title: Human-level 3D shape perception emerges from multi-view learning
summary: Humans can infer the three-dimensional structure of objects from two-dimensional visual inputs. Modeling this ability has been a longstanding goal for the science and engineering of visual intelligence, yet decades of computational methods have fallen short of human performance. Here we develop a modeling framework that predicts human 3D shape inferences for arbitrary objects, directly from experimental stimuli. We achieve this with a novel class of neural networks trained using a visual-spatial objective over naturalistic sensory data; given a set of images taken from different locations within a natural scene, these models learn to predict spatial information related to these images, such as camera location and visual depth, without relying on any object-related inductive biases. Notably, these visual-spatial signals are analogous to sensory cues readily available to humans. We design a zero-shot evaluation approach to determine the performance of these `multi-view' models on a well established 3D perception task, then compare model and human behavior. Our modeling framework is the first to match human accuracy on 3D shape inferences, even without task-specific training or fine-tuning. Remarkably, independent readouts of model responses predict fine-grained measures of human behavior, including error patterns and reaction times, revealing a natural correspondence between model dynamics and human perception. Taken together, our findings indicate that human-level 3D perception can emerge from a simple, scalable learning objective over naturalistic visual-spatial data. All code, human behavioral data, and experimental stimuli needed to reproduce our findings can be found on our project page.

AI.tldr: A neural network framework learns 3D shape inferences from multi-view natural scenes using a visual-spatial objective, achieving human-level accuracy in zero-shot 3D perception without task-specific training; model readouts align with human behavior, and code/data are released.
AI.motivation: To explain human 3D structure perception from 2D inputs and overcome limitations of prior computational models by using data-driven visual-spatial cues without object-specific inductive biases.
AI.method: Train a class of neural networks with a visual-spatial objective: given images from different viewpoints within a natural scene, predict spatial information (camera location, depth) and renderings; evaluate zero-shot on 3D shape tasks; compare with human data; use multi-view readouts to relate to human behavior.
AI.result: Models match human accuracy on 3D shape inferences without task-specific training; their responses' readouts predict human error patterns and reaction times; demonstrates emergent human-like 3D perception from simple, scalable objective; strong alignment with human data.
AI.conclusion: Human-level 3D perception can emerge from learning solely from naturalistic visual-spatial data using simple objectives; the approach provides a scalable, generalizable framework and offers access to code and stimuli for reproducibility.

title: Mechanistic Interpretability of Cognitive Complexity in LLMs via Linear Probing using Bloom's Taxonomy
summary: The black-box nature of Large Language Models necessitates novel evaluation frameworks that transcend surface-level performance metrics. This study investigates the internal neural representations of cognitive complexity using Bloom's Taxonomy as a hierarchical lens. By analyzing high-dimensional activation vectors from different LLMs, we probe whether different cognitive levels, ranging from basic recall (Remember) to abstract synthesis (Create), are linearly separable within the model's residual streams.
