Our results demonstrate that linear classifiers achieve approximately 95% mean accuracy across all Bloom levels, providing strong evidence that cognitive level is encoded in a linearly accessible subspace of the model's representations. These findings provide evidence that the model resolves the cognitive difficulty of a prompt early in the forward pass, with representations becoming increasingly separable across layers.

AI.tldr: Linearly separable Bloom-level signals exist in LLM activations; linear probes reach ~95% accuracy across levels, indicating cognitive difficulty is encoded early and becomes more separable across layers.
AI.motivation: To probe whether cognitive complexity, as captured by Bloom’s taxonomy, is embedded in internal representations of LLMs and whether a lightweight linear readout can extract the cognitive level from activations, informing interpretability and evaluation beyond surface metrics.
AI.method: Analyze high-dimensional activation vectors (residual streams) from several LLMs labeled by Bloom levels (Remember to Create). Train linear classifiers (e.g., logistic regression) to predict Bloom level from activations, evaluate across layers and models, and assess separability versus depth.
AI.result: Mean accuracy ~95% across Bloom levels; evidence for a linearly accessible subspace encoding cognitive level; separability increases across layers, suggesting early resolution of cognitive difficulty in the forward pass.
AI.conclusion: Cognitive complexity is encoded in linearly decodable subspaces within LLM representations. Linear probes suffice to recover Bloom-level information, supporting a linear decodability view and highlighting the value of simple evaluation probes beyond surface performance; further work should address generalization, labeling scope, and potential model-specific effects.

title: Synergizing Transport-Based Generative Models and Latent Geometry for Stochastic Closure Modeling
summary: Diffusion models recently developed for generative AI tasks can produce high-quality samples while still maintaining diversity among samples to promote mode coverage, providing a promising path for learning stochastic closure models. Compared to other types of generative AI models, such as GANs and VAEs, the sampling speed is known as a key disadvantage of diffusion models. By systematically comparing transport-based generative models on a numerical example of 2D Kolmogorov flows, we show that flow matching in a lower-dimensional latent space is suited for fast sampling of stochastic closure models, enabling single-step sampling that is up to two orders of magnitude faster than iterative diffusion-based approaches. To control the latent space distortion and thus ensure the physical fidelity of the sampled closure term, we compare the implicit regularization offered by a joint training scheme against two explicit regularizers: metric-preserving (MP) and geometry-aware (GA) constraints. Besides offering a faster sampling speed, both explicitly and implicitly regularized latent spaces inherit the key topological information from the lower-dimensional manifold of the original complex dynamical system, which enables the learning of stochastic closure models without demanding a huge amount of training data.

AI.tldr: Flow matching in a latent space enables fast, single-step sampling of stochastic closure models for 2D Kolmogorov flows, outperforming diffusion-based methods by ~100x; both implicit (joint training) and explicit (MP/GA) regularization preserve topological information with reduced training data.
AI.motivation: Overcome the slow sampling inherent to diffusion models while maintaining sample diversity and physical fidelity for stochastic closure modeling of complex dynamical systems; leverage lower-dimensional latent spaces to enable fast, scalable generation.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of transport-based generative models on a 2D Kolmogorov-flow example. Implement flow matching in a lower-dimensional latent space to achieve single-step sampling. Evaluate regularization strategies: (i) implicit regularization via a joint training scheme, and (ii) explicit regularizers: metric-preserving (MP) and geometry-aware (GA) constraints. Assess topological fidelity and data efficiency.
AI.result: Single-step sampling is up to two orders of magnitude faster than iterative diffusion-based approaches. Both implicit and explicit regularized latent spaces retain key topological information from the original low-dimensional manifold, enabling learning of stochastic closure models with substantially less training data.
AI.conclusion: Latent-space flow matching with appropriate regularization achieves fast, physically faithful sampling for stochastic closure models and preserves essential topological structure, offering a viable alternative to diffusion models in turbulence-like systems; further validation across higher dimensions and broader regimes is warranted.

title: All Leaks Count, Some Count More: Interpretable Temporal Contamination Detection in LLM Backtesting
summary: To evaluate whether LLMs can accurately predict future events, we need the ability to \textit{backtest} them on events that have already resolved. This requires models to reason only with information available at a specified past date. Yet LLMs may inadvertently leak post-cutoff knowledge encoded during training, undermining the validity of retrospective evaluation. We introduce a claim-level framework for detecting and quantifying this \emph{temporal knowledge leakage}. Our approach decomposes model rationales into atomic claims and categorizes them by temporal verifiability, then applies \textit{Shapley values} to measure each claim's contribution to the prediction. This yields the \textbf{Shapley}-weighted \textbf{D}ecision-\textbf{C}ritical \textbf{L}eakage \textbf{R}ate (\textbf{Shapley-DCLR}), an interpretable metric that captures what fraction of decision-driving reasoning derives from leaked information. Building on this framework, we propose \textbf{Time}-\textbf{S}upervised \textbf{P}rediction with \textbf{E}xtracted \textbf{C}laims (\textbf{TimeSPEC}), which interleaves generation with claim verification and regeneration to proactively filter temporal contamination -- producing predictions where every supporting claim can be traced to sources available before the cutoff date. Experiments on 350 instances spanning U.S. Supreme Court case prediction, NBA salary estimation, and stock return ranking reveal substantial leakage in standard prompting baselines. TimeSPEC reduces Shapley-DCLR while preserving task performance, demonstrating that explicit, interpretable claim-level verification outperforms prompt-based temporal constraints for reliable backtesting.

AI.tldr: Introduces a claim-level framework to detect and quantify temporal knowledge leakage in LLM retrospective evaluations, via Shapley-valued attribution of atomic claims; proposes TimeSPEC to interleave generation with claim verification, reducing leakage (Shapley-DCLR) while preserving performance.
AI.motivation: Backtesting LLMs requires using information available only up to a past date. However models may leak post-cutoff knowledge learned during training, biasing retrospective evaluations. A principled, interpretable metric is needed to quantify and mitigate this temporal leakage.
AI.method: Decompose model rationales into atomic claims and categorize by temporal verifiability. Compute Shapley values to attribute prediction to each claim, yielding Shapley-DCLR (Shapley-weighted Decision-Critical Leakage Rate). Propose TimeSPEC, which alternates generation with claim verification and regeneration to ensure all supporting claims originate from sources before the cutoff.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 350 instances across Supreme Court case prediction, NBA salary estimation, and stock return ranking shows substantial temporal leakage in standard prompts. TimeSPEC reduces Shapley-DCLR while preserving task performance.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, interpretable claim-level verification can outperform prompt-based temporal constraints for reliable backtesting, suggesting a practical approach to mitigate temporal contamination in LLM evaluations.

title: A Locality Radius Framework for Understanding Relational Inductive Bias in Database Learning
summary: Foreign key discovery and related schema-level prediction tasks are often modeled using graph neural networks (GNNs), implicitly assuming that relational inductive bias improves performance. However, it remains unclear when multi-hop structural reasoning is actually necessary. In this work, we introduce locality radius, a formal measure of the minimum structural neighborhood required to determine a prediction in relational schemas. We hypothesize that model performance depends critically on alignment between task locality radius and architectural aggregation depth. We conduct a controlled empirical study across foreign key prediction, join cost estimation, blast radius regression, cascade impact classification, and additional graph-derived schema tasks. Our evaluation includes multi-seed experiments, capacity-matched comparisons, statistical significance testing, scaling analysis, and synthetic radius-controlled benchmarks. Results reveal a consistent bias-radius alignment effect.

AI.tldr: Introduces locality radius as the minimum structural neighborhood needed for predictions in relational schemas; finds that model performance hinges on alignment between task locality and aggregation depth in GNNs.
AI.motivation: To clarify when multi-hop relational reasoning is necessary in graph-based schema tasks and to provide a formal, actionable measure to guide model design.
AI.method: Define locality radius; conduct controlled experiments across foreign key prediction, join cost estimation, blast radius regression, cascade impact classification, and related graph-derived tasks. Use multi-seed experiments, capacity-matched comparisons, statistical significance testing, scaling analysis, and synthetic radius-controlled benchmarks.
AI.result: Across tasks, a consistent bias-radius alignment effect emerges: performance improves when the GNN's aggregation depth matches the task's locality radius; misalignment degrades performance.
AI.conclusion: Locality radius is a practical diagnostic for designing relational GNNs. Aligning aggregation depth with task locality enhances performance; radius-controlled benchmarks and analysis offer guidance for architecture choice and efficiency.

title: OpenEarthAgent: A Unified Framework for Tool-Augmented Geospatial Agents
summary: Recent progress in multimodal reasoning has enabled agents that can interpret imagery, connect it with language, and perform structured analytical tasks. Extending such capabilities to the remote sensing domain remains challenging, as models must reason over spatial scale, geographic structures, and multispectral indices while maintaining coherent multi-step logic. To bridge this gap, OpenEarthAgent introduces a unified framework for developing tool-augmented geospatial agents trained on satellite imagery, natural-language queries, and detailed reasoning traces. The training pipeline relies on supervised fine-tuning over structured reasoning trajectories, aligning the model with verified multistep tool interactions across diverse analytical contexts. The accompanying corpus comprises 14,538 training and 1,169 evaluation instances, with more than 100K reasoning steps in the training split and over 7K reasoning steps in the evaluation split. It spans urban, environmental, disaster, and infrastructure domains, and incorporates GIS-based operations alongside index analyses such as NDVI, NBR, and NDBI. Grounded in explicit reasoning traces, the learned agent demonstrates structured reasoning, stable spatial understanding, and interpretable behaviour through tool-driven geospatial interactions across diverse conditions. We report consistent improvements over a strong baseline and competitive performance relative to recent open and closed-source models.

AI.tldr: Unified, tool-augmented geospatial agent trained with structured reasoning traces over satellite imagery, achieving consistent improvements over baselines and competitive performance with open/closed models across urban, environmental, disaster, and infrastructure tasks, using GIS operations and indices like NDVI, NBR, and NDBI.
AI.motivation: Remote sensing reasoning must cope with spatial scale, geographic structures, and multispectral indices; existing multimodal agents struggle to maintain coherent multi-step logic in geospatial contexts. A unified, tool-enabled framework can align reasoning with verified tool interactions to improve interpretability and performance.
AI.method: Supervised fine-tuning over structured reasoning trajectories in a unified framework (OpenEarthAgent). Training corpus includes 14,538 training and 1,169 evaluation instances, with over 100k reasoning steps in training and over 7k in evaluation. The approach integrates GIS-based operations with multispectral index analyses (NDVI, NBR, NDBI) and enforces explicit reasoning traces to achieve stable spatial understanding and interpretable tool-driven interactions across urban, environmental, disaster, and infrastructure domains.
AI.result: The learned agent exhibits structured reasoning, stable spatial understanding, and interpretable behavior through tool-driven geospatial interactions. It achieves consistent improvements over a strong baseline and competitive performance relative to recent open- and closed-source models across diverse conditions.
AI.conclusion: OpenEarthAgent’s framework effectively enables tool-augmented geospatial reasoning with explicit tracing, yielding robust, interpretable performance across multiple geospatial domains. The approach demonstrates potential for broader adoption in GIS-enabled decision support and suggests directions for scaling reasoning traces, expanding indices, and integrating additional tools for real-time analysis.

title: Web Verbs: Typed Abstractions for Reliable Task Composition on the Agentic Web
summary: The Web is evolving from a medium that humans browse to an environment where software agents act on behalf of users. Advances in large language models (LLMs) make natural language a practical interface for goal-directed tasks, yet most current web agents operate on low-level primitives such as clicks and keystrokes. These operations are brittle, inefficient, and difficult to verify. Complementing content-oriented efforts such as NLWeb's semantic layer for retrieval, we argue that the agentic web also requires a semantic layer for web actions. We propose \textbf{Web Verbs}, a web-scale set of typed, semantically documented functions that expose site capabilities through a uniform interface, whether implemented through APIs or robust client-side workflows. These verbs serve as stable and composable units that agents can discover, select, and synthesize into concise programs. This abstraction unifies API-based and browser-based paradigms, enabling LLMs to synthesize reliable and auditable workflows with explicit control and data flow. Verbs can carry preconditions, postconditions, policy tags, and logging support, which improves \textbf{reliability} by providing stable interfaces, \textbf{efficiency} by reducing dozens of steps into a few function calls, and \textbf{verifiability} through typed contracts and checkable traces. We present our vision, a proof-of-concept implementation, and representative case studies that demonstrate concise and robust execution compared to existing agents. Finally, we outline a roadmap for standardization to make verbs deployable and trustworthy at web scale.

AI.tldr: Proposes Web Verbs, a typed, semantic action layer for the web that unifies API and browser workflows to improve reliability, efficiency, and verifiability of web agents using LLMs.
AI.motivation: Current web automation relies on brittle, low-level actions (clicks/keystrokes). A stable, composable action layer with pre/postconditions and policy/logging can improve reliability, efficiency, and verifiability and enable LLMs to synthesize robust workflows.
AI.method: Introduce Web Verbs: a web-scale catalog of typed, semantically documented functions exposing site capabilities via a uniform interface (APIs or robust client-side workflows). Verbs can be discovered, selected, and composed into concise programs. They support preconditions, postconditions, policy tags, and logging, enabling checkable traces and controlled data flow.
AI.result: Proof-of-concept implementation and representative case studies showing concise, robust execution compared with existing agents.
AI.conclusion: Outline a standardization roadmap to deploy and trust Web Verbs at web scale.

title: FLoRG: Federated Fine-tuning with Low-rank Gram Matrices and Procrustes Alignment
summary: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques such as low-rank adaptation (LoRA) enable large language models (LLMs) to adapt to downstream tasks efficiently. Federated learning (FL) further facilitates this process by enabling collaborative fine-tuning across distributed clients without sharing private data. However, the use of two separate low-rank matrices in LoRA for federated fine-tuning introduces two types of challenges. The first challenge arises from the error induced by separately aggregating those two low-rank matrices. The second challenge occurs even when the product of two low-rank matrices is aggregated. The server needs to recover factors via matrix decomposition, which is non-unique and can introduce decomposition drift. To tackle the aforementioned challenges, we propose FLoRG, a federated fine-tuning framework which employs a single low-rank matrix for fine-tuning and aggregates its Gram matrix (i.e., the matrix of inner products of its column vectors), eliminating the aggregation error while also reducing the communication overhead. FLoRG minimizes the decomposition drift by introducing a Procrustes alignment approach which aligns the decomposed matrix between consecutive fine-tuning rounds for consistent updates. We theoretically analyze the convergence of FLoRG and prove that adopting the Procrustes alignment results in a tighter convergence bound. Experimental results across multiple LLM fine-tuning benchmarks demonstrate that FLoRG outperforms five state-of-the-art baseline schemes in the downstream task accuracy and can reduce the communication overhead by up to 2041$\times$.

AI.tldr: FLoRG: a FL framework for LoRA-based fine-tuning using a single low-rank matrix with Gram-matrix aggregation and Procrustes alignment, achieving lower communication, reduced decomposition drift, provable convergence improvements, and superior downstream accuracy versus baselines (up to ~2000x communication savings).
AI.motivation: To fix two core issues in federated LoRA fine-tuning: (1) aggregation error when combining two separate low-rank matrices, and (2) non-unique factor recovery (decomposition drift) when aggregating products, which hampers convergence.
AI.method: Replace dual low-rank factors with a single low-rank matrix. Aggregate the Gram matrix (inner products of its columns) instead of the matrix itself. Apply Procrustes alignment between consecutive rounds to reduce decomposition drift. Provide convergence analysis showing tighter bounds with alignment.
AI.result: Empirical results on multiple LLM fine-tuning benchmarks show FLoRG outperforms five state-of-the-art baselines in downstream task accuracy and achieves substantial communication savings (up to 2041×).
AI.conclusion: FLoRG offers an effective and communication-efficient federated fine-tuning approach for LoRA-based LLM adaptation, with theoretical convergence benefits and strong empirical gains.

title: ArXiv-to-Model: A Practical Study of Scientific LM Training
summary: While frontier large language models demonstrate strong reasoning and mathematical capabilities, the practical process of training domain-specialized scientific language models from raw sources remains under-documented. In this work, we present a detailed case study of training a 1.36B-parameter scientific language model directly from raw arXiv LaTeX sources spanning mathematics, computer science, and theoretical physics. We describe an end-to-end pipeline covering metadata filtering, archive validation, LaTeX extraction, text normalization, domain-aware tokenization, and dense transformer training under constrained compute (2xA100 GPUs). Through 24 experimental runs, we analyze training stability, scaling behavior, data yield losses, and infrastructure bottlenecks. Our findings highlight how preprocessing decisions significantly affect usable token volume, how tokenization impacts symbolic stability, and how storage and I/O constraints can rival compute as limiting factors. We further analyze convergence dynamics and show stable training behavior in a data-rich regime (52B pretraining tokens). Rather than proposing a novel architecture, this work provides an engineering-grounded, transparent account of training a small scientific language model from scratch. We hope these insights support researchers operating under moderate compute budgets who seek to build domain-specialized models.

AI.tldr: Engineering-grounded case study of training a 1.36B scientific language model from raw arXiv LaTeX sources under constrained compute (2x A100 GPUs). It details an end-to-end pipeline, analyzes 24 experimental runs, and highlights how preprocessing, tokenization, and I/O/storage bottlenecks shape data yield and convergence, offering practical guidance for domain-specific LMs without proposing new architectures.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between frontier LLMs and domain-specific models by documenting practical, under-explored aspects of data preprocessing, tokenization, and infrastructure in training scientific LMs from raw sources; provide actionable insights for researchers with moderate compute budgets.
AI.method: End-to-end pipeline: metadata filtering, arXiv archive validation, LaTeX extraction, text normalization, domain-aware tokenization, and dense transformer pretraining on 2x A100 GPUs. 24 experimental runs to study training stability, scaling, data-yield losses, and infrastructure bottlenecks. Evaluation includes convergence dynamics in a data-rich regime (52B pretraining tokens).
AI.result: Key findings: preprocessing decisions significantly affect usable token volume; tokenization impacts symbolic stability; storage and I/O constraints can rival compute as limiting factors. The model training is stable in the data-rich regime (52B tokens). The work is an engineering case study rather than a new architecture, offering transparent, replicable insights for practitioners.
AI.conclusion: Provides an engineering-grounded, transparent account of training a small scientific language model from scratch, aiding researchers with moderate compute budgets in building domain-specialized models; emphasizes practical bottlenecks and reproducibility over architectural novelty.

title: Operationalization of Machine Learning with Serverless Architecture: An Industrial Operationalization of Machine Learning with Serverless Architecture: An Industrial Implementation for Harmonized System Code Prediction
summary: This paper presents a serverless MLOps framework orchestrating the complete ML lifecycle from data ingestion, training, deployment, monitoring, and retraining to using event-driven pipelines and managed services. The architecture is model-agnostic, supporting diverse inference patterns through standardized interfaces, enabling rapid adaptation without infrastructure overhead. We demonstrate practical applicability through an industrial implementation for Harmonized System (HS) code prediction, a compliance-critical task where short, unstructured product descriptions are mapped to standardized codes used by customs authorities in global trade. Frequent updates and ambiguous descriptions make classification challenging, with errors causing shipment delays and financial losses. Our solution uses a custom text embedding encoder and multiple deep learning architectures, with Text-CNN achieving 98 percent accuracy on ground truth data. Beyond accuracy, the pipeline ensures reproducibility, auditability, and SLA adherence under variable loads via auto-scaling. A key feature is automated A/B testing, enabling dynamic model selection and safe promotion in production. Cost-efficiency drives model choice; while transformers may achieve similar accuracy, their long-term operational costs are significantly higher. Deterministic classification with predictable latency and explainability is prioritized, though the architecture remains extensible to transformer variants and LLM-based inference. The paper first introduces the deep learning architectures with simulations and model comparisons, then discusses industrialization through serverless architecture, demonstrating automated retraining, prediction, and validation of HS codes. This work provides a replicable blueprint for operationalizing ML using serverless architecture, enabling enterprises to scale while optimizing performance and economics.

AI.tldr: A model-agnostic, serverless MLOps framework enabling end-to-end ML lifecycle management with event-driven pipelines, automated retraining, and A/B testing, demonstrated on HS code prediction with a Text-CNN achieving high accuracy; emphasizes reproducibility, SLA adherence, and cost-efficiency over transformer-based approaches; provides a replicable blueprint for scalable ML deployment.
AI.motivation: Meet the need for scalable, auditable, low-latency ML in production across data ingestion, training, deployment, monitoring, and retraining, especially for compliance-critical tasks like HS code classification where data are short and ambiguous.
AI.method: Proposes a serverless, event-driven MLOps architecture with managed services and standardized interfaces; uses a custom text embedding encoder and multiple DL models (notably Text-CNN) with systematic evaluation; implements automated retraining, prediction validation, A/B testing, auto-scaling, and SLA guarantees.
AI.result: Text-CNN achieves 98% accuracy on ground truth HS code mapping; pipeline ensures reproducibility, auditability, and SLA adherence under variable loads; enables deterministic latency and explainability; demonstrates cost-aware model selection favoring lighter architectures when transformers are not significantly superior; supports safe production promotions and extensibility to transformers/LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Offers a replicable blueprint for operationalizing ML with serverless architecture, enabling scalable, economical deployment while remaining extensible to advanced models; positions serverless MLOps as a practical approach for industry-critical ML tasks.

title: MedClarify: An information-seeking AI agent for medical diagnosis with case-specific follow-up questions
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for diagnostic tasks in medicine. In clinical practice, the correct diagnosis can rarely be immediately inferred from the initial patient presentation alone. Rather, reaching a diagnosis often involves systematic history taking, during which clinicians reason over multiple potential conditions through iterative questioning to resolve uncertainty. This process requires considering differential diagnoses and actively excluding emergencies that demand immediate intervention. Yet, the ability of medical LLMs to generate informative follow-up questions and thus reason over differential diagnoses remains underexplored. Here, we introduce MedClarify, an AI agent for information-seeking that can generate follow-up questions for iterative reasoning to support diagnostic decision-making. Specifically, MedClarify computes a list of candidate diagnoses analogous to a differential diagnosis, and then proactively generates follow-up questions aimed at reducing diagnostic uncertainty. By selecting the question with the highest expected information gain, MedClarify enables targeted, uncertainty-aware reasoning to improve diagnostic performance. In our experiments, we first demonstrate the limitations of current LLMs in medical reasoning, which often yield multiple, similarly likely diagnoses, especially when patient cases are incomplete or relevant information for diagnosis is missing. We then show that our information-theoretic reasoning approach can generate effective follow-up questioning and thereby reduces diagnostic errors by ~27 percentage points (p.p.) compared to a standard single-shot LLM baseline. Altogether, MedClarify offers a path to improve medical LLMs through agentic information-seeking and to thus promote effective dialogues with medical LLMs that reflect the iterative and uncertain nature of real-world clinical reasoning.

AI.tldr: MedClarify is an AI agent that enhances diagnostic reasoning in medicine by generating follow-up questions to reduce uncertainty, using an information-gain approach to select the most informative query, achieving a ~27 percentage point reduction in diagnostic errors compared to a single-shot LLM baseline.
AI.motivation: Medical LLMs often struggle with iterative diagnostic reasoning; diagnosis usually requires sequential history-taking and ruling out emergencies. There is a need for agentic information-seeking to mimic real-world clinical reasoning and improve diagnostic accuracy.
AI.method: Identify candidate diagnoses analogous to differential diagnoses, generate follow-up questions aimed at reducing diagnostic uncertainty, and select the question with the highest expected information gain. Evaluate against a standard single-shot LLM baseline on diagnostic performance.
AI.result: The information-theoretic questioning approach reduces diagnostic errors by ~27 percentage points and demonstrates limitations of current LLMs when information is missing or incomplete. MedClarify improves diagnostic performance via targeted, uncertainty-aware questioning.
AI.conclusion: Agentic information-seeking enables more accurate and robust medical LLM reasoning, facilitating iterative dialogues that reflect real-world clinical uncertainty and potentially improving diagnostic decision-making.

title: Online Learning with Improving Agents: Multiclass, Budgeted Agents and Bandit Learners
summary: We investigate the recently introduced model of learning with improvements, where agents are allowed to make small changes to their feature values to be warranted a more desirable label. We extensively extend previously published results by providing combinatorial dimensions that characterize online learnability in this model, by analyzing the multiclass setup, learnability in a bandit feedback setup, modeling agents' cost for making improvements and more.

AI.tldr: Introduces and extends 'learning with improvements', identifying combinatorial dimensions that characterize online learnability and covering multiclass, bandit feedback, and agent-improvement costs.
AI.motivation: To understand how learnability changes when agents can tweak features to achieve desirable labels, and to generalize existing results to multiclass and bandit settings while incorporating cost considerations.
AI.method: Theoretically analyzes the model to derive combinatorial dimensions that characterize online learnability; extends the framework to multiclass and bandit feedback; models agents' costs for improvements; provides broader implications.
AI.result: Derivation of combinatorial dimensions that govern online learnability in the learning-with-improvements model; extended analyses for multiclass and bandit feedback; a cost-model for agent-improvements; broader scope compared to prior work.
AI.conclusion: The work substantially advances the theory of learning with actionable feature improvements, establishing foundational dimensions for online learnability and extending applicability to more complex feedback and cost scenarios.

title: Dataless Weight Disentanglement in Task Arithmetic via Kronecker-Factored Approximate Curvature
summary: Task Arithmetic yields a modular, scalable way to adapt foundation models. Combining multiple task vectors, however, can lead to cross-task interference, causing representation drift and degraded performance. Representation drift regularization provides a natural remedy to disentangle task vectors; however, existing approaches typically require external task data, conflicting with modularity and data availability constraints (e.g., privacy requirements). We propose a dataless approach by framing regularization against representation drift as a curvature matrix approximation problem. This allows us to leverage well-established techniques; in particular, we adopt Kronecker-Factored Approximate Curvature and obtain a practical regularizer that achieves state-of-the-art results in task addition and negation. Our method has constant complexity in the number of tasks and promotes robustness to task vector rescaling, eliminating the need for held-out tuning.

AI.tldr: Dataless regularization for task drift using Kronecker-Factored Approximate Curvature; achieves state-of-the-art in task addition/negation with constant complexity and robustness to task vector rescaling.
AI.motivation: Address cross-task interference and representation drift when combining multiple task vectors in Task Arithmetic; enable modular, privacy-preserving adaptation by avoiding external data.
AI.method: Model drift regularization as a curvature matrix approximation problem and apply K-FAC to derive a practical regularizer; design with constant complexity in number of tasks and resilience to vector rescaling; no held-out data required.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on task addition and negation; demonstrates dataless viability and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides a scalable, data-free regularizer for multi-task vector composition, preserving modularity and data privacy while maintaining strong performance.

title: i-PhysGaussian: Implicit Physical Simulation for 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Physical simulation predicts future states of objects based on material properties and external loads, enabling blueprints for both Industry and Engineering to conduct risk management. Current 3D reconstruction-based simulators typically rely on explicit, step-wise updates, which are sensitive to step time and suffer from rapid accuracy degradation under complicated scenarios, such as high-stiffness materials or quasi-static movement. To address this, we introduce i-PhysGaussian, a framework that couples 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) with an implicit Material Point Method (MPM) integrator. Unlike explicit methods, our solution obtains an end-of-step state by minimizing a momentum-balance residual through implicit Newton-type optimization with a GMRES solver. This formulation significantly reduces time-step sensitivity and ensures physical consistency. Our results demonstrate that i-PhysGaussian maintains stability at up to 20x larger time steps than explicit baselines, preserving structural coherence and smooth motion even in complex dynamic transitions.

AI.tldr: Implicitly integrate 3D Gaussian Splatting-based simulators by coupling 3DGS with an implicit MPM integrator that minimizes a momentum-balance residual, enabling Newton-type optimization with GMRES to achieve large-stability time stepping and better physical consistency compared to explicit methods.
AI.motivation: Current 3D reconstruction-based simulators rely on explicit, step-wise updates that are highly sensitive to time step and degrade accuracy with high-stiffness materials or quasi-static movements, limiting reliability for risk management in Industry and Engineering.
AI.method: Integrate 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) with an implicit Material Point Method (MPM) integrator. Compute the end-of-step state by minimizing a momentum-balance residual via an implicit Newton-type optimization using a GMRES solver.
AI.result: The i-PhysGaussian framework demonstrates reduced time-step sensitivity, maintaining stability up to 20x larger time steps than explicit baselines, while preserving structural coherence and smooth motion during complex dynamic transitions.
AI.conclusion: Implicit, momentum-balance-based optimization within a 3DGS framework improves physical consistency and stability, enabling significantly larger stable time steps compared to explicit methods.

title: Visual Model Checking: Graph-Based Inference of Visual Routines for Image Retrieval
summary: Information retrieval lies at the foundation of the modern digital industry. While natural language search has seen dramatic progress in recent years largely driven by embedding-based models and large-scale pretraining, the field still faces significant challenges. Specifically, queries that involve complex relationships, object compositions, or precise constraints such as identities, counts and proportions often remain unresolved or unreliable within current frameworks. In this paper, we propose a novel framework that integrates formal verification into deep learning-based image retrieval through a synergistic combination of graph-based verification methods and neural code generation. Our approach aims to support open-vocabulary natural language queries while producing results that are both trustworthy and verifiable. By grounding retrieval results in a system of formal reasoning, we move beyond the ambiguity and approximation that often characterize vector representations. Instead of accepting uncertainty as a given, our framework explicitly verifies each atomic truth in the user query against the retrieved content. This allows us to not only return matching results, but also to identify and mark which specific constraints are satisfied and which remain unmet, thereby offering a more transparent and accountable retrieval process while boosting the results of the most popular embedding-based approaches.

AI.tldr: Integrates formal verification with deep learning-based image retrieval to support open-vocabulary natural language queries, delivering verifiable, constraint-level results and improving transparency over pure embeddings.
AI.motivation: Embedding-based retrieval often fails to satisfy precise relational constraints (identities, counts, proportions) and complex queries; a verifiable framework is needed for trustworthy retrieval.
AI.method: A framework that combines graph-based verification with neural code generation to ground image retrieval in formal reasoning. It verifies each atomic truth in the user query against retrieved content, marks which constraints are satisfied or unmet, and can enhance embedding-based retrieval by providing verifiable explanations for results.
AI.result: Returns not only matching items but also a per-constraint satisfaction report, offering transparency and accountability; potentially boosts the performance of existing embedding-based methods by adding formal verification.
AI.conclusion: Grounding retrieval in formal reasoning yields trustworthy, verifiable open-vocabulary image retrieval, reducing ambiguity of vector representations and enabling a transparent, constraint-aware retrieval process.

title: TIFO: Time-Invariant Frequency Operator for Stationarity-Aware Representation Learning in Time Series
summary: Nonstationary time series forecasting suffers from the distribution shift issue due to the different distributions that produce the training and test data. Existing methods attempt to alleviate the dependence by, e.g., removing low-order moments from each individual sample. These solutions fail to capture the underlying time-evolving structure across samples and do not model the complex time structure. In this paper, we aim to address the distribution shift in the frequency space by considering all possible time structures. To this end, we propose a Time-Invariant Frequency Operator (TIFO), which learns stationarity-aware weights over the frequency spectrum across the entire dataset. The weight representation highlights stationary frequency components while suppressing non-stationary ones, thereby mitigating the distribution shift issue in time series. To justify our method, we show that the Fourier transform of time series data implicitly induces eigen-decomposition in the frequency space. TIFO is a plug-and-play approach that can be seamlessly integrated into various forecasting models. Experiments demonstrate our method achieves 18 top-1 and 6 top-2 results out of 28 forecasting settings. Notably, it yields 33.3% and 55.3% improvements in average MSE on the ETTm2 dataset. In addition, TIFO reduces computational costs by 60% -70% compared to baseline methods, demonstrating strong scalability across diverse forecasting models.

AI.tldr: TIFO learns stationary-frequency weights to mitigate distribution shift in nonstationary time-series forecasting, offering a plug-and-play solution with strong accuracy gains and lower computation.
AI.motivation: Nonstationary time series exhibit distribution shift between training and testing; existing approaches that remove low-order moments fail to capture evolving, cross-sample time structures.
AI.method: Introduce Time-Invariant Frequency Operator (TIFO) that learns weights over the frequency spectrum across the entire dataset to emphasize stationary components and suppress non-stationary ones; leverages the Fourier transform and its implicit eigen-decomposition in frequency space; designed as a plug-and-play module compatible with various forecasting models.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across 28 forecasting settings shows 18 top-1 and 6 top-2 results; on ETTm2 dataset, average MSE improvements of 33.3% and 55.3%; computational cost reduced by 60-70% relative to baselines, indicating scalability.
AI.conclusion: TIFO provides a scalable, versatile approach to address distribution shift in time-series forecasting by focusing on stationary frequency components, yielding strong empirical gains and computational efficiency.

title: Unified Latents (UL): How to train your latents
summary: We present Unified Latents (UL), a framework for learning latent representations that are jointly regularized by a diffusion prior and decoded by a diffusion model. By linking the encoder's output noise to the prior's minimum noise level, we obtain a simple training objective that provides a tight upper bound on the latent bitrate. On ImageNet-512, our approach achieves competitive FID of 1.4, with high reconstruction quality (PSNR) while requiring fewer training FLOPs than models trained on Stable Diffusion latents. On Kinetics-600, we set a new state-of-the-art FVD of 1.3.

AI.tldr: Unified Latents (UL) jointly regularizes latent encodings with a diffusion prior and decodes them via a diffusion model, yielding a tight latent bitrate bound and competitive or state-of-the-art generative metrics with lower compute.
AI.motivation: Improve latent representations for diffusion-based generation by aligning encoder noise with the prior's minimum noise, enabling an efficient training objective and tighter information bounds.
AI.method: Regularize latent encodings using a diffusion prior while decoding with a diffusion model; relate encoder noise to the prior's minimum noise to derive a training objective that upper-bounds latent bitrate; evaluate on ImageNet-512 and Kinetics-600.
AI.result: ImageNet-512: FID 1.4 with high PSNR and reduced training FLOPs compared to Stable Diffusion latents; Kinetics-600: state-of-the-art FVD of 1.3.
AI.conclusion: UL demonstrates that joint latent regularization with a diffusion prior and decoder can improve efficiency (lower FLOPs) while maintaining or enhancing perceptual and temporal metrics, achieving strong results on image and video benchmarks.

title: A Contrastive Variational AutoEncoder for NSCLC Survival Prediction with Missing Modalities
comment: Accepted at The 13th IEEE International Conference on Big Data (IEEE BigData 2025)
summary: Predicting survival outcomes for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) patients is challenging due to the different individual prognostic features. This task can benefit from the integration of whole-slide images, bulk transcriptomics, and DNA methylation, which offer complementary views of the patient's condition at diagnosis. However, real-world clinical datasets are often incomplete, with entire modalities missing for a significant fraction of patients. State-of-the-art models rely on available data to create patient-level representations or use generative models to infer missing modalities, but they lack robustness in cases of severe missingness. We propose a Multimodal Contrastive Variational AutoEncoder (MCVAE) to address this issue: modality-specific variational encoders capture the uncertainty in each data source, and a fusion bottleneck with learned gating mechanisms is introduced to normalize the contributions from present modalities. We propose a multi-task objective that combines survival loss and reconstruction loss to regularize patient representations, along with a cross-modal contrastive loss that enforces cross-modal alignment in the latent space. During training, we apply stochastic modality masking to improve the robustness to arbitrary missingness patterns. Extensive evaluations on the TCGA-LUAD (n=475) and TCGA-LUSC (n=446) datasets demonstrate the efficacy of our approach in predicting disease-specific survival (DSS) and its robustness to severe missingness scenarios compared to two state-of-the-art models. Finally, we bring some clarifications on multimodal integration by testing our model on all subsets of modalities, finding that integration is not always beneficial to the task.

AI.tldr: MCVAE for robust multimodal survival prediction in NSCLC under missing modalities, using modality-specific encoders, a learned fusion bottleneck with gating, a multi-task objective (survival + reconstruction), and a cross-modal contrastive loss with stochastic modality masking; validated on TCGA LUAD/LUSC, showing improved DSS prediction and highlighting that full modality integration is not always beneficial.
AI.motivation: Real-world multimodal cancer datasets often have missing modalities; robust fusion methods are needed to leverage available data without being brittle to missingness.
AI.method: Multimodal Contrastive Variational AutoEncoder (MCVAE) with modality-specific variational encoders, a fusion bottleneck with learned gating to normalize contributions, a multi-task objective combining survival loss and reconstruction loss, a cross-modal contrastive loss to align latent spaces, and stochastic modality masking during training to simulate missing modalities.
AI.result: Evaluations on TCGA-LUAD (n=475) and TCGA-LUSC (n=446) show improved disease-specific survival prediction and robustness to severe missingness compared to two state-of-the-art models; analysis across all modality subsets indicates that integrating all modalities is not always beneficial for the task.
AI.conclusion: MCVAE offers robust multimodal fusion under missing data and provides insight that full modality integration is not universally advantageous; modality-aware fusion and cross-modal alignment improve survival prediction in NSCLC.

title: VP-VAE: Rethinking Vector Quantization via Adaptive Vector Perturbation
summary: Vector Quantized Variational Autoencoders (VQ-VAEs) are fundamental to modern generative modeling, yet they often suffer from training instability and "codebook collapse" due to the inherent coupling of representation learning and discrete codebook optimization. In this paper, we propose VP-VAE (Vector Perturbation VAE), a novel paradigm that decouples representation learning from discretization by eliminating the need for an explicit codebook during training. Our key insight is that, from the neural network's viewpoint, performing quantization primarily manifests as injecting a structured perturbation in latent space. Accordingly, VP-VAE replaces the non-differentiable quantizer with distribution-consistent and scale-adaptive latent perturbations generated via Metropolis--Hastings sampling. This design enables stable training without a codebook while making the model robust to inference-time quantization error. Moreover, under the assumption of approximately uniform latent variables, we derive FSP (Finite Scalar Perturbation), a lightweight variant of VP-VAE that provides a unified theoretical explanation and a practical improvement for FSQ-style fixed quantizers. Extensive experiments on image and audio benchmarks demonstrate that VP-VAE and FSP improve reconstruction fidelity and achieve substantially more balanced token usage, while avoiding the instability inherent to coupled codebook training.

AI.tldr: Codebook-free VQ-VAE by Metropolis–Hastings latent perturbations; FSP variant for fixed quantizers; improves stability and token balance.
AI.motivation: Overcome training instability and codebook collapse due to coupling of representation learning and discrete codebook optimization in VQ-VAEs.
AI.method: Replace non-differentiable quantizer with distribution-consistent, scale-adaptive latent perturbations generated via Metropolis–Hastings sampling; eliminate explicit codebook during training. Introduce FSP (Finite Scalar Perturbation) as a lightweight variant under approximate uniformity to explain and improve FSQ-style fixed quantizers.
AI.result: Better reconstruction fidelity; more balanced token usage; stable training; robustness to inference-time quantization; effective on image and audio benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: VP-VAE provides a viable codebook-free alternative to VQ-VAEs with a practical FSP variant that theoretically and empirically addresses discretization challenges.

title: The Sound of Death: Deep Learning Reveals Vascular Damage from Carotid Ultrasound
summary: Cardiovascular diseases (CVDs) remain the leading cause of mortality worldwide, yet early risk detection is often limited by available diagnostics. Carotid ultrasound, a non-invasive and widely accessible modality, encodes rich structural and hemodynamic information that is largely untapped. Here, we present a machine learning (ML) framework that extracts clinically meaningful representations of vascular damage (VD) from carotid ultrasound videos, using hypertension as a weak proxy label. The model learns robust features that are biologically plausible, interpretable, and strongly associated with established cardiovascular risk factors, comorbidities, and laboratory measures. High VD stratifies individuals for myocardial infarction, cardiac death, and all-cause mortality, matching or outperforming conventional risk models such as SCORE2. Explainable AI analyses reveal that the model relies on vessel morphology and perivascular tissue characteristics, uncovering novel functional and anatomical signatures of vascular damage. This work demonstrates that routine carotid ultrasound contains far more prognostic information than previously recognized. Our approach provides a scalable, non-invasive, and cost-effective tool for population-wide cardiovascular risk assessment, enabling earlier and more personalized prevention strategies without reliance on laboratory tests or complex clinical inputs.

AI.tldr: ML framework extracts vascular damage from carotid ultrasound videos using hypertension as a weak proxy; prognostic for MI, cardiac death, and all-cause mortality; matches/outperforms SCORE2; interpretable vessel morphology and perivascular cues; scalable non-invasive risk assessment.
AI.motivation: CVD remains leading cause of death; early risk detection limited by available diagnostics; carotid ultrasound is non-invasive and widely available; need to leverage its rich information for risk stratification without heavy lab inputs.
AI.method: Weakly supervised ML on carotid ultrasound videos using hypertension as label proxy; learns robust representations of vascular damage; explainable AI to highlight morphology and perivascular tissue features; associates VD with established risk factors and lab measures; evaluates prognostic performance for MI, cardiac death, and all-cause mortality, comparing to SCORE2.
AI.result: VD stratification predicts adverse outcomes; performance on par with or better than SCORE2; interpretable features align with vessel structure and perivascular tissue; supports routine carotid ultrasound as a prognostic tool.
AI.conclusion: Routine carotid ultrasound contains substantial prognostic information for cardiovascular risk; the framework enables scalable, non-invasive, and cost-effective population-level risk assessment without requiring labs or complex clinical inputs.

title: A Privacy by Design Framework for Large Language Model-Based Applications for Children
summary: Children are increasingly using technologies powered by Artificial Intelligence (AI). However, there are growing concerns about privacy risks, particularly for children. Although existing privacy regulations require companies and organizations to implement protections, doing so can be challenging in practice. To address this challenge, this article proposes a framework based on Privacy-by-Design (PbD), which guides designers and developers to take on a proactive and risk-averse approach to technology design. Our framework includes principles from several privacy regulations, such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) from the European Union, the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA) from Canada, and the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) from the United States. We map these principles to various stages of applications that use Large Language Models (LLMs), including data collection, model training, operational monitoring, and ongoing validation. For each stage, we discuss the operational controls found in the recent academic literature to help AI service providers and developers reduce privacy risks while meeting legal standards. In addition, the framework includes design guidelines for children, drawing from the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC), the UK's Age-Appropriate Design Code (AADC), and recent academic research. To demonstrate how this framework can be applied in practice, we present a case study of an LLM-based educational tutor for children under 13. Through our analysis and the case study, we show that by using data protection strategies such as technical and organizational controls and making age-appropriate design decisions throughout the LLM life cycle, we can support the development of AI applications for children that provide privacy protections and comply with legal requirements.

AI.tldr: A Privacy-by-Design framework for child-focused AI using LLMs, integrating GDPR, PIPEDA, COPPA with UNCRC/AADC guidance, mapped to data collection, training, monitoring, and validation, illustrated by a case study showing how lifecycle-wide protections enhance privacy and regulatory compliance.
AI.motivation: Growing privacy risks for children in AI-enabled technologies and the complexity of meeting diverse regulations; the need for proactive, risk-aware design practices.
AI.method: Develop a PbD framework bridging multiple privacy regulations and child-rights guidelines; map principles to stages of LLM-based applications (data collection, model training, operational monitoring, ongoing validation); review operational controls from recent literature; formulate design guidelines for children; demonstrate with a case study of an LLM-based educational tutor for under-13s.
AI.result: The integrated approach shows that applying technical and organizational controls, along with age-appropriate design decisions throughout the LLM lifecycle, can reduce privacy risks and help meet legal requirements.
AI.conclusion: A practical framework for designing privacy-preserving, regulation-compliant child-oriented AI; supports implementation across the LLM lifecycle and invites future empirical validation and broader application across domains.

title: When More Experts Hurt: Underfitting in Multi-Expert Learning to Defer
summary: Learning to Defer (L2D) enables a classifier to abstain from predictions and defer to an expert, and has recently been extended to multi-expert settings. In this work, we show that multi-expert L2D is fundamentally more challenging than the single-expert case. With multiple experts, the classifier's underfitting becomes inherent, which seriously degrades prediction performance, whereas in the single-expert setting it arises only under specific conditions. We theoretically reveal that this stems from an intrinsic expert identifiability issue: learning which expert to trust from a diverse pool, a problem absent in the single-expert case and renders existing underfitting remedies failed. To tackle this issue, we propose PiCCE (Pick the Confident and Correct Expert), a surrogate-based method that adaptively identifies a reliable expert based on empirical evidence. PiCCE effectively reduces multi-expert L2D to a single-expert-like learning problem, thereby resolving multi expert underfitting. We further prove its statistical consistency and ability to recover class probabilities and expert accuracies. Extensive experiments across diverse settings, including real-world expert scenarios, validate our theoretical results and demonstrate improved performance.

AI.tldr: Multi-expert Learning to Defer (L2D) is harder than the single-expert case due to an intrinsic expert identifiability issue. The proposed PiCCE method selects a reliable expert via surrogate evidence, reducing the problem to a single-expert-like learning task, with theoretical consistency guarantees and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: In multi-expert L2D, the classifier’s underfitting becomes inherent because the model must learn which expert to trust from a diverse pool. This identifiability problem is absent in the single-expert setting, leading to degraded performance and ineffectiveness of existing remedies.
AI.method: PiCCE (Pick the Confident and Correct Expert) is a surrogate-based method that adaptively identifies a reliable expert using empirical evidence. By selecting a single reliable expert, PiCCE transforms the multi-expert L2D problem into a single-expert-like learning task, mitigating underfitting.
AI.result: The work reveals an intrinsic expert identifiability issue causing underfitting in multi-expert L2D. It proves that PiCCE reduces the problem to single-expert learning, and establishes statistical consistency along with the ability to recover class probabilities and expert accuracies. Extensive experiments across diverse settings, including real-world experts, validate the theoretical claims and show improved performance.
AI.conclusion: PiCCE effectively resolves multi-expert L2D underfitting by reliably picking the confident expert, yielding a robust, theoretically sound approach that generalizes to real-world scenarios.

title: WarpRec: Unifying Academic Rigor and Industrial Scale for Responsible, Reproducible, and Efficient Recommendation
summary: Innovation in Recommender Systems is currently impeded by a fractured ecosystem, where researchers must choose between the ease of in-memory experimentation and the costly, complex rewriting required for distributed industrial engines. To bridge this gap, we present WarpRec, a high-performance framework that eliminates this trade-off through a novel, backend-agnostic architecture. It includes 50+ state-of-the-art algorithms, 40 metrics, and 19 filtering and splitting strategies that seamlessly transition from local execution to distributed training and optimization. The framework enforces ecological responsibility by integrating CodeCarbon for real-time energy tracking, showing that scalability need not come at the cost of scientific integrity or sustainability. Furthermore, WarpRec anticipates the shift toward Agentic AI, leading Recommender Systems to evolve from static ranking engines into interactive tools within the Generative AI ecosystem. In summary, WarpRec not only bridges the gap between academia and industry but also can serve as the architectural backbone for the next generation of sustainable, agent-ready Recommender Systems. Code is available at https://github.com/sisinflab/warprec/

AI.tldr: WarpRec is a backend-agnostic, high-performance recommender framework unifying in-memory experimentation with distributed industrial engines, featuring 50+ algorithms, 40 metrics, 19 filtering/splitting strategies, backend-agnostic design, real-time CodeCarbon energy tracking, and an ecosystem-ready path toward agentic AI.
AI.motivation: The recommender-system research landscape is fragmented between quick local prototyping and costly, complex deployment on distributed engines. There is a need for a unified, scalable, and sustainable framework that preserves scientific integrity and eases transition to industry-scale systems.
AI.method: Develop a backend-agnostic architecture that supports local execution and seamless scaling to distributed training/optimization; integrate 50+ algorithms, 40 metrics, and 19 filtering/splitting strategies; embed CodeCarbon energy tracking; design with an eye toward integration with Generative AI and agentic interfaces.
AI.result: A comprehensive framework enabling seamless transition from local experimentation to distributed training/optimization, with built-in sustainability metrics and broad algorithm/metric coverage. Code is publicly available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: WarpRec has the potential to bridge academia and industry and can serve as the architectural backbone for sustainable, agent-ready Recommender Systems within the Generative AI ecosystem.

title: TimeOmni-VL: Unified Models for Time Series Understanding and Generation
summary: Recent time series modeling faces a sharp divide between numerical generation and semantic understanding, with research showing that generation models often rely on superficial pattern matching, while understanding-oriented models struggle with high-fidelity numerical output. Although unified multimodal models (UMMs) have bridged this gap in vision, their potential for time series remains untapped. We propose TimeOmni-VL, the first vision-centric framework that unifies time series understanding and generation through two key innovations: (1) Fidelity-preserving bidirectional mapping between time series and images (Bi-TSI), which advances Time Series-to-Image (TS2I) and Image-to-Time Series (I2TS) conversions to ensure near-lossless transformations. (2) Understanding-guided generation. We introduce TSUMM-Suite, a novel dataset consists of six understanding tasks rooted in time series analytics that are coupled with two generation tasks. With a calibrated Chain-of-Thought, TimeOmni-VL is the first to leverage time series understanding as an explicit control signal for high-fidelity generation. Experiments confirm that this unified approach significantly improves both semantic understanding and numerical precision, establishing a new frontier for multimodal time series modeling.

AI.tldr: TimeOmni-VL unifies time-series understanding and generation via fidelity-preserving bidirectional mappings (TS2I and I2TS) and understanding-guided generation; introduces TSUMM-Suite (six understanding tasks + two generation tasks); reports improvements in semantic accuracy and numerical fidelity.
AI.motivation: Bridge the long-standing gap between numerical generation and semantic understanding in time series; extend unified multimodal modeling from vision to time series, enabling near-lossless transformations and control-guided high-fidelity generation.
AI.method: Propose Bi-TSI for TS2I and I2TS with fidelity-preserving properties; integrate into TimeOmni-VL as a vision-centric framework; introduce TSUMM-Suite with six understanding tasks coupled with two generation tasks; employ calibrated Chain-of-Thought to use understanding as explicit control signals for generation.
AI.result: Experimental results show substantial gains in both semantic understanding and numerical precision, establishing a new frontier for multimodal time-series modeling.
AI.conclusion: First framework to unify time-series understanding and generation in a vision-centric multimodal setting; demonstrates feasibility and effectiveness of near-lossless transformations and understanding-guided generation to advance the field.

title: Pushing the Frontier of Black-Box LVLM Attacks via Fine-Grained Detail Targeting
summary: Black-box adversarial attacks on Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) are challenging due to missing gradients and complex multimodal boundaries. While prior state-of-the-art transfer-based approaches like M-Attack perform well using local crop-level matching between source and target images, we find this induces high-variance, nearly orthogonal gradients across iterations, violating coherent local alignment and destabilizing optimization. We attribute this to (i) ViT translation sensitivity that yields spike-like gradients and (ii) structural asymmetry between source and target crops. We reformulate local matching as an asymmetric expectation over source transformations and target semantics, and build a gradient-denoising upgrade to M-Attack. On the source side, Multi-Crop Alignment (MCA) averages gradients from multiple independently sampled local views per iteration to reduce variance. On the target side, Auxiliary Target Alignment (ATA) replaces aggressive target augmentation with a small auxiliary set from a semantically correlated distribution, producing a smoother, lower-variance target manifold. We further reinterpret momentum as Patch Momentum, replaying historical crop gradients; combined with a refined patch-size ensemble (PE+), this strengthens transferable directions. Together these modules form M-Attack-V2, a simple, modular enhancement over M-Attack that substantially improves transfer-based black-box attacks on frontier LVLMs: boosting success rates on Claude-4.0 from 8% to 30%, Gemini-2.5-Pro from 83% to 97%, and GPT-5 from 98% to 100%, outperforming prior black-box LVLM attacks. Code and data are publicly available at: https://github.com/vila-lab/M-Attack-V2.

AI.tldr: M-Attack-V2 is a modular enhancement to transfer-based black-box LVLM attacks that reduces gradient variance in local crop matching and uses auxiliary target alignment and patch momentum, boosting transfer success rates across frontier LVLMs, including Claude-4.0, Gemini-2.5-Pro, and GPT-5; code released.
AI.motivation: Identify and mitigate high gradient variance and misalignment in local crop-based black-box attacks on LVLMs due to ViT translation sensitivity and crop asymmetry; achieve more stable optimization and higher transferability.
AI.method: Reformulate local matching as an asymmetric expectation over source transformations and target semantics. Introduce MCA to average gradients from multiple source crops; ATA to use a small semantically correlated auxiliary target set instead of aggressive augmentation; reinterpret momentum as Patch Momentum using historical crop gradients; apply a refined patch-size ensemble (PE+) to strengthen transfer directions; combine into M-Attack-V2.
AI.result: Substantial gains in transfer success rates on frontier LVLMs: Claude-4.0 from 8% to 30%, Gemini-2.5-Pro from 83% to 97%, GPT-5 from 98% to 100%; outperforms previous black-box LVLM attacks; publicly released code and data.
AI.conclusion: M-Attack-V2 offers a lightweight, modular upgrade to existing transfer-based black-box LVLM attacks, delivering more stable optimization and higher success rates; practical impact on robustness evaluation and security research; potential generalization to other multimodal models.

title: Pareto Optimal Benchmarking of AI Models on ARM Cortex Processors for Sustainable Embedded Systems
summary: This work presents a practical benchmarking framework for optimizing artificial intelligence (AI) models on ARM Cortex processors (M0+, M4, M7), focusing on energy efficiency, accuracy, and resource utilization in embedded systems. Through the design of an automated test bench, we provide a systematic approach to evaluate across key performance indicators (KPIs) and identify optimal combinations of processor and AI model. The research highlights a nearlinear correlation between floating-point operations (FLOPs) and inference time, offering a reliable metric for estimating computational demands. Using Pareto analysis, we demonstrate how to balance trade-offs between energy consumption and model accuracy, ensuring that AI applications meet performance requirements without compromising sustainability. Key findings indicate that the M7 processor is ideal for short inference cycles, while the M4 processor offers better energy efficiency for longer inference tasks. The M0+ processor, while less efficient for complex AI models, remains suitable for simpler tasks. This work provides insights for developers, guiding them to design energy-efficient AI systems that deliver high performance in realworld applications.

AI.tldr: A practical benchmarking framework for optimizing AI on ARM Cortex-M0+/M4/M7 in embedded systems, using automated tests and Pareto analysis to balance energy, accuracy, and latency; FLOPs correlate with inference time; M7 best for short inferences, M4 for energy-efficient longer tasks, M0+ for simple tasks.
AI.motivation: Enable energy-aware deployment of AI on resource-constrained microcontrollers by providing systematic KPIs and a scalable benchmarking workflow.
AI.method: Design an automated test bench to evaluate multiple AI models across processors, measuring energy, latency, accuracy, and resource use; analyze FLOPs vs inference time; apply Pareto analysis to identify energy-accuracy trade-offs.
AI.result: Demonstrates near-linear FLOPs-inference-time relationship; identifies Pareto-optimal configurations; recommends M7 for short inferences, M4 for longer tasks, M0+ for simple tasks; provides practical guidelines for energy-efficient embedded AI design.
AI.conclusion: The framework supports developers in choosing processor-model pairs that meet performance and sustainability constraints in real-world embedded AI deployments.

title: Powering Up Zeroth-Order Training via Subspace Gradient Orthogonalization
summary: Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization provides a gradient-free alternative to first-order (FO) methods by estimating gradients via finite differences of function evaluations, and has recently emerged as a memory-efficient paradigm for fine-tuning large-scale models by avoiding backpropagation. However, ZO optimization has a fundamental tension between accuracy and query efficiency. In this work, we show that ZO optimization can be substantially improved by unifying two complementary principles: (i) a projection-based subspace view that reduces gradient estimation variance by exploiting the intrinsic low-rank structure of model updates, and (ii) Muon-style spectral optimization that applies gradient orthogonalization to extract informative spectral structure from noisy ZO gradients. These findings form a unified framework of subspace gradient orthogonalization, which we instantiate in a new method, ZO-Muon, admitting a natural interpretation as a low-rank Muon optimizer in the ZO setting. Extensive experiments on large language models (LLMs) and vision transformers (ViTs) demonstrate that ZO-Muon significantly accelerates convergence and achieves a win-win improvement in accuracy and query/runtime efficiency. Notably, compared to the popular MeZO baseline, ZO-Muon requires only 24.7% of the queries to reach the same SST-2 performance for LLM fine-tuning, and improves accuracy by 25.1% on ViT-B fine-tuning on CIFAR-100.

AI.tldr: ZO-Muon: a subspace gradient orthogonalization method for zeroth-order optimization that unifies projection-based subspace variance reduction with Muon-style spectral optimization, significantly accelerating convergence for fine-tuning LLMs and ViTs while reducing query counts, outperforming MeZO.
AI.motivation: ZO optimization trades off gradient estimation accuracy against query efficiency. By exploiting the intrinsic low-rank structure of model updates (projection-based subspace) and extracting informative spectral components from noisy ZO gradients (Muon-style optimization), the authors aim to substantially improve both convergence speed and accuracy with fewer queries.
AI.method: Proposes a unified framework called subspace gradient orthogonalization and instantiates it as ZO-Muon, a low-rank Muon optimizer in the ZO setting. Combines a projection-based subspace view to reduce gradient variance with gradient orthogonalization to extract informative spectral structure from ZO gradients.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on LLMs and ViTs show substantial acceleration and improved accuracy along with better query/runtime efficiency. Compared to MeZO, ZO-Muon uses 24.7% of the queries to reach the same SST-2 performance for LLM fine-tuning and improves ViT-B CIFAR-100 accuracy by 25.1%.
AI.conclusion: The subspace gradient orthogonalization framework yields meaningful gains in zeroth-order optimization for large-scale model fine-tuning, demonstrating strong practical utility and suggesting a promising direction for memory-efficient optimization in deep learning.

title: Enhancing Large Language Models (LLMs) for Telecom using Dynamic Knowledge Graphs and Explainable Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown strong potential across a variety of tasks, but their application in the telecom field remains challenging due to domain complexity, evolving standards, and specialized terminology. Therefore, general-domain LLMs may struggle to provide accurate and reliable outputs in this context, leading to increased hallucinations and reduced utility in telecom operations.To address these limitations, this work introduces KG-RAG-a novel framework that integrates knowledge graphs (KGs) with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to enhance LLMs for telecom-specific tasks. In particular, the KG provides a structured representation of domain knowledge derived from telecom standards and technical documents, while RAG enables dynamic retrieval of relevant facts to ground the model's outputs. Such a combination improves factual accuracy, reduces hallucination, and ensures compliance with telecom specifications.Experimental results across benchmark datasets demonstrate that KG-RAG outperforms both LLM-only and standard RAG baselines, e.g., KG-RAG achieves an average accuracy improvement of 14.3% over RAG and 21.6% over LLM-only models. These results highlight KG-RAG's effectiveness in producing accurate, reliable, and explainable outputs in complex telecom scenarios.

AI.tldr: KG-RAG fuses a telecom-specific knowledge graph with retrieval-augmented generation to ground LLM outputs, achieving higher factual accuracy and fewer hallucinations in telecom tasks; outperforms LLM-only and standard RAG baselines.
AI.motivation: Domain complexity in telecom: evolving standards and specialized terminology cause general LLMs to hallucinate or misstate specs; need reliable, standards-compliant outputs.
AI.method: Construct a knowledge graph from telecom standards and technical docs; integrate with retrieval-augmented generation to ground LLM outputs in retrieved facts; provides explainability.
AI.result: On benchmark datasets, KG-RAG improves average accuracy by 14.3% over RAG and 21.6% over LLM-only; claims to reduce hallucinations and improve compliance.
AI.conclusion: KG-RAG is effective for telecom scenarios, enabling accurate, reliable, and explainable outputs; suggests value of KG grounding for domain-specific LLM tasks.

title: In-Context Learning in Linear vs. Quadratic Attention Models: An Empirical Study on Regression Tasks
summary: Recent work has demonstrated that transformers and linear attention models can perform in-context learning (ICL) on simple function classes, such as linear regression. In this paper, we empirically study how these two attention mechanisms differ in their ICL behavior on the canonical linear-regression task of Garg et al. We evaluate learning quality (MSE), convergence, and generalization behavior of each architecture. We also analyze how increasing model depth affects ICL performance. Our results illustrate both the similarities and limitations of linear attention relative to quadratic attention in this setting.

AI.tldr: Empirical comparison of linear and quadratic attention for in-context learning (ICL) on a canonical linear-regression task; analyzes depth effects; finds similarities and notable limitations of linear attention relative to quadratic attention.
AI.motivation: Clarify how the choice of attention mechanism shapes ICL behavior on a simple, well-studied function-learning task to inform scalable model design.
AI.method: Empirical study on the Garg et al. linear-regression ICL task. Metrics assessed include learning quality (MSE), convergence dynamics, and generalization; experiments vary model depth to observe its impact on ICL.
AI.result: Linear attention reproduces some ICL patterns seen with quadratic attention but exhibits limitations; depth modulates performance, improving some aspects but not fully bridging gaps with quadratic attention.
AI.conclusion: Linear attention can support ICL on linear regression but does not fully substitute quadratic attention. Depth scaling helps but does not eliminate the differences; results delineate where linear attention is viable vs. where quadratic attention remains advantageous.

title: Evaluating Chain-of-Thought Reasoning through Reusability and Verifiability
summary: In multi-agent IR pipelines for tasks such as search and ranking, LLM-based agents exchange intermediate reasoning in terms of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) with each other. Current CoT evaluation narrowly focuses on target task accuracy. However, this metric fails to assess the quality or utility of the reasoning process itself. To address this limitation, we introduce two novel measures: reusability and verifiability. We decouple CoT generation from execution using a Thinker-Executor framework. Reusability measures how easily an Executor can reuse the Thinker's CoT. Verifiability measures how frequently an Executor can match the Thinker's answer using the CoT. We evaluated four Thinker models against a committee of ten Executor models across five benchmarks. Our results reveal that reusability and verifiability do not correlate with standard accuracy, exposing a blind spot in current accuracy-based leaderboards for reasoning capability. Surprisingly, we find that CoTs from specialized reasoning models are not consistently more reusable or verifiable than those from general-purpose LLMs like Llama and Gemma.

AI.tldr: Proposes two CoT metrics, reusability and verifiability, in a Thinker-Executor framework for multi-agent IR; evaluation shows these metrics diverge from standard accuracy and are not always aligned with specialized vs general-purpose LLMs.
AI.motivation: Current CoT evaluation focuses on task accuracy, which fails to capture the intrinsic quality and utility of the reasoning process in multi-agent pipelines; new metrics are needed to assess reuse of reasoning and verifiability of outcomes.
AI.method: Introduce Thinker-Executor framework that decouples CoT generation (Thinker) from execution (Executor).
