Despite significant progress in post-hoc explanation methods for neural networks, many remain heuristic and lack provable guarantees. A key approach for obtaining explanations with provable guarantees is by identifying a cardinally-minimal subset of input features which by itself is provably sufficient to determine the prediction. However, for standard neural networks, this task is often computationally infeasible, as it demands a worst-case exponential number of verification queries in the number of input features, each of which is NP-hard.
  In this work, we show that for Neural Additive Models (NAMs), a recent and more interpretable neural network family, we can efficiently generate explanations with such guarantees. We present a new model-specific algorithm for NAMs that generates provably cardinally-minimal explanations using only a logarithmic number of verification queries
  in the number of input features, after a parallelized preprocessing step with logarithmic runtime in the required precision is applied to each small univariate NAM component.
  Our algorithm not only makes the task of obtaining cardinally-minimal explanations feasible, but even outperforms existing algorithms designed to find the relaxed variant of subset-minimal explanations - which may be larger and less informative but easier to compute - despite our algorithm solving a much more difficult task.
  Our experiments demonstrate that, compared to previous algorithms, our approach provides provably smaller explanations than existing works and substantially reduces the computation time. Moreover, we show that our generated provable explanations offer benefits that are unattainable by standard sampling-based techniques typically used to interpret NAMs.

AI.tldr: NAM-specific algorithm yields provably cardinally-minimal explanations with logarithmic verification queries, after a preprocessing step; significantly faster and more informative than prior, relaxed subset-minimal methods and sampling-based explanations.
AI.motivation: To provide explanations for neural predictions with provable guarantees, overcoming NP-hardness in general networks by exploiting Neural Additive Models (NAMs).
AI.method: Developed a model-specific algorithm for NAMs that achieves provably cardinally-minimal explanations using only a logarithmic number of verification queries in the number of input features; includes a parallelizable preprocessing step with logarithmic runtime per univariate NAM component.
AI.result: The algorithm yields provably smaller explanations than existing methods, greatly reduces computation time, and delivers benefits beyond sampling-based approaches used for NAMs.
AI.conclusion: Provable, minimal explanations are feasible for NAMs, outperforming relaxed methods; the approach enhances interpretability with guarantees and may influence future literature on model-specific explainability bounds.

title: Position: Evaluation of ECG Representations Must Be Fixed
summary: This position paper argues that current benchmarking practice in 12-lead ECG representation learning must be fixed to ensure progress is reliable and aligned with clinically meaningful objectives. The field has largely converged on three public multi-label benchmarks (PTB-XL, CPSC2018, CSN) dominated by arrhythmia and waveform-morphology labels, even though the ECG is known to encode substantially broader clinical information. We argue that downstream evaluation should expand to include an assessment of structural heart disease and patient-level forecasting, in addition to other evolving ECG-related endpoints, as relevant clinical targets. Next, we outline evaluation best practices for multi-label, imbalanced settings, and show that when they are applied, the literature's current conclusion about which representations perform best is altered. Furthermore, we demonstrate the surprising result that a randomly initialized encoder with linear evaluation matches state-of-the-art pre-training on many tasks. This motivates the use of a random encoder as a reasonable baseline model. We substantiate our observations with an empirical evaluation of three representative ECG pre-training approaches across six evaluation settings: the three standard benchmarks, a structural disease dataset, hemodynamic inference, and patient forecasting.

AI.tldr: Benchmarking in ECG representation learning is biased toward limited multi-label tasks; broader, clinically meaningful evaluations are needed. Random encoders can match supervised pretraining, implying strong baselines. Six-task empirical study across benchmarks and real-world targets shows task-dependent results.
AI.motivation: ECG representations should capture broader clinical information beyond arrhythmia/morphology labels. Current public benchmarks (PTB-XL, CPSC2018, CSN) may not reflect real-world clinical objectives.
AI.method: Propose best practices for multi-label, imbalanced evaluation. Empirically compare three ECG pre-training approaches across six settings: standard benchmarks (PTB-XL, CPSC2018, CSN) plus a structural disease dataset, hemodynamic inference, and patient forecasting; include a randomly initialized encoder with linear evaluation as a baseline.
AI.result: Rigorous evaluation alters conclusions about which representations are best; a randomly initialized encoder often matches state-of-the-art pre-training on many tasks; three pretraining methods show varying performance across tasks; random baseline is a strong comparator.
AI.conclusion: Expand evaluation targets to include structural heart disease and patient-level forecasting; adopt robust evaluation protocols and random baselines; align benchmarking with clinically meaningful endpoints to drive reliable progress in ECG representation learning.

title: MASPO: Unifying Gradient Utilization, Probability Mass, and Signal Reliability for Robust and Sample-Efficient LLM Reasoning
summary: Existing Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) algorithms, such as GRPO, rely on rigid, uniform, and symmetric trust region mechanisms that are fundamentally misaligned with the complex optimization dynamics of Large Language Models (LLMs). In this paper, we identify three critical challenges in these methods: (1) inefficient gradient utilization caused by the binary cutoff of hard clipping, (2) insensitive probability mass arising from uniform ratio constraints that ignore the token distribution, and (3) asymmetric signal reliability stemming from the disparate credit assignment ambiguity between positive and negative samples. To bridge these gaps, we propose Mass-Adaptive Soft Policy Optimization (MASPO), a unified framework designed to harmonize these three dimensions. MASPO integrates a differentiable soft Gaussian gating to maximize gradient utility, a mass-adaptive limiter to balance exploration across the probability spectrum, and an asymmetric risk controller to align update magnitudes with signal confidence. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that MASPO serves as a robust, all-in-one RLVR solution, significantly outperforming strong baselines. Our code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ma1/README.md.

AI.tldr: MASPO is a unified RLVR framework for LLMs that addresses three core issues in existing methods—inefficient gradient use, skewed probability mass due to uniform ratio constraints, and asymmetric signal reliability in credit assignment—by introducing a differentiable soft Gaussian gate, a mass-adaptive limiter, and an asymmetric risk controller, yielding superior performance over strong baselines; code available at the provided URL.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR algorithms (e.g., GRPO) use rigid, uniform, symmetric trust-region mechanisms that do not align with the complex optimization dynamics of large language models, leading to inefficient gradients, misbalanced exploration, and unreliable credit assignment.
AI.method: MASPO integrates three components: (1) differentiable soft Gaussian gating to maximize gradient utility; (2) mass-adaptive limiter to balance exploration across the probability spectrum; (3) asymmetric risk controller to align update magnitudes with signal confidence, forming a unified MASPO framework.
AI.result: Comprehensive evaluations show that MASPO robustly improves performance, significantly outperforming strong baselines across tasks; code is provided at the referenced URL.
AI.conclusion: MASPO offers an all-in-one RLVR solution tailored for LLM optimization, addressing key deficiencies of prior methods and delivering enhanced robustness and performance; the approach is supported by empirical results and available code.

title: A Theoretical Framework for Modular Learning of Robust Generative Models
summary: Training large-scale generative models is resource-intensive and relies heavily on heuristic dataset weighting. We address two fundamental questions: Can we train Large Language Models (LLMs) modularly-combining small, domain-specific experts to match monolithic performance-and can we do so robustly for any data mixture, eliminating heuristic tuning? We present a theoretical framework for modular generative modeling where a set of pre-trained experts are combined via a gating mechanism. We define the space of normalized gating functions, $G_{1}$, and formulate the problem as a minimax game to find a single robust gate that minimizes divergence to the worst-case data mixture. We prove the existence of such a robust gate using Kakutani's fixed-point theorem and show that modularity acts as a strong regularizer, with generalization bounds scaling with the lightweight gate's complexity. Furthermore, we prove that this modular approach can theoretically outperform models retrained on aggregate data, with the gap characterized by the Jensen-Shannon Divergence. Finally, we introduce a scalable Stochastic Primal-Dual algorithm and a Structural Distillation method for efficient inference. Empirical results on synthetic and real-world datasets confirm that our modular architecture effectively mitigates gradient conflict and can robustly outperform monolithic baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes a modular gating-based framework to combine pre-trained experts for LLMs, with a minimax objective ensuring robustness to any data mixture; proves existence of a robust gate and shows modularity acts as a strong regularizer, plus scalable algorithms and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Training large generative models is resource-intensive and relies on heuristic dataset weighting; aim to achieve robust performance across arbitrary data mixtures without tuning.
AI.method: Define normalized gating function space G1; formulate minimax problem to minimize worst-case divergence; prove robust gate exists using Kakutani; show modularity regularizes; develop stochastic primal-dual algorithm and structural distillation for inference.
AI.result: Theoretical results show existence of a robust gate; generalization bounds scale with gate complexity; modular approach can surpass retraining on aggregate data, gap characterized by Jensen-Shannon divergence; empirical results show reduced gradient conflict and robust outperformance.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, robust modular framework for combining experts outperforms monolithic baselines with provable guarantees and practical inference techniques.

title: Revisiting Weight Regularization for Low-Rank Continual Learning
summary: Continual Learning (CL) with large-scale pre-trained models (PTMs) has recently gained wide attention, shifting the focus from training from scratch to continually adapting PTMs. This has given rise to a promising paradigm: parameter-efficient continual learning (PECL), where task interference is typically mitigated by assigning a task-specific module during training, such as low-rank adapters. However, weight regularization techniques, such as Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC)-a key strategy in CL-remain underexplored in this new paradigm. In this paper, we revisit weight regularization in low-rank CL as a new perspective for mitigating task interference in PECL. Unlike existing low-rank CL methods, we mitigate task interference by regularizing a shared low-rank update through EWC, thereby keeping the storage requirement and inference costs constant regardless of the number of tasks. Our proposed method EWC-LoRA leverages a low-rank representation to estimate parameter importance over the full-dimensional space. This design offers a practical, computational- and memory-efficient solution for CL with PTMs, and provides insights that may inform the broader application of regularization techniques within PECL. Extensive experiments on various benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of EWC-LoRA, achieving a stability-plasticity trade-off superior to existing low-rank CL approaches. These results indicate that, even under low-rank parameterizations, weight regularization remains an effective mechanism for mitigating task interference. Code is available at: https://github.com/yaoyz96/low-rank-cl.

AI.tldr: A new PECL approach (EWC-LoRA) uses Elastic Weight Consolidation to regularize a shared, low-rank update (LoRA) for continual learning with large pre-trained models, maintaining constant storage and inference costs while mitigating task interference.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of task interference in parameter-efficient continual learning (PECL) with PTMs, where many task-specific adapters can inflate storage; explore whether weight regularization (EWC) can be effective when applied to a shared low-rank update.
AI.method: Introduce EWC-LoRA: regularize a low-rank parameter update shared across tasks using EWC to estimate importance in full-dimensional space; apply to continual learning with PTMs using adapters like LoRA; compare against existing low-rank CL methods on standard benchmarks; provide code.
AI.result: Achieves a better stability-plasticity trade-off than existing low-rank CL methods across multiple benchmarks, showing that weight regularization remains effective for mitigating task interference even with low-rank parameterizations.
AI.conclusion: Regularization-based approaches (EWC) can be effectively integrated with low-rank, parameter-efficient CL to maintain performance across many tasks without increasing storage or inference costs; this insight informs broader use of regularization in PECL.

title: Be Wary of Your Time Series Preprocessing
comment: Accepted at the AI4TS workshop at AAAI-26
summary: Normalization and scaling are fundamental preprocessing steps in time series modeling, yet their role in Transformer-based models remains underexplored from a theoretical perspective. In this work, we present the first formal analysis of how different normalization strategies, specifically instance-based and global scaling, impact the expressivity of Transformer-based architectures for time series representation learning. We propose a novel expressivity framework tailored to time series, which quantifies a model's ability to distinguish between similar and dissimilar inputs in the representation space. Using this framework, we derive theoretical bounds for two widely used normalization methods: Standard and Min-Max scaling. Our analysis reveals that the choice of normalization strategy can significantly influence the model's representational capacity, depending on the task and data characteristics. We complement our theory with empirical validation on classification and forecasting benchmarks using multiple Transformer-based models. Our results show that no single normalization method consistently outperforms others, and in some cases, omitting normalization entirely leads to superior performance. These findings highlight the critical role of preprocessing in time series learning and motivate the need for more principled normalization strategies tailored to specific tasks and datasets.

AI.tldr: Transformers for time series: normalization affects expressivity; the paper introduces a time-series expressivity framework, derives bounds for Standard and Min-Max scaling, and finds no universal winner across tasks; in some cases, no normalization is best.
AI.motivation: There is a theoretical gap in understanding how normalization affects the expressivity of Transformer-based time-series models; quantifying the discriminability of representations under different preprocessing is needed.
AI.method: Propose a time-series–tailored expressivity framework to measure representation distinguishability; derive theoretical bounds for Standard and Min-Max normalization; compare with instance-based/global scaling; validate with classification and forecasting benchmarks using multiple Transformer architectures.
AI.result: Normalization strategy materially impacts representational capacity; no single normalization consistently dominates across tasks; in some scenarios, omitting normalization yields the best performance.
AI.conclusion: Preprocessing is critical in time-series learning; results motivate developing principled, task- and data-specific normalization strategies rather than a one-size-fits-all approach.

title: Canonicalizing Multimodal Contrastive Representation Learning
summary:
As models and data scale, independently trained networks often induce analogous notions of similarity. But, matching similarities is weaker than establishing an explicit correspondence between the representation spaces, especially for multimodal models, where consistency must hold not only within each modality, but also for the learned image-text coupling. We therefore ask: given two independently trained multimodal contrastive models (with encoders $(f, g)$ and $(\widetilde{f},\widetilde{g})$) -- trained on different distributions and with different architectures -- does a systematic geometric relationship exist between their embedding spaces? If so, what form does it take, and does it hold uniformly across modalities? In this work, we show that across model families such as CLIP, SigLIP, and FLAVA, this geometric relationship is well approximated by an orthogonal map (up to a global mean shift), i.e., there exists an orthogonal map $Q$ where $Q^\top Q = I$ such that $\widetilde{f}(x)\approx Q f(x)$ for paired images $x$. Strikingly, the same $Q$ simultaneously aligns the text encoders i.e., $\widetilde{g}(y)\approx Q g(y)$ for texts $y$. Theoretically, we prove that if the multimodal kernel agrees across models on a small anchor set i.e. $\langle f(x), g(y)\rangle \approx \langle \widetilde{f}(x), \widetilde{g}(y)\rangle$, then the two models must be related by a single orthogonal map $Q$ and the same $Q$ maps images and text across models. More broadly, this finding enables backward-compatible model upgrades, avoiding costly re-embedding, and has implications for the privacy of learned representations.
  Our project page: https://canonical-multimodal.github.io/

AI.tldr: Across independently trained multimodal contrastive models (e.g., CLIP, SigLIP, FLAVA), the embeddings of images and texts can be aligned by a single orthogonal transformation Q (up to a mean shift). The same Q aligns both modalities, enabling backward-compatible upgrades and raising privacy considerations. Theoretical results show that if cross-modal inner products match on a small anchor set, the two models are related by an orthogonal map that applies to both image and text encodings.
AI.motivation: To understand whether independently trained multimodal models learn embedding spaces that are geometrically aligned, enabling cross-model compatibility, efficient upgrades, and insight into the relationship between image-text representations.
AI.method: Empirical analysis across multiple model families (CLIP, SigLIP, FLAVA) to test whether an orthogonal map can align the embeddings from two independently trained models. The authors show that there exists Q with Q^TQ = I such that tilde f(x) ≈ Q f(x) and tilde g(y) ≈ Q g(y) for paired data. They provide a theoretical result: if the multimodal kernels are close on an anchor set, then a single orthogonal map exists that aligns both modalities.
AI.result: The embeddings from different models are well approximated by a shared orthogonal transformation, up to a global mean shift, consistently for both image and text encoders. The proposed Q can align images and texts across models, backed by theoretical guarantees under small anchor-set kernel agreement. This holds across CLIP, SigLIP, and FLAVA.
AI.conclusion: This orthogonal alignment enables backward-compatible model upgrades without re-embedding, and has privacy implications for learned representations. The work provides a principled geometric relation between independently trained multimodal models and suggests practical benefits and potential risks.

title: Asymptotic Smoothing of the Lipschitz Loss Landscape in Overparameterized One-Hidden-Layer ReLU Networks
summary: We study the topology of the loss landscape of one-hidden-layer ReLU networks under overparameterization. On the theory side, we (i) prove that for convex $L$-Lipschitz losses with an $\ell_1$-regularized second layer, every pair of models at the same loss level can be connected by a continuous path within an arbitrarily small loss increase $ε$ (extending a known result for the quadratic loss); (ii) obtain an asymptotic upper bound on the energy gap $ε$ between local and global minima that vanishes as the width $m$ grows, implying that the landscape flattens and sublevel sets become connected in the limit. Empirically, on a synthetic Moons dataset and on the Wisconsin Breast Cancer dataset, we measure pairwise energy gaps via Dynamic String Sampling (DSS) and find that wider networks exhibit smaller gaps; in particular, a permutation test on the maximum gap yields $p_{perm}=0$, indicating a clear reduction in the barrier height.

AI.tldr: Topology of loss landscapes for overparameterized one-hidden-layer ReLU networks: connectivity along loss-epsilon paths for convex Lipschitz losses with L1 second-layer regularization; energy-gap between local/global minima vanishes with width; empirical DSS confirms smaller gaps for wider networks (p=0 in permutation test).
AI.motivation: Understand optimization landscape in overparameterized neural networks beyond quadratic losses, to explain why training succeeds and why wide nets generalize; extend known quadratic-loss connectivity results to convex Lipschitz losses with L1-regularized second layer; quantify energy barriers as width grows.
AI.method: Theoretical: prove that for convex L-Lipschitz losses with an L1-regularized second layer, any two models sharing the same loss can be connected by a continuous path with arbitrarily small loss increase ε; derive an asymptotic upper bound on the energy gap between local and global minima that vanishes as width m increases. Empirical: use Dynamic String Sampling (DSS) to measure pairwise energy gaps on synthetic Moons dataset and Wisconsin Breast Cancer dataset; perform permutation tests on the maximum gap.
AI.result: Theoretically, establishes path-connectedness of level sets with small ε and a vanishing energy gap as width grows; empirically, broader networks exhibit smaller energy gaps, with DSS revealing reduced barrier heights; permutation test yields p_perm = 0 for the maximum gap, indicating a significant reduction.
AI.conclusion: Loss landscapes of wide, overparameterized one-hidden-layer ReLU networks become progressively flatter and more connected as width increases, aligning with empirical success of large networks. The results generalize prior quadratic-loss connectivity to broader convex Lipschitz losses with L1 regularization, and provide empirical corroboration via DSS.

title: Towards Anytime-Valid Statistical Watermarking
summary: The proliferation of Large Language Models (LLMs) necessitates efficient mechanisms to distinguish machine-generated content from human text. While statistical watermarking has emerged as a promising solution, existing methods suffer from two critical limitations: the lack of a principled approach for selecting sampling distributions and the reliance on fixed-horizon hypothesis testing, which precludes valid early stopping. In this paper, we bridge this gap by developing the first e-value-based watermarking framework, Anchored E-Watermarking, that unifies optimal sampling with anytime-valid inference. Unlike traditional approaches where optional stopping invalidates Type-I error guarantees, our framework enables valid, anytime-inference by constructing a test supermartingale for the detection process. By leveraging an anchor distribution to approximate the target model, we characterize the optimal e-value with respect to the worst-case log-growth rate and derive the optimal expected stopping time. Our theoretical claims are substantiated by simulations and evaluations on established benchmarks, showing that our framework can significantly enhance sample efficiency, reducing the average token budget required for detection by 13-15% relative to state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: First e-value-based, anytime-valid watermarking framework (Anchored E-Watermarking) that unifies optimal sampling with adaptive stopping for detecting machine-generated text, achieving 13-15% token-budget savings vs baselines.
AI.motivation: Address two key limitations of existing statistical watermarking: (i) absence of a principled method to choose sampling distributions, and (ii) reliance on fixed-horizon hypothesis testing which prevents valid early stopping.
AI.method: Develops an e-value-based detection framework by constructing a test supermartingale. Introduces an anchor distribution to approximate the target model, derives the optimal e-value with respect to the worst-case log-growth rate, and characterizes the optimal expected stopping time.
AI.result: Simulation and benchmark evaluations indicate significantly enhanced sample efficiency, reducing the average token budget required for detection by 13-15% compared with state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: Presents Anchored E-Watermarking, a principled framework that unifies optimal sampling with anytime-valid inference for watermarking LLM outputs, achieving improved detection efficiency while maintaining valid Type-I error control.

title: Guarding the Middle: Protecting Intermediate Representations in Federated Split Learning
comment: Accepted for Publication in IEEE International Conference on Big Data (IEEE BigData) 2025
summary: Big data scenarios, where massive, heterogeneous datasets are distributed across clients, demand scalable, privacy-preserving learning methods. Federated learning (FL) enables decentralized training of machine learning (ML) models across clients without data centralization. Decentralized training, however, introduces a computational burden on client devices. U-shaped federated split learning (UFSL) offloads a fraction of the client computation to the server while keeping both data and labels on the clients' side. However, the intermediate representations (i.e., smashed data) shared by clients with the server are prone to exposing clients' private data. To reduce exposure of client data through intermediate data representations, this work proposes k-anonymous differentially private UFSL (KD-UFSL), which leverages privacy-enhancing techniques such as microaggregation and differential privacy to minimize data leakage from the smashed data transferred to the server. We first demonstrate that an adversary can access private client data from intermediate representations via a data-reconstruction attack, and then present a privacy-enhancing solution, KD-UFSL, to mitigate this risk. Our experiments indicate that, alongside increasing the mean squared error between the actual and reconstructed images by up to 50% in some cases, KD-UFSL also decreases the structural similarity between them by up to 40% on four benchmarking datasets. More importantly, KD-UFSL improves privacy while preserving the utility of the global model. This highlights its suitability for large-scale big data applications where privacy and utility must be balanced.

AI.tldr: KD-UFSL proposes a privacy-enhanced UFSL approach that uses k-anonymity via microaggregation and differential privacy to reduce leakage from intermediate smashed data while maintaining global model utility; experiments show higher reconstruction error and lower similarity, across four datasets, indicating improved privacy with preserved utility.
AI.motivation: To address privacy risks from intermediate representations in U-shaped federated split learning (UFSL), where smashed data exchanged with the server can allow data reconstruction, necessitating privacy-preserving mechanisms without sacrificing model performance.
AI.method: Apply a privacy-enhancing pipeline combining k-anonymous microaggregation and differential privacy to the smashed data before server interaction in UFSL (KD-UFSL). This reduces data leakage via intermediate representations while enabling decentralized training.
AI.result: Empirical results show increased reconstruction error (mean squared error up to 50%) and reduced structural similarity (up to 40% lower SSIM) between actual and reconstructed data, on four benchmarking datasets, with preserved or improved global model utility.
AI.conclusion: KD-UFSL effectively mitigates privacy leakage in UFSL by integrating microaggregation-based k-anonymity with differential privacy, achieving a favorable privacy-utility balance suitable for large-scale big data applications.

title: Stable Asynchrony: Variance-Controlled Off-Policy RL for LLMs
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) is widely used to improve large language models on reasoning tasks, and asynchronous RL training is attractive because it increases end-to-end throughput. However, for widely adopted critic-free policy-gradient methods such as REINFORCE and GRPO, high asynchrony makes the policy-gradient estimator markedly $\textbf{higher variance}$: training on stale rollouts creates heavy-tailed importance ratios, causing a small fraction of samples to dominate updates. This amplification makes gradients noisy and learning unstable relative to matched on-policy training. Across math and general reasoning benchmarks, we find collapse is reliably predicted by effective sample size (ESS) and unstable gradient norms. Motivated by this diagnosis, we propose $\textbf{V}$ariance $\textbf{C}$ontrolled $\textbf{P}$olicy $\textbf{O}$ptimization ($\textbf{VCPO}$), a general stabilization method for REINFORCE/GRPO-style algorithms that (i) scales learning rate based on effective sample size to dampen unreliable updates, and (ii) applies a closed-form minimum-variance baseline for the off-policy setting, avoiding an auxiliary value model and adding minimal overhead. Empirically, VCPO substantially improves robustness for asynchronous training across math, general reasoning, and tool-use tasks, outperforming a broad suite of baselines spanning masking/clipping stabilizers and algorithmic variants. This reduces long-context, multi-turn training time by 2.5$\times$ while matching synchronous performance, demonstrating that explicit control of policy-gradient variance is key for reliable asynchronous RL at scale.

AI.tldr: Variance-controlled policy optimization (VCPO) stabilizes asynchronous REINFORCE/GRPO-style training by explicit variance control: adaptive learning-rate scaling via effective sample size (ESS) and a closed-form minimum-variance baseline for off-policy updates. It improves robustness and throughput, achieving synchronous performance with 2.5x speedup on long-context tasks.
AI.motivation: In highly asynchronous critic-free policy-gradient training, stale rollouts induce heavy-tailed importance ratios, causing high variance, noisy gradients, and unstable learning. Effective sample size (ESS) and gradient norms reliably predict collapse across math and general reasoning tasks; a general stabilization method is needed.
AI.method: VCPO introduces two components: (i) adaptive learning-rate scaling based on ESS to dampen unreliable updates from rare, high-variance samples; (ii) a closed-form minimum-variance baseline for the off-policy setting that eliminates the need for an auxiliary value model and incurs minimal overhead.
AI.result: Empirically, VCPO substantially improves robustness across math, general reasoning, and tool-use tasks, outperforming a broad range of baselines (masking/clipping stabilizers, and algorithmic variants). It reduces long-context, multi-turn training time by 2.5× while matching synchronous performance.
AI.conclusion: Explicit variance control is key for reliable asynchronous RL at scale; VCPO provides a general, lightweight stabilization method for REINFORCE/GRPO-style algorithms that enhances robustness and throughput without extra modeling overhead.

title: Catastrophic Forgetting Resilient One-Shot Incremental Federated Learning
comment: Accepted for publication in the IEEE International Conference on Big Data (IEEE BigData) 2025
summary: Modern big-data systems generate massive, heterogeneous, and geographically dispersed streams that are large-scale and privacy-sensitive, making centralization challenging. While federated learning (FL) provides a privacy-enhancing training mechanism, it assumes a static data flow and learns a collaborative model over multiple rounds, making learning with \textit{incremental} data challenging in limited-communication scenarios. This paper presents One-Shot Incremental Federated Learning (OSI-FL), the first FL framework that addresses the dual challenges of communication overhead and catastrophic forgetting. OSI-FL communicates category-specific embeddings, devised by a frozen vision-language model (VLM) from each client in a single communication round, which a pre-trained diffusion model at the server uses to synthesize new data similar to the client's data distribution. The synthesized samples are used on the server for training. However, two challenges still persist: i) tasks arriving incrementally need to retrain the global model, and ii) as future tasks arrive, retraining the model introduces catastrophic forgetting. To this end, we augment training with Selective Sample Retention (SSR), which identifies and retains the top-p most informative samples per category and task pair based on sample loss. SSR bounds forgetting by ensuring that representative retained samples are incorporated into training in further iterations. The experimental results indicate that OSI-FL outperforms baselines, including traditional and one-shot FL approaches, in both class-incremental and domain-incremental scenarios across three benchmark datasets.

AI.tldr: OSI-FL enables one-shot incremental federated learning by exchanging category-specific embeddings and synthesizing client-like data via a diffusion model, with selective sample retention (SSR) to mitigate forgetting; achieves strong gains over baselines in class- and domain-incremental settings.
AI.motivation: Federated learning reduces privacy risks but assumes static data flows; in large, heterogeneous, privacy-sensitive, geographically distributed streams, incremental learning with limited communication is challenging due to both high communication costs and catastrophic forgetting.
AI.method: Clients provide category-specific embeddings derived from a frozen vision-language model in a single communication round; a server uses a pre-trained diffusion model to synthesize new data matching each client's distribution; this synthetic data is used to retrain the global model; Selective Sample Retention (SSR) keeps the top-p most informative samples per category-task pair based on loss, to bound forgetting across incremental tasks.
AI.result: OSI-FL outperforms baselines including traditional FL and one-shot FL in both class-incremental and domain-incremental settings across three benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: The framework addresses both communication efficiency and catastrophic forgetting in incremental FL by combining embedding-based data換 synthesis with SSR, showing strong empirical gains; practical benefits depend on the diffusion model and VLM quality and may entail computational overhead at the server.

title: When to Trust the Cheap Check: Weak and Strong Verification for Reasoning
summary: Reasoning with LLMs increasingly unfolds inside a broader verification loop. Internally, systems use cheap checks, such as self-consistency or proxy rewards, which we call weak verification. Externally, users inspect outputs and steer the model through feedback until results are trustworthy, which we call strong verification. These signals differ sharply in cost and reliability: strong verification can establish trust but is resource-intensive, while weak verification is fast and scalable but noisy and imperfect. We formalize this tension through weak--strong verification policies, which decide when to accept or reject based on weak verification and when to defer to strong verification. We introduce metrics capturing incorrect acceptance, incorrect rejection, and strong-verification frequency. Over population, we show that optimal policies admit a two-threshold structure and that calibration and sharpness govern the value of weak verifiers. Building on this, we develop an online algorithm that provably controls acceptance and rejection errors without assumptions on the query stream, the language model, or the weak verifier.

AI.tldr: Proposes a two-threshold policy for combining cheap internal checks (weak verification) with costly external verification (strong verification) in LLM systems, and provides an online algorithm with provable guarantees to control acceptance/rejection errors without relying on specifics of the query stream, model, or weak verifier.
AI.motivation: Address the trade-off between fast, noisy weak verification and slower, reliable strong verification. Formalize how to decide when to accept a result based on weak checks and when to defer to strong checks, with an eye toward minimizing incorrect acceptances/rejections while keeping costly verification in play.
AI.method: Formal model of weak-strong verification policies; define metrics (incorrect acceptance, incorrect rejection, strong-verification frequency); prove the optimal policy has a two-threshold structure; analyze how calibration (trust in weak verifier) and sharpness (contrast in outputs) affect the value of weak verifiers; develop an online algorithm that guarantees control of acceptance/rejection errors without assumptions on the query stream, the language model, or the weak verifier.
AI.result: Analytical result showing that optimal policies are two-threshold; calibration and sharpness determine the benefit of weak verification; practical online algorithm with provable error control that does not rely on bounded streams or specific model details.
AI.conclusion: A general framework for integrating cheap internal checks with expensive external verification in LLM workflows, enabling controlled acceptance/rejection decisions and robust performance across diverse settings without strict modeling assumptions.

title: Reverso: Efficient Time Series Foundation Models for Zero-shot Forecasting
summary: Learning time series foundation models has been shown to be a promising approach for zero-shot time series forecasting across diverse time series domains. Insofar as scaling has been a critical driver of performance of foundation models in other modalities such as language and vision, much recent work on time series foundation modeling has focused on scaling. This has resulted in time series foundation models with hundreds of millions of parameters that are, while performant, inefficient and expensive to use in practice. This paper describes a simple recipe for learning efficient foundation models for zero-shot time series forecasting that are orders of magnitude smaller. We show that large-scale transformers are not necessary: small hybrid models that interleave long convolution and linear RNN layers (in particular DeltaNet layers) can match the performance of larger transformer-based models while being more than a hundred times smaller. We also describe several data augmentation and inference strategies that further improve performance. This recipe results in Reverso, a family of efficient time series foundation models for zero-shot forecasting that significantly push the performance-efficiency Pareto frontier.

AI.tldr: Efficient zero-shot time series foundation models (Reverso) using small DeltaNet-based hybrids (long convolution + linear RNN) that rival large transformers, plus data augmentation/inference strategies, achieving a favorable performance–efficiency Pareto frontier.
AI.motivation: Current time series foundation models scale to hundreds of millions of parameters, delivering strong performance but at high computational and practical cost; there is a need for compact, efficient models for zero-shot forecasting across diverse domains.
AI.method: Propose a small hybrid architecture that interleaves long convolution and linear RNN (DeltaNet) layers, rather than large transformers, complemented by data augmentation and inference strategies; define and evaluate a family of efficient models called Reverso for zero-shot forecasting.
AI.result: Small DeltaNet-based hybrids match the performance of large transformer models while being more than 100x smaller; data augmentation and inference strategies further improve performance, yielding Reverso that enhances the performance-efficiency Pareto frontier for zero-shot forecasting.
AI.conclusion: A simple recipe yields highly efficient time series foundation models that attain competitive or superior zero-shot forecasting performance with orders-of-magnitude smaller models, expanding practical applicability through the Reverso family.

title: FAMOSE: A ReAct Approach to Automated Feature Discovery
summary: Feature engineering remains a critical yet challenging bottleneck in machine learning, particularly for tabular data, as identifying optimal features from an exponentially large feature space traditionally demands substantial domain expertise. To address this challenge, we introduce FAMOSE (Feature AugMentation and Optimal Selection agEnt), a novel framework that leverages the ReAct paradigm to autonomously explore, generate, and refine features while integrating feature selection and evaluation tools within an agent architecture. To our knowledge, FAMOSE represents the first application of an agentic ReAct framework to automated feature engineering, especially for both regression and classification tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FAMOSE is at or near the state-of-the-art on classification tasks (especially tasks with more than 10K instances, where ROC-AUC increases 0.23% on average), and achieves the state-of-the-art for regression tasks by reducing RMSE by 2.0% on average, while remaining more robust to errors than other algorithms. We hypothesize that FAMOSE's strong performance is because ReAct allows the LLM context window to record (via iterative feature discovery and evaluation steps) what features did or did not work. This is similar to a few-shot prompt and guides the LLM to invent better, more innovative features. Our work offers evidence that AI agents are remarkably effective in solving problems that require highly inventive solutions, such as feature engineering.

AI.tldr: A novel ReAct-based agent framework, FAMOSE, autonomously performs feature engineering for tabular data, achieving near/SOTA performance in classification and state-of-the-art in regression while improving robustness.
AI.motivation: Feature engineering on tabular data is a major bottleneck due to the exponentially large feature space and need for domain expertise. An autonomous agentic approach could explore, generate, and evaluate features with integrated selection to improve performance and robustness.
AI.method: FAMOSE is an agent-based framework leveraging ReAct prompts to iteratively discover, generate, and refine features, integrating feature selection and evaluation tools within the agent. It records feature performance in the LLM context to guide subsequent feature invention and evaluation, and is evaluated on regression and classification tasks with datasets including over 10K instances.
AI.result: Classification tasks achieve near state-of-the-art performance, with average ROC-AUC gains of 0.23% on larger datasets; regression tasks reach state-of-the-art by reducing RMSE by 2.0% on average; FAMOSE exhibits greater robustness to errors compared to competing methods.
AI.conclusion: Agentic ReAct-style frameworks are effective for inventive tasks like feature engineering, with FAMOSE demonstrating that AI agents can autonomously explore and optimize feature spaces and improve performance in tabular learning settings.

title: A.R.I.S.: Automated Recycling Identification System for E-Waste Classification Using Deep Learning
summary: Traditional electronic recycling processes suffer from significant resource loss due to inadequate material separation and identification capabilities, limiting material recovery. We present A.R.I.S. (Automated Recycling Identification System), a low-cost, portable sorter for shredded e-waste that addresses this efficiency gap. The system employs a YOLOx model to classify metals, plastics, and circuit boards in real time, achieving low inference latency with high detection accuracy. Experimental evaluation yielded 90% overall precision, 82.2% mean average precision (mAP), and 84% sortation purity. By integrating deep learning with established sorting methods, A.R.I.S. enhances material recovery efficiency and lowers barriers to advanced recycling adoption. This work complements broader initiatives in extending product life cycles, supporting trade-in and recycling programs, and reducing environmental impact across the supply chain.

AI.tldr: A.R.I.S. is a low-cost portable e-waste sorter using YOLOx to classify shredded materials in real time, achieving 90% precision, 82.2% mAP, and 84% sortation purity.
AI.motivation: Significant resource loss in traditional electronic recycling due to inadequate material separation and identification; a need for accessible, real-time material classification to boost recovery.
AI.method: Deploys a YOLOx-based detector to identify metals, plastics, and circuit boards within shredded e-waste, enabling low-latency real-time inference and integration with conventional sorting methods; evaluated experimentally.
AI.result: Quantitative metrics reported: 90% overall precision, 82.2% mAP, and 84% sortation purity, with low inference latency and high detection accuracy, supporting improved recovery efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Merges deep learning with established sorting processes to enhance material recovery and lower barriers to advanced recycling, contributing to sustainability efforts across the supply chain.

title: Multi-Round Human-AI Collaboration with User-Specified Requirements
summary: As humans increasingly rely on multiround conversational AI for high stakes decisions, principled frameworks are needed to ensure such interactions reliably improve decision quality. We adopt a human centric view governed by two principles: counterfactual harm, ensuring the AI does not undermine human strengths, and complementarity, ensuring it adds value where the human is prone to err. We formalize these concepts via user defined rules, allowing users to specify exactly what harm and complementarity mean for their specific task. We then introduce an online, distribution free algorithm with finite sample guarantees that enforces the user-specified constraints over the collaboration dynamics. We evaluate our framework across two interactive settings: LLM simulated collaboration on a medical diagnostic task and a human crowdsourcing study on a pictorial reasoning task. We show that our online procedure maintains prescribed counterfactual harm and complementarity violation rates even under nonstationary interaction dynamics. Moreover, tightening or loosening these constraints produces predictable shifts in downstream human accuracy, confirming that the two principles serve as practical levers for steering multi-round collaboration toward better decision quality without the need to model or constrain human behavior.

AI.tldr: User-defined rules for counterfactual harm and complementarity enable online, distribution-free enforcement in multi-round AI-human collaboration, improving decision quality across tasks.
AI.motivation: As AI aids high-stakes decisions, align behavior via counterfactual harm and complementarity to preserve human strengths and add value where humans err; provide user-customizable, provable guarantees even with nonstationary interactions.
AI.method: Formalize harm/complementarity via user-specified rules; present an online, distribution-free algorithm with finite-sample guarantees to enforce constraints on collaboration dynamics; evaluate in LLM-medical diagnostic simulation and human crowdsourcing pictorial reasoning.
AI.result: The online procedure maintains prescribed harm/complementarity violation rates under nonstationary dynamics; tightening/loosening constraints yields predictable shifts in downstream human accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Counterfactual harm and complementarity, as user-defined rules, are practical levers to steer multi-round AI–human collaboration toward better decision quality without modeling human behavior; the approach is robust and adaptable to high-stakes tasks.

title: MARS: Margin-Aware Reward-Modeling with Self-Refinement
summary: Reward modeling is a core component of modern alignment pipelines including RLHF and RLAIF, underpinning policy optimization methods including PPO and TRPO. However, training reliable reward models relies heavily on human-labeled preference data, which is costly and limited, motivating the use of data augmentation. Existing augmentation approaches typically operate at the representation or semantic level and remain agnostic to the reward model's estimation difficulty. In this paper, we propose MARS, an adaptive, margin-aware augmentation and sampling strategy that explicitly targets ambiguous and failure modes of the reward model. Our proposed framework, MARS, concentrates augmentation on low-margin (ambiguous) preference pairs where the reward model is most uncertain, and iteratively refines the training distribution via hard-sample augmentation. We provide theoretical guarantees showing that this strategy increases the average curvature of the loss function hence enhance information and improves conditioning, along with empirical results demonstrating consistent gains over uniform augmentation for robust reward modeling.

AI.tldr: MARS is a margin-aware augmentation and sampling approach for reward modeling that focuses on ambiguous, low-margin preferences to improve learning efficiency and robustness, with theoretical curvature guarantees and empirical gains over uniform augmentation.
AI.motivation: Reward models based on human preferences are costly and data-scarce; existing augmentation methods ignore reward-estimation difficulty, leading to inefficient learning. Adaptive, targeted augmentation aims to improve information gain and conditioning.
AI.method: Adaptive, margin-aware augmentation and sampling (MARS) that concentrates on low-margin (ambiguous) preference pairs, uses hard-sample augmentation, and iteratively refines the training distribution; provides theoretical guarantees (increased loss curvature) and empirical evaluation.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows increased average curvature of the loss, enhancing information content and conditioning; empirical results show consistent gains over uniform augmentation in robust reward modeling tasks (RLHF/RLAIF pipelines).
AI.conclusion: MARS offers a principled, adaptive augmentation strategy that targets the most informative, uncertain regions of the reward model’s space, improving reliability and efficiency of reward modeling for policy optimization.
